Althought not heavy, an anomolous event of snow falling during the month of May in Kansas is noteworthy.  A strong cold front had moved through ushering in unseasonably cold temperatures. Upper level dynamics were sufficient to produce precipitation and with the very cold May temperatures there were several bands of snow.  The heaviest amount reported was 5 inches in Scott county.  Measureable snow was reported in Dodge City for only the 3rd time in May since 1874.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of two inches was reported in Ulysses.

First major winter storm of the season dumped intense snow rates across much of the county warning area.  Many of the counties received 6 inches or more of snow within a 2 to 4 hour period.  Thundersnow was common during the early mornings of Thursday morning February 21st.  The snowfall rates of 1 to as much as 3 inches per hour cause widespread travel difficulties.  The bulk of the snow fell from around 4am CST through 11am CST.  Additional light accumulations of less than 2 inches occurred through the late afternoon when areas of light freezing drizzle also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP, trained spotters and EM all provided snowfall measurements and estimates of 8 to 12 inches across Wabaunsee County from this winter storm. Freezing drizzle also occurred for several hours however any ice accumulation was less than one tenth of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system tracked across north Texas into Arkansas.  Northeast Kansas was on the far northwest fringe of the storm system.  The southerly track and mild temperatures in the 30 to 35 degree range helped to mitigate the impacts of the heavy wet snow that fell across parts of the area.  In a nutshell, areas along and southeast of the Kansas Turnpike from Wichita to Emporia to Topeka and east toward Kansas City all received several inches or more of wet snow.  The snow began during the late evening of February 25th and ended by midday on the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer and EM reported 6 to 8 inches of wet snow across the county from the winter storm.  Included a 7 inch report from Garnett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the morning of Saturday, December 21, 2013 an area of light to moderate rain moved northward into eastern Kansas and western Missouri. At the time of the rain surface temperatures were in the middle to upper 20s. The result was several hours of freezing rain across the aforementioned eastern Kansas and western and central Missouri areas, including the Kansas City metro area. Widespread reports of around .25 of ice accumulation were received by the NWS, with the highest report of .45 coming from Clinton, Missouri. After sunset the freezing rain gradually transitioned into sleet, then snow.  By 9 pm Saturday night most of the area was receiving light to moderate snow.  Areas north of Interstate 70 received about 6 to 8 inches of snow, with some locations reporting higher amounts. The highest official snow total the NWS received was 9.0 from spotters near St. Joseph and Grant City.  The snow moved out of the area late Saturday night, leaving behind very slick roads and some minor damage caused by the ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow picked up during the afternoon hours on December 21. Preceding the snow freezing rain produced some minor icing in and around the area. Once the snow began it quickly accumulated between 6 and 9 inches across the area. The highest amount received came from St. Joseph, where 9 inches of snow fell.  While there were several vehicle spin-outs across the area, and despite the ice accumulation the widespread effects were rather minimal.

The southerly track and mild temperatures in the 30 to 35 degree range helped to mitigate the impacts of the heavy wet snow that fell across parts of the area.  In a nutshell, areas along and southeast of the Kansas Turnpike from Wichita to Emporia to Topeka and east toward Kansas City all received several inches or more of wet snow.  The snow began during the late evening of February 25th and ended by midday on the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported 4 to 8 inches of wet snow across the county from the winter storm.  COOP observer reported 6 inches in Lane. A bit of sleet was also reported early in the storm during the late afternoon and evening of February 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of freezing rain, sleet and snow developed across eastern Kansas during the early morning hours of Saturday December 21st.  Widespread snow, sleet and freezing rain overspread the area during the afternoon of the 21st with the height of the storm occurring between 5pm Saturday and 3am Sunday.  Freezing rain accumulating to around one quarter inch across parts of the area while snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches were common across other areas north of interstate 70 by Sunday morning the 22nd. Impacts were mainly limited to hazardous travel with numerous accidents reported. Only sporadic power outages were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter (0.25) inches of ice was reported at Colony. This was in addition to around one half inch of sleet and a half inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There have been several storm systems that have impacted the region in the last half of January. Most of the precipitation from these systems has fallen along and southeast of a Kansas City to Kirksville line. This has resulted in some improvement to the drought across portions of central to northern and northeastern Missouri. However, western and far northwestern Missouri and eastern Kansas remain in a severe drought (D2) with only a small portion of Leavenworth County in an extreme drought (D3).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe D2 drought conditions prevailed across the county. Atchison reported 0.85 inches of precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: First major winter storm of the season dumped intense snow rates across much of the county warning area.  Many of the counties received 6 inches or more of snow within a 2 to 4 hour period.  Thundersnow was common during the early mornings of Thursday morning February 21st.  The snowfall rates of 1 to as much as 3 inches per hour cause widespread travel difficulties.  The bulk of the snow fell from around 4am CST through 11am CST.  Additional light accumulations of less than 2 inches occurred through the late afternoon when areas of light freezing drizzle also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported 6 to 8 inches of snow county wide.  The snow fell mainly in a 6 hour window from 4am through 10am CST.

Althought not heavy, an anomolous event of snow falling during the month of May in Kansas is noteworthy.  A strong cold front had moved through ushering in unseasonably cold temperatures. Upper level dynamics were sufficient to produce precipitation and with the very cold May temperatures there were several bands of snow.  The heaviest amount reported was 5 inches in Scott county.  Measureable snow was reported in Dodge City for only the 3rd time in May since 1874.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow was reported in Garden City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Althought not heavy, an anomolous event of snow falling during the month of May in Kansas is noteworthy.  A strong cold front had moved through ushering in unseasonably cold temperatures. Upper level dynamics were sufficient to produce precipitation and with the very cold May temperatures there were several bands of snow.  The heaviest amount reported was 5 inches in Scott county.  Measureable snow was reported in Dodge City for only the 3rd time in May since 1874.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow was reported in Lakin.

A mixture of freezing rain, sleet and snow developed across eastern Kansas during the early morning hours of Saturday December 21st.  Widespread snow, sleet and freezing rain overspread the area during the afternoon of the 21st with the height of the storm occurring between 5pm Saturday and 3am Sunday.  Freezing rain accumulating to around one quarter inch across parts of the area while snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches were common across other areas north of interstate 70 by Sunday morning the 22nd. Impacts were mainly limited to hazardous travel with numerous accidents reported. Only sporadic power outages were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer measured 6.5 inches at Minneapolis with widespread 5 to 7 inch reports across Ottawa County from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: First major winter storm of the season dumped intense snow rates across much of the county warning area.  Many of the counties received 6 inches or more of snow within a 2 to 4 hour period.  Thundersnow was common during the early mornings of Thursday morning February 21st.  The snowfall rates of 1 to as much as 3 inches per hour cause widespread travel difficulties.  The bulk of the snow fell from around 4am CST through 11am CST.  Additional light accumulations of less than 2 inches occurred through the late afternoon when areas of light freezing drizzle also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP, trained spotters and EM all provided snowfall measurements and estimates of 8 to 12 inches across Franklin County from this winter storm.  Freezing drizzle also occurred for several hours however any ice accumulation was less than one tenth of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High instability was in place as an upper level wind maxima moved out of the base of a deep upper trough in the west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was well documented by storm chasers, spotters and a research team with portable doppler radar.  The tornado did turn north towards the end of it's life cycle.  Low end EF4 damage was done to a home along US156.  The roof and walls were blown away of this well constructed house.  Several large tanks were carried a long distance.  There were 2 people inside but they escaped injury by taking shelter.  Video shows that sub-vortices within the tornado may have actually had a little stronger wind.  The Doppler on wheels that was observing the tornado had near surface measured wind speeds of 165 to 185 MPH.

The largest hail reported was 1 near Bogue.  ||In the evening a second round of storms moved from Colorado into the Kansas.  These storms were not as strong as earlier in the afternoon.  Hail up to 3/4 was the largest size reported with these storms.||The rainfall from the previous day, in combination with the slow moving storms coming in from Colorado producing heavy rainfall, caused flash flooding in rural Greeley county. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wild storm system brought a variety of weather types rarely seen in a single day, and even some strange weather combinations occurring at the same time. For instance: One particular strong thunderstorm in north central Kansas produced a combination of nickel size hail and heavy freezing rain as it traveled over ground level air that was only 30 degrees.  At Concordia, the temperature fell from 64 degrees at 7 AM to 40 degrees at 2 PM as the cold front passed through.  During the afternoon and evening, hail up to the size of golfballs fell from severe thunderstorms in Dickinson county.  By Sunrise on Wednesday, a quarter of an inch of freezing rain was clinging to the trees at the same location.  Driving conditions became quite hazardous on Wednesday morning across areas west of a Marysville to Manhattan to Council Grove line due to repeated rounds of freezing rain that eventually froze to the roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A year long period of prolonged dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of May 2013. An unusually dry period from May 2012 through March, 2013, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for portions of Central and South Central Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of April 2013, as spring showers with soaking rains occurred, which led to continued improvement of the drought during the late part of April into the early part of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across Central Kansas during the month of May. This was a year long continuation of a drought that started back in late May of 2012 and continued through the year and into April 2013.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system with an associated cold front produced rounds of thunderstorms and heavy rainfall. Isolated flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to flooding around Fort Scott.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall during the month of September eased drought conditions across northwest Kansas. Precipitation was widespread during a major, slow-moving storm system in mid-September.  Rainfall generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.50.  Highest rain totals occurred in Goodland, Oakley and Wallace with amounts between six and seven inches. Drought ratings area-wide at the beginning of the month were D3-D4 (Extreme to Exceptional).  By the end of the month, ratings improved to D2-D3 (Severe to Extreme) Drought.  Wallace county fared best with a drought rating improving to D1 (Moderate) Drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wild storm system brought a variety of weather types rarely seen in a single day, and even some strange weather combinations occurring at the same time. For instance: One particular strong thunderstorm in north central Kansas produced a combination of nickel size hail and heavy freezing rain as it traveled over ground level air that was only 30 degrees.  At Concordia, the temperature fell from 64 degrees at 7 AM to 40 degrees at 2 PM as the cold front passed through.  During the afternoon and evening, hail up to the size of golfballs fell from severe thunderstorms in Dickinson county.  By Sunrise on Wednesday, a quarter of an inch of freezing rain was clinging to the trees at the same location.  Driving conditions became quite hazardous on Wednesday morning across areas west of a Marysville to Manhattan to Council Grove line due to repeated rounds of freezing rain that eventually froze to the roadways.
A cold front dropped southeast across extreme eastern Kansas, during the morning hours of Septermber 1, 2013. A few severe thunderstorms with damaging winds were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Structural damage was reported to two barns. Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated up to 60 mph.

On the morning of Aug 15 storms were ongoing along a stalled front across Nebraska. The remnant outflow boundary from that convection moved southward across north central Kansas. Surface heating south of the boundary allowed the air mass to become unstable and increased the potential for severe wind gusts. Storms reformed during the afternoon along the outflow in central Kansas causing high winds up to almost 70 mph and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust reported through the Herington middle school mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into Central Kansas on the afternoon of July 9th, 2013.  As this cold front approached...a hot and humid airmass was located just to the south of it, to produce scattered thunderstorms across portions of Central Kansas.  A few elevated supercells developed along Interstate 70 across Lincoln, and Russell counties. The storms produced some isolated reports of damaging wind and a few large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The winds were reported by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper low pressure system moved across the region during the day. Behind the system, strong winds aloft mixed down to the surface with wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph producing localized dust storms, vehicle accidents and road closures.  Interstate 70 was closed between Limon, Colorado and Goodland, Kansas due to zero visibility and strong winds, with road closures also occurring on adjacent U.S., state and county roads. Law enforcement reported near zero visibility at times in blowing dirt in Kit Carson County Colorado, as well as Greeley, Wallace and Sherman Counties in western Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds developed late in the morning and continued through the afternoon. Peak wind gusts estimated at 60 mph from nearby mesonet sites, as well as zero visibility in blowing dirt, resulted in numerous traffic accidents involving a total of 10 vehicles. One accident three miles north of Tribune involved five semitrailers.  When one trucker tried to turn around due to poor visibility, a multiple vehicle accident ensued resulting in one injury. Two other accidents occurred in the far western portion of the county on Highway 96. Highways 27 and 96 were both closed to traffic for several hours during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Aug 2 elevated thunderstorms formed along a stalled front across Nebraska before moving south across north central Kansas. These storms were efficient rain producers given the very moist air mass that moved in behind a strong high pressure. On Aug 3 the front in Nebraska began to move south into the area, which was able to become unstable during the day before. The event was mostly strong damaging winds with a few large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof blown off a barn. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A year long period of prolonged dry weather, came to a rapid end during the last week of July 2013, as tropical moisture streamed north leading to numerous rounds of showers and thunderstorms.  The night after night rainfall rapidly refilled lakes and ponds and restored soil moisture. This ended an unusually dry period from May 2012 through March, 2013, which kept an extreme drought (D3) going for portions of Central Kansas early into the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across Central Kansas during the first part of the month of July. This was a yearlong continuation of a drought that started in late May of 2012 and continued through the year into June 2012. Copious amounts of rainfall returned to the area by the end of the month, dropping the drought to Moderate (D1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into Central Kansas on the afternoon of July 9th, 2013.  As this cold front approached...a hot and humid airmass was located just to the south of it, to produce scattered thunderstorms across portions of Central Kansas.  A few elevated supercells developed along Interstate 70 across Lincoln, and Russell counties. The storms produced some isolated reports of damaging wind and a few large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was knocked down from estimated wind gusts to 60 mph. The report was relayed via social media.

On the afternoon of Sep 19 an unstable air mass moved into eastern KS ahead of a cold front. Most of the severe wind occurred within a one hour period before the front/outflow surged out ahead of the storms and the loss of daytime heating. The isolated downbursts mainly caused damage to large tree branches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measured on the southwest side of Eudora. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A year long period of prolonged dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of May 2013. An unusually dry period from May 2012 through March, 2013, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for portions of Central and South Central Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of April 2013, as spring showers with soaking rains occurred, which led to continued improvement of the drought during the late part of April into the early part of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of May. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May 2012, and continued through the month of April and into May 2013. Some improvement in the drought conditions did occur, as most locations across South Central Kansas saw near normal rainfall which led to the drought dropping to severe limits (D2) by the middle of the month.

On the morning of Aug 15 storms were ongoing along a stalled front across Nebraska. The remnant outflow boundary from that convection moved southward across north central Kansas. Surface heating south of the boundary allowed the air mass to become unstable and increased the potential for severe wind gusts. Storms reformed during the afternoon along the outflow in central Kansas causing high winds up to almost 70 mph and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of very heavy rainfall occurred across portions of Central and Southeast Kansas during the morning hours of August 9th.  A nearly stationary frontal boundary located across southern Kansas and abundant moisture streaming over the top of the boundary led to line of stationary convection that extended from Hutchinson, Kansas to Eureka to Iola, Kansas.  This round of convection led to renewed flooding problems near Hutchinson and Newton, and also led to flash flooding near Yates Center and Iola, Kansas. One to three inches fell across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported several rural roads across the county were impassable due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall during the month of September eased drought conditions across northwest Kansas. Precipitation was widespread during a major, slow-moving storm system in mid-September.  Rainfall generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.50.  Highest rain totals occurred in Goodland, Oakley and Wallace with amounts between six and seven inches. Drought ratings area-wide at the beginning of the month were D3-D4 (Extreme to Exceptional).  By the end of the month, ratings improved to D2-D3 (Severe to Extreme) Drought.  Wallace county fared best with a drought rating improving to D1 (Moderate) Drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wild storm system brought a variety of weather types rarely seen in a single day, and even some strange weather combinations occurring at the same time. For instance: One particular strong thunderstorm in north central Kansas produced a combination of nickel size hail and heavy freezing rain as it traveled over ground level air that was only 30 degrees.  At Concordia, the temperature fell from 64 degrees at 7 AM to 40 degrees at 2 PM as the cold front passed through.  During the afternoon and evening, hail up to the size of golfballs fell from severe thunderstorms in Dickinson county.  By Sunrise on Wednesday, a quarter of an inch of freezing rain was clinging to the trees at the same location.  Driving conditions became quite hazardous on Wednesday morning across areas west of a Marysville to Manhattan to Council Grove line due to repeated rounds of freezing rain that eventually froze to the roadways.

A large weather system moved out into the High Plains from the Rockies during the day. Strong atmospheric instability and wind shear resulted in several clusters of strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening.  A second round of marginally severe storms also redeveloped during the overnight hours.  Most hail was quarter size but one report near Jennings was golf ball sized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of quarter size hail near mile marker 61 on Interstate 70, verified by sheriff dispatch in Thomas county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was quite warm on Thursday, October 17, with highs across southwestern Kansas in the 60s.  A strong, small scale upper wave dove into southwestern Kansas early on Friday, October 18th.  The mid-levels of the atmosphere cooled very quickly, bringing colder air down to the surface on Friday.  By afternoon, much of the light rain that developed turned over to snow.  By Friday evening, There was a band of 4 inches plus from just northwest of Rozel in western Pawnee County to just north of Howell in northwestern Ford County, to north of Holcomb in Northwest Finney County.  Small areas of 5 inch snow fell from extreme southwestern Pawnee County into far southeastern Hodgeman County, as well as in northern Finney County north of Garden City.  Two inches or more of snow fell south of line from near Liebenthal in Rush County, to near Pendennis in northeastern Lane County, to northwest of Lakin in Kearny County.  This 2 inch or more area was also north of a line from near Lakin to south of Dodge City in Ford County, to just northeast of Larned in northeastern Pawnee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4.0 inches fell at a location 12 miles ENE of Garden City.

On the morning of Aug 2 elevated thunderstorms formed along a stalled front across Nebraska before moving south across north central Kansas. These storms were efficient rain producers given the very moist air mass that moved in behind a strong high pressure. On Aug 3 the front in Nebraska began to move south into the area, which was able to become unstable during the day before. The event was mostly strong damaging winds with a few large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rescue near highway 18 and fair rd. Vehicle drove into 2 to 3 feet of running water. The vehicle then became hung up on a stump...allowing rescuers to access the vehicle and perform the rescue.

The observer reported seeing a large plume of dirt which briefly brought visibility to near zero as the strong winds arrived.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second line of thunderstorms moved into extreme western Kansas during the evening and produced locally strong outflow with blowing dirt reducing visibility briefly to one quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall during the month of September eased drought conditions across northwest Kansas. Precipitation was widespread during a major, slow-moving storm system in mid-September.  Rainfall generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.50.  Highest rain totals occurred in Goodland, Oakley and Wallace with amounts between six and seven inches. Drought ratings area-wide at the beginning of the month were D3-D4 (Extreme to Exceptional).  By the end of the month, ratings improved to D2-D3 (Severe to Extreme) Drought.  Wallace county fared best with a drought rating improving to D1 (Moderate) Drought.

On the morning of Aug 2 elevated thunderstorms formed along a stalled front across Nebraska before moving south across north central Kansas. These storms were efficient rain producers given the very moist air mass that moved in behind a strong high pressure. On Aug 3 the front in Nebraska began to move south into the area, which was able to become unstable during the day before. The event was mostly strong damaging winds with a few large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just west of K-15 on 4th road has water flowing over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A year long period of prolonged dry weather, came to a rapid end during the last week of July 2013, as tropical moisture streamed north leading to numerous rounds of showers and thunderstorms.  The night after night rainfall rapidly refilled lakes and ponds and restored soil moisture. This ended an unusually dry period from May 2012 through March, 2013, which kept an extreme drought (D3) going for portions of Central Kansas early into the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across Central Kansas during the first part of the month of July. This was a yearlong continuation of a drought that started in late May of 2012 and continued through the year into June 2012. Copious amounts of rainfall returned to the area by the end of the month, dropping the drought to Moderate (D1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon severe thunderstorms developed initially along a stationary front near the Colorado/Kansas border, moving to the north.  The storm activity gradually spread to the east through the afternoon and evening.  Large hail up to 1.75 inches in diameter, wind gusts up to 62 MPH, a couple tornadoes were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Short-lived landspout tornado reported by emergency management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wild storm system brought a variety of weather types rarely seen in a single day, and even some strange weather combinations occurring at the same time. For instance: One particular strong thunderstorm in north central Kansas produced a combination of nickel size hail and heavy freezing rain as it traveled over ground level air that was only 30 degrees.  At Concordia, the temperature fell from 64 degrees at 7 AM to 40 degrees at 2 PM as the cold front passed through.  During the afternoon and evening, hail up to the size of golfballs fell from severe thunderstorms in Dickinson county.  By Sunrise on Wednesday, a quarter of an inch of freezing rain was clinging to the trees at the same location.  Driving conditions became quite hazardous on Wednesday morning across areas west of a Marysville to Manhattan to Council Grove line due to repeated rounds of freezing rain that eventually froze to the roadways.

A few storms fired up across northeast Kansas, bringing penny to quarter sized hail. The largest stone was reported near Fort Riley Airfield in Geary County.  Also, a creek overflowed near Onaga in Pottawatomie County.

A strong storm system brought severe thunderstorms along with sharply colder temperatures to north central and northeast Kansas on Tuesday night April 9th into the early morning hours of April 10th.  Surface air temperatures behind a strong cold front dropped to or just below freezing while thunderstorms continued to develop across the area. Those storms produced large hail and eventually produced areas of rain and freezing rain where air temperatures fell to below freezing.  By midday Wednesday around one quarter inch of ice had accumulated across parts of central Kansas.  No significant impacts were reported and no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported around one quarter inch of ice on elevated surfaces around Abilene in Dickinson County by mid morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed over West Central Kansas during the evening, moving east.  Large hail 1.5 in diameter was reported in Wichita county from one of the stronger storms.

During the afternoon and early evening severe wind gusts were reported from a couple thunderstorms over Northwest Kansas.  The strongest wind gust of 59 MPH was measured at Norton airport when a thunderstorm moved over the site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two different wind gusts moved through; the first one lasted for about 5 minutes, then about 10 minutes later another wind gust moved through with the same strength and duration.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of scattered thunderstorms moved into Northwest Kansas from Colorado during the mid to late evening.  Along the leading edge of the storms wind gusts of 60-69 MPH were measured in Goodland.  The largest hail was 1.25, and was reported in northern Cheyenne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from 3/4 to 1.25 in diameter on Highway 27 at mile marker 224.  Estimated time of report from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From mid-afternoon to late in the evening of the May 29th, severe thunderstorms unleashed damaging winds from Barton and Lincoln Counties in Central Kansas to Chautauqua and Montgomery Counties in Southeast Kansas. Reaching 60 to 70 mph, the winds caused significant tree and property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A year long period of prolonged dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of May 2013. An unusually dry period from May 2012 through March, 2013, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for portions of Central and South Central Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of April 2013, as spring showers with soaking rains occurred, which led to continued improvement of the drought during the late part of April into the early part of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of May. This was a continuation of a drought that started last May of 2012 and continued throughout the year into April 2013.

Another round of very heavy rainfall occurred across portions of Central and Southeast Kansas during the morning hours of August 9th.  A nearly stationary frontal boundary located across southern Kansas and abundant moisture streaming over the top of the boundary led to line of stationary convection that extended from Hutchinson, Kansas to Eureka to Iola, Kansas.  This round of convection led to renewed flooding problems near Hutchinson and Newton, and also led to flash flooding near Yates Center and Iola, Kansas. One to three inches fell across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of streets in town were closed due to high water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff's office reported hail up to 2 inches deep, on County Road 500 near Highway 69.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was quite warm on Thursday, October 17, with highs across southwestern Kansas in the 60s.  A strong, small scale upper wave dove into southwestern Kansas early on Friday, October 18th.  The mid-levels of the atmosphere cooled very quickly, bringing colder air down to the surface on Friday.  By afternoon, much of the light rain that developed turned over to snow.  By Friday evening, There was a band of 4 inches plus from just northwest of Rozel in western Pawnee County to just north of Howell in northwestern Ford County, to north of Holcomb in Northwest Finney County.  Small areas of 5 inch snow fell from extreme southwestern Pawnee County into far southeastern Hodgeman County, as well as in northern Finney County north of Garden City.  Two inches or more of snow fell south of line from near Liebenthal in Rush County, to near Pendennis in northeastern Lane County, to northwest of Lakin in Kearny County.  This 2 inch or more area was also north of a line from near Lakin to south of Dodge City in Ford County, to just northeast of Larned in northeastern Pawnee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5.0 inches fell at a location 10 miles NNE of Pierceville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An especially nasty winter storm that arrived Southeast Kansas the evening of the 20th greatly intensified on the 21st. The vicious winter storm buried Central Kansas with 6 to 13 inches of snow while Southeast Kansas was ravaged by an ice storm in which freezing rain and sleet produced widespread 1/2 to 1 inch accumulations with the greater accumulations occurring along the Oklahoma Border. Snowfalls ranging from 3 to 6 inches were common across South-Central Kansas, but prior to the snow's arrival, freezing rain produced 1/4 to 1/2 inch of glaze. Travel across all areas was obviously treacherous if not impossible. No doubt hundreds of accidents occurred with several injuries. One accident in East Wichita involved 10-15 vehicles. The winter storm departed the area the morning of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulations ranging from 6 to 7.5 inches were measured from late in the evening to very early the following morning. A CO-OP observer contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Oct 3, a line of thunderstorms formed along a cold front in southern NE. The line of storms moved south towards interstate 70 and remained mainly elevated. One cell along the leading edge caught up to the gust front allowing strong winds to reach the surface in Clay Center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limb blocking the road on the outskirts of city. No diameter given.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms impacted parts of northern Kansas.  The most notable event occurred just west of Marysville where a strong tornado damaged approximately 30 homes and businesses.  No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Survey revealed EF2 damage with winds to around 130 mph produced significant damage to a John Deere dealership and a roofing warehouse just south of highway 36 and just west of Marysville, Kansas.  The tornado traveled approximately 3 to 4 miles before weakening and lifting near highway 77 north of Marysville.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of June 27, 2013 a very moist and unstable air mass moved into the area out ahead of a front advancing through KS. During the morning hours elevated convection moved through extreme NE KS, SE NE, and WN MO. During the afternoon dew points reached the mid to upper 70s and temperatures reached the 90s,  while temps in central KS reached the 100s. Outflow from previous convection stabilized the environment until late afternoon when thunderstorms developed over the center portion of our area. The storms were mostly elevated hail producers, which went on to produce wind gusts up to 70 mph over southern portions of our area later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small branches down.

On the afternoon of Oct 4 a strong system moving through the northern plains brought a cold front to the region. An unstable air mass was able to move into the area ahead of the system setting the stage for thunderstorms. As the main trough moved through the plains in continued to deepen allowing for strong winds. The combination of wind shear and instability lead to an outbreak of severe thunderstorms in northeast KS as the cold front progressed eastward. Damaging winds were the primary outcome from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Relayed from social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe wind gusts developed west of a dry line that moved northeast across Northwest Kansas. Wind gusts up to 58 MPH caused areas of blowing dust to develop behind the dry line. Visibilities of less than a quarter mile were reported across Sherman county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly coincident with near zero visibility reported 8N of Goodland and visibility under a mile at Goodland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An especially nasty winter storm that arrived Southeast Kansas the evening of the 20th greatly intensified on the 21st. The vicious winter storm buried Central Kansas with 6 to 13 inches of snow while Southeast Kansas was ravaged by an ice storm in which freezing rain and sleet produced widespread 1/2 to 1 inch accumulations with the greater accumulations occurring along the Oklahoma Border. Snowfalls ranging from 3 to 6 inches were common across South-Central Kansas, but prior to the snow's arrival, freezing rain produced 1/4 to 1/2 inch of glaze. Travel across all areas was obviously treacherous if not impossible. No doubt hundreds of accidents occurred with several injuries. One accident in East Wichita involved 10-15 vehicles. The winter storm departed the area the morning of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced around 0.25 inch of ice in Fredonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed in Cheyenne County Kansas during the late afternoon and moved south-southeast across Sherman County.  The storm produced half-inch diameter hail and wind gusts which broke a few tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds broke a few 4-inch diameter live tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of June 27, 2013 a very moist and unstable air mass moved into the area out ahead of a front advancing through KS. During the morning hours elevated convection moved through extreme NE KS, SE NE, and WN MO. During the afternoon dew points reached the mid to upper 70s and temperatures reached the 90s,  while temps in central KS reached the 100s. Outflow from previous convection stabilized the environment until late afternoon when thunderstorms developed over the center portion of our area. The storms were mostly elevated hail producers, which went on to produce wind gusts up to 70 mph over southern portions of our area later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of Oct 4 a strong system moving through the northern plains brought a cold front to the region. An unstable air mass was able to move into the area ahead of the system setting the stage for thunderstorms. As the main trough moved through the plains in continued to deepen allowing for strong winds. The combination of wind shear and instability lead to an outbreak of severe thunderstorms in northeast KS as the cold front progressed eastward. Damaging winds were the primary outcome from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of severe thunderstorms produced large hail during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Storms near Oakley, Kansas dropped hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter during the late afternoon before moving south and dissipating. A second round of storms developed just before sunset over Yuma County Colorado and Cheyenne County Kansas which ultimately moved slowly north before dissipating.  These supercells produced hail up to the size of golf balls. A window was broken in a home in western Cheyenne County as a result of golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the morning of Saturday, December 21, 2013 an area of light to moderate rain moved northward into eastern Kansas and western Missouri. At the time of the rain surface temperatures were in the middle to upper 20s. The result was several hours of freezing rain across the aforementioned eastern Kansas and western and central Missouri areas, including the Kansas City metro area. Widespread reports of around .25 of ice accumulation were received by the NWS, with the highest report of .45 coming from Clinton, Missouri. After sunset the freezing rain gradually transitioned into sleet, then snow.  By 9 pm Saturday night most of the area was receiving light to moderate snow.  Areas north of Interstate 70 received about 6 to 8 inches of snow, with some locations reporting higher amounts. The highest official snow total the NWS received was 9.0 from spotters near St. Joseph and Grant City.  The snow moved out of the area late Saturday night, leaving behind very slick roads and some minor damage caused by the ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained weather spotters from across the area reported between .20 and .50 of freezing rain on December 21. The highest amount of ice accumulation in the area came out of Clinton, Missouri, where .45 of ice accumulated. Once the freezing rain ended light snow accumulated throughout the area during the overnight hours. Between 1 and 3 inches of snow fell on top of the ice accumulation. Some power outages occurred, but no widespread effects were reported from this ice storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the late afternoon and into the evening of July 9, 2013 temperatures were very hot. Some areas reached a temperature of 108 and heat index up to 114. The dew points across the area ranged from the mid 60s to mid 70s. Temperatures and high dew points led to a very unstable environment as a surface boundary approached. With weak deep layer shear pulse like thunderstorms developed over central KS which moved along the boundary causing new updrafts to from. Isolated winds occurred in certain areas along the large outflow, and isolated large hail occurred with the strongest updrafts.

Early on the morning of Saturday, December 21, 2013 an area of light to moderate rain moved northward into eastern Kansas and western Missouri. At the time of the rain surface temperatures were in the middle to upper 20s. The result was several hours of freezing rain across the aforementioned eastern Kansas and western and central Missouri areas, including the Kansas City metro area. Widespread reports of around .25 of ice accumulation were received by the NWS, with the highest report of .45 coming from Clinton, Missouri. After sunset the freezing rain gradually transitioned into sleet, then snow.  By 9 pm Saturday night most of the area was receiving light to moderate snow.  Areas north of Interstate 70 received about 6 to 8 inches of snow, with some locations reporting higher amounts. The highest official snow total the NWS received was 9.0 from spotters near St. Joseph and Grant City.  The snow moved out of the area late Saturday night, leaving behind very slick roads and some minor damage caused by the ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain moved into the area early in the afternoon on December 21. The time in which the ice accumulated was not very long, but in the 6-8 hours between when the freezing rain began and when it transitioned into light snow about .10 to .30 of ice accumulated. After the freezing rain transitioned to snow between 1 and 3 inches of snow fell on top of the accumulated ice. Several vehicle spin outs were reported across the Kansas City Metro area. While scattered power outages were reported, and despite the several disabled vehicles on the roads, widespread effects were not incredibly high impact.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An especially nasty winter storm that arrived Southeast Kansas the evening of the 20th greatly intensified on the 21st. The vicious winter storm buried Central Kansas with 6 to 13 inches of snow while Southeast Kansas was ravaged by an ice storm in which freezing rain and sleet produced widespread 1/2 to 1 inch accumulations with the greater accumulations occurring along the Oklahoma Border. Snowfalls ranging from 3 to 6 inches were common across South-Central Kansas, but prior to the snow's arrival, freezing rain produced 1/4 to 1/2 inch of glaze. Travel across all areas was obviously treacherous if not impossible. No doubt hundreds of accidents occurred with several injuries. One accident in East Wichita involved 10-15 vehicles. The winter storm departed the area the morning of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced a wide range of ice accumulations, from around 0.25 inch across Southern Montgomery County to nearly 1 inch across Northern Montgomery County. Travel was treacherous.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought severe thunderstorms along with sharply colder temperatures to north central and northeast Kansas on Tuesday night April 9th into the early morning hours of April 10th.  Surface air temperatures behind a strong cold front dropped to or just below freezing while thunderstorms continued to develop across the area. Those storms produced large hail and eventually produced areas of rain and freezing rain where air temperatures fell to below freezing.  By midday Wednesday around one quarter inch of ice had accumulated across parts of central Kansas.  No significant impacts were reported and no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported around one quarter inch of ice on elevated surfaces around Clay Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, October 2013 marked the 17th consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity resided in some portion of this six county North Central Kansas area, prolonging one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. Unfortunately, for the majority of the local area October was at least the second consecutive month of below normal precipitation. Over the course of the month, the U.S. Drought Monitor made no change whatsoever to drought classification within the area, keeping a west (worse) to east (better) disparity intact. As a result, the lingering effects of longer-term dryness kept D2 Severe Drought in place across northwestern Phillips and far western Rooks counties. Meanwhile, category D1 Moderate Drought prevailed across southeastern Phillips, northwestern Smith and the majority of Rooks counties. On the somewhat better-off side of things, category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought) continued across most of Smith, western Osborne and northern Jewell counties. Finally, all of Mitchell, most of Osborne and southern Jewell counties remained void of all drought or abnormally dry conditions for the time being. ||Reports from around 20 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, revealed that nearly the entire six-county area measured below normal October precipitation (based on long-term October normals generally between 1.80-2.10). Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest October precipitation totals featured only 1.48 at Burr Oak, 1.36 two miles southwest of Alton and 1.33 at Webster Dam. On the driest side of the spectrum were sites such as Hunter (0.45), Glen Elder Lake (0.66) and Phillipsburg and Logan (0.76). Taking a look at longer-term year-to-date precipitation trends (Jan. 1-Oct. 23), most of the six-county area stood somewhere between 70-100% of normal, with Phillips County clearly the overall-driest including only 66% of normal at Phillipsburg. In contrast, Mitchell County remained the overall best-off, featuring 102% of normal at Beloit. The USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending October 20th indicated that topsoil moisture within the north central district continued to degrade a bit, now rating 55% short or very short, compared to 48% in mid-September. During that same time frame, subsoil moisture in the north central counties also degraded slightly from 64 to 70% short or very short. The state-averaged corn crop rated 43% good or excellent and 27% poor or very poor, a slight improvement since mid-September. The soybean crop conditions changed little during the preceding five weeks, now rating 50% good or excellent and only 14% poor or very poor. Kansas sorghum rated 49% good or excellent and only 20% poor or very poor. State-averaged range and pasture conditions checked in 36% poor or very poor and 33% good or excellent, very similar to mid-September values. Stock water supplies also exhibited little change during that time frame, currently rating 73% adequate or surplus and only 27% short or very short.

First major winter storm of the season dumped intense snow rates across much of the county warning area.  Many of the counties received 6 inches or more of snow within a 2 to 4 hour period.  Thundersnow was common during the early mornings of Thursday morning February 21st.  The snowfall rates of 1 to as much as 3 inches per hour cause widespread travel difficulties.  The bulk of the snow fell from around 4am CST through 11am CST.  Additional light accumulations of less than 2 inches occurred through the late afternoon when areas of light freezing drizzle also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP, trained spotters and EM all provided snowfall measurements and estimates of 8 to 12 inches across Shawnee County from this winter storm. Freezing drizzle also occurred for several hours however any ice accumulation was less than one tenth of an inch.

An isolated intense thunderstorm developed over Rawlins county during the late afternoon producing 2.5 inches of rain near Herndon, which ultimately resulted in up to a foot of water covering some roads.  During the evening, a large area of moderate to heavy rainfall moved across west-central Kansas producing 3-6 inches of rain and resultant flooding of county roads and rivers over Wallace, Logan, Wichita and Gove counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flood report received the following morning from a spotter who stated that heavy rainfall the previous evening resulted in numerous county roads being under water up to a foot and a half deep. Time of event was estimated from Doppler radar rainfall estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds developed over portions of Central Kansas on November 3rd, 2013. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of roads signs were blown down at Wilson Lake State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope surface and boundary layer flow in advance of an upper level short wave trough along with a brief period of frontogenesis set up for a snow event that reached warning criteria at a few locatoins.  By the time the snow ended late on the 22nd, there was a 20 to 30 mile-wide swath of 6 to 7 inches of snow, from just east of Sublette in Haskell County through southern Grant and northern Stevens Counties, and into northwest Morton County near Richfield.  From 4 to 5 inches of snow fell inside a line from 15 miles southeast of Syracuse in Hamilton County, to Northwestern Morton County to near Meade, and back to southeastern Hamilton County.  A larger area of 2 to 3 inches of snow fell south of a line from north of Syracuse in Hamilton County to near Cimarron in Gray County to northeast of Pratt in Pratt County.  North of this line, a trace to one inch of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of six inches of snow across western Meade county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm in Finney county produced a wind gust that barely reached severe wind criteria.

During the early morning hours of May 31 some rather strong thunderstorms formed ahead and along a front that was stretched across eastern Kansas. These thunderstorms were able to produce hail that ranged from one to two inches in diameter. The upper air sounding earlier in the night revealed a large amount of CAPE located in the hail growth zone. These storms reformed and trained over the same areas which caused some minor flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope surface and boundary layer flow in advance of an upper level short wave trough along with a brief period of frontogenesis set up for a snow event that reached warning criteria at a few locatoins.  By the time the snow ended late on the 22nd, there was a 20 to 30 mile-wide swath of 6 to 7 inches of snow, from just east of Sublette in Haskell County through southern Grant and northern Stevens Counties, and into northwest Morton County near Richfield.  From 4 to 5 inches of snow fell inside a line from 15 miles southeast of Syracuse in Hamilton County, to Northwestern Morton County to near Meade, and back to southeastern Hamilton County.  A larger area of 2 to 3 inches of snow fell south of a line from north of Syracuse in Hamilton County to near Cimarron in Gray County to northeast of Pratt in Pratt County.  North of this line, a trace to one inch of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The largest snow amount reported was six inches at a location 10 miles west of Moscow.

Only 5 days after a big winter storm hit Southwest Kansas with up to 21 inches of snow, another very strong winter storm tracked across parts of our forecast area.  This time, the upper level storm track was slightly more south, and the southeast part of the area received the brunt of the snow, as well as significant blizzard conditions.  Snow started falling as early as 3 am CST in Comanche, Clark, Barber, Meade and Stafford Counties.  Northerly winds increased significantly by 8 am CST, and a full blown blizzard was under way.  Blizzard conditions were experienced southeast of line from near Larned in Pawnee County to near Dodge City in Ford County, to extreme southeastern Stevens County.  Many reports of zero visibility were received, as well as many reports of closed roads due to poor visibilities and/or snow packed roads.  New snowfall amounts from this event include a narrow band of 12 inches along the Oklahoma border from Kiowa in southeast Barber County to near Englewood in southern Clark County.  From 8 to 12 inches of snow fell southeast of a line from Pratt in Pratt County to Greensburg in Kiowa County, to near Meade in Meade County, to Liberal in southern Seward County.  Lesser snowfall from 1 to 6 inches fell southeast of a line from Schoenchen in southern Ellis County to northwestern Hodgeman County, to around Sublette in Haskell County, to near Rolla in eastern Morton County.  A trace to up to a half inch of light snow fell northwest of the above defined areas.

A major winter storm impacted much of Kansas and Missouri on February 21 with very heavy snow and some sleet and freezing rain.  Some areas received up to a foot of snow, placing this snow storm among the largest snow events in recent memory.  Many locations reported thunder with heavy snow and sleet, with snowfall rates as high as 3 inches per hour!  ||The very heavy snowfall rates created havoc on area roadways and many travelers became stuck and stranded.  Many roads became clogged with stalled vehicles, essentially closing them down. Many motorists abandoned their cars altogether leaving roadways littered with snow-covered vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow was reported across the county.

A early spring winter storm clobbered parts of northeast and east central Kansas with anywhere from 5 to as much as 9 inches of heavy wet snow.  The snow began to fall during the late afternoon on the 23rd and intensified overnight.  The snow ended across the area by around noon on the 24th. North winds of 20 to 30 mph produced widespread blowing and drifting of snow into the 24th especially in the outlying, rural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported widespread 6 to 8 inch snowfall amounts across the county.  Conditions were so bad during the morning of the 24th that state highway 59 was closed due to the snow and drifting.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters measured 6 inches of snow 8 miles southwest of Wamego.

Lesser snowfall from 1 to 6 inches fell southeast of a line from Schoenchen in southern Ellis County to northwestern Hodgeman County, to around Sublette in Haskell County, to near Rolla in eastern Morton County.  A trace to up to a half inch of light snow fell northwest of the above defined areas.
A late season snow event led to widespread snow accumulations across much of southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of heavy snow and freezing rain caused significant travel issues across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sluggish spring snowstorm affected the area with significant snowfall over a 36-48 hour period.  The band of heaviest snow, with amounts up to 17 inches, fell across Yuma and Kit Carson counties in Colorado, eastward into Cheyenne, Sherman and Thomas counties in northwest Kansas.  Some notable amounts in Colorado include 14 at Yuma, Joes and Kirk, 13 in Stratton with 17 in Burlington. In Kansas, 12 was reported in St Francis, 15 in Goodland, 12 in Colby and 9 in Atwood. Across southwest Nebraska, snow totals were less, with 8 in Parks, 9 in Benkelman and 8.5 near Stratton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports included 12 near St Francis and 10 south of Wheeler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Only 5 days after a big winter storm hit Southwest Kansas with up to 21 inches of snow, another very strong winter storm tracked across parts of our forecast area.  This time, the upper level storm track was slightly more south, and the southeast part of the area received the brunt of the snow, as well as significant blizzard conditions.  Snow started falling as early as 3 am CST in Comanche, Clark, Barber, Meade and Stafford Counties.  Northerly winds increased significantly by 8 am CST, and a full blown blizzard was under way.  Blizzard conditions were experienced southeast of line from near Larned in Pawnee County to near Dodge City in Ford County, to extreme southeastern Stevens County.  Many reports of zero visibility were received, as well as many reports of closed roads due to poor visibilities and/or snow packed roads.  New snowfall amounts from this event include a narrow band of 12 inches along the Oklahoma border from Kiowa in southeast Barber County to near Englewood in southern Clark County.  From 8 to 12 inches of snow fell southeast of a line from Pratt in Pratt County to Greensburg in Kiowa County, to near Meade in Meade County, to Liberal in southern Seward County.  Lesser snowfall from 1 to 6 inches fell southeast of a line from Schoenchen in southern Ellis County to northwestern Hodgeman County, to around Sublette in Haskell County, to near Rolla in eastern Morton County.  A trace to up to a half inch of light snow fell northwest of the above defined areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of high winds and high snow fall amounts caused treacherous conditions across much of South Central and portions of Central and Southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with heavy snow produced near white out conditions throughout the morning. As the day progressed, light snow continued across the county and continued to make travel hazardous.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 5 pm CDT on this Wednesday, a thunderstorm rapidly formed over northwestern Russell county and was quickly joined by a string of other storms. These storms formed a short line segment, the northeastern end of which extended into Osborne county. Just before 5:30 pm CDT, a new storm developed near the town of Osborne. The storm remained stationary until an apparent storm split occurred around 6 pm CDT. This newly split cell produced several reports of severe hail, roughly 5 miles west of Osborne. The hail was as large as golf balls. The southwestern terminus of the line segment of storms remained stationary over northeastern Ellis county, until around 6:20 pm CDT, when the storm split. The newly split left storm slowly drifted into southern Rooks county, briefly producing one inch hail south of Plainville just after 7 pm CDT. Thereafter, the storm rapidly weakened.||The upper-level flow was very complex, but an upper-level trough was crossing the Northern Plains. The storms formed within an inverted trough, between low pressure over the Oklahoma Panhandle, and a cold front progressing through the Northern Plains. Temperatures in the mid-70s and dewpoints in the mid-50s resulted in MLCAPE between 1000 and 1500 J/kg. While mostly short-lived multicells occurred, deep layer and low-level shear was marginally strong enough for the supercells.

Severe weather was widespread across the Central Plains on this Wednesday, and North Central Kansas was not spared. An EF-1 tornado occurred, as well as straight-line wind damage. During the afternoon, an extensive squall line formed from north-central Nebraska to western Kansas. Around 8 pm CDT, as the squall line approached North Central Kansas, the part of the line over Norton and Graham counties surged outward, resulting in an embedded bow echo. Spotters estimated winds of 60 to 70 mph, with the main swath of damage occurring over Phillips and Smith Counties. The spotter-estimated winds were confirmed by a measurement of 58 MPH at the Smith Center municipal airport. Trees and power poles were blown down in parts of Phillips county. Some of that damage was associated with a brief, non-supercell tornado south of Prairie View. Winds were estimated as high as 90 MPH. The line weakened once it past Smith Center, but it still produced winds as high as 50 mph in some locations.||These numerous thunderstorms, and the associated severe weather, were a result of lee cyclogenesis over Colorado. A negatively tilted upper-level trough extended from Colorado southeastward to Texas, with the leading edge of a 100 knot upper-level jet streak just to the west. The atmosphere was extremely unstable. Temperatures nearing 80 degrees, with dewpoints approaching 70 degrees, resulted in MLCAPE of 3000-3500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 130am CDT on this early Sunday morning, a 67 MPH wind gust occurred at the Smith Center airport, resulting in at least a few instances of tree damage in town. These strong gusts, likely out of the south-southeast, occurred on the back side of a steadily-weakening line of thunderstorms and might have been associated with a wake low. Although there were no other reports of severe-criteria wind within this six-county portion of North Central Kansas during the night, it's likely that fairly widespread gusts of at least 40-50 MPH occurred both along and behind this line of convection. Breaking down event timing, a small cluster of multicell storms first rumbled into Phillips and Rooks counties from the west around 11pm CDT on the 25th...a continuation of scattered activity that had been slowly drifting east from extreme northwest Kansas since mid-afternoon. During the ensuing few hours, this strong multicell cluster evolved into a linear mode as it pushed into Smith and Osborne counties before rapidly dissipating. A small, briefly strong storm redeveloped over Rooks County between 230-330am CDT, but that was pretty much it for the night. ||On the large scale aloft, this event occurred under fairly modest quasi-zonal flow, as North Central Kansas remained well-removed from stronger flow associated with broad longwave troughs centered near both the northwest and northeast corners of the United States. However, the presence of subtle, low-amplitude shortwave troughs passing through the flow helped ignite strong to severe convection during the afternoon and evening hours to the west and northwest of the local area, in an environment featuring steep low to mid level lapse rates, around 2500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE, and around 35 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear. The modest intensification of storms well after nightfall was likely aided by an increasing low level jet evident at 850 millibars. At the surface, south to southeast breezes prevailed across North Central Kansas through the night, as the area resided south of a quasi-stationary front stretched from north central into eastern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 67 mph was recorded by the Smith Center Airport AWOS and was the result of a decaying thunderstorm in the area.  Law enforcement reported tree damage in Smith Center, including one tree which was knocked down and blocking a driveway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary and produced large tornadoes during the afternoon and evening of May 28th 2013.  2 tornadoes occurred in Nemaha county Kansas and at least 1 violent long lived tornado occurred in Ottawa County Kansas.  Amazingly no serious injuries and no fatalities were reported.  Later that night slow moving storms dumped 5 to 10 inches of rain across parts of Brown and Nemaha counties in Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tornado developed around 545pm CDT and proceeded to move slowly east for approximately 2 miles before turning back to the west and south toward highway 18. The tornado was lost in rain and was not visible after approximately 630 pm however mobile doppler radar supplemental data indicates that this tornado likely persisted through around 645 pm CDT.  This tornado was nearly 1 mile wide at times and NWS damage survey found EF3 damage indicators suggesting winds to around 150 mph.  2 mobile doppler radars were within 5-6km of this tornado and did sample winds around and above 100m well in excess of 166 mph. In fact they measured winds of 247 mph at 100m! Nothing close to EF5 damage was revealed on the survey.  However, given the lack of damage indicators available within the track of the tornado we have incorporated the mobile doppler wind data into our assessment of the tornado strength and we believe that this tornado contained at least EF4 winds in excess of 166 mph.  We acknowledge the current directive which states that one cannot rate tornado intensity by non-damage data sources so we will enter this in as EF3 however we are stating in the comments that this was a violent tornado with most likely EF4 intensity at some point during its life. Amazingly no serious injuries or fatalities were reported.  However there were approximately 100 head of cattle that were killed by the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the post frontal area during the afternoon.  Lapse rates supported elevated thunderstorms as a weak mid level wind maximum moved across the area.
An upper level short wave through moved into the plains helping to produce the necessary lift along a retreating dry line to produce thunderstorms, some of which were severe.
An upper level short wave through moved into the plains helping to produce the necessary lift along a retreating dry line to produce thunderstorms, some of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was mostly dime sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave through moved into the plains helping to produce the necessary lift along a retreating dry line to produce thunderstorms, some of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was several inches deep and the largest stones were the size of golf balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave through moved into the plains helping to produce the necessary lift along a retreating dry line to produce thunderstorms, some of which were severe.

This event occurred on a Monday, which was also Memorial Day. The first and most intense thunderstorm of the day developed over northern Phillips county just after 4:30 pm CDT. This was the southern storm in a line segment of supercell storms, which exhibited multicell characteristics. This line extended northeast across the border into southern Nebraska. During the following four hours, this storm tracked east across Phillips, Smith and Jewell counties. Numerous storm chasers followed this storm. The initial storm report came from the rear flank of the storm, over Phillips county. It was here that the RFD produced winds estimated around 60 mph. As the storm moved east, two weak, brief tornadoes were spotted just northwest and northeast of Smith Center. A rain-wrapped EF-3 tornado, described as a wedge, then followed, tracking just north of Lebanon and Esbon. The tornado track was roughly 6 miles long and at its widest point, was nearly one mile wide. Even though this tornado was over rural areas, it still managed to produce damage. The worst damage was to a home that lost its roof and part of its second story. ||On radar, the hook echo was short and stubby. Because of this, there was substantial rain within the mesocyclone, which made it difficult to see the tornado except in its immediate vicinity. The Tornado Intercept Vehicle endured a direct hit from the tornado, with video confirmation. As this storm continued into Jewell County, no more reports of wind damage or tornadoes were received. However, the hail dramatically increased with multiple reports of hail to the size of tennis balls. The largest hail was yet to occur. Near Montrose, hail a little large than grapefruit size was reported (5 and 1/4 inches). After 7:00 pm CDT, other multicell storms developed, mainly affecting Mitchell county. These storms only produced a single report of 1 inch hail northwest of Beloit.||With the 8 am CDT Severe Weather Outlook, the Storm Prediction Center upgraded North Central Kansas to a moderate risk for severe thunderstorms. A weak short-wave trough was approaching the region, embedded within a broad upper-level trough, over the western United States. A quasi-stationary front, oriented in an east-west fashion, extended across Nebraska. During the early morning hours, a mesoscale convective system tracked through eastern Nebraska southeastward into Missouri. This MCS laid down an outflow boundary that extended across northern Kansas, to a lee-side low. It was to the north of this outflow boundary that storms initiated. At the time of initial thunderstorm development, temperatures were in the lower to middle 80s to near 90, with dewpoints in the middle 60s. This resulted in an extremely unstable atmosphere, with MLCAPE between 3000 and 4000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 40 to 50 knots, which was more that strong enough for supercell storm structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Sunday afternoon event was part of the same large-scale system that produced a couple severe thunderstorms the previous day. A digging upper-level trough was over the western United States, while low pressure continued to organize over South Dakota, becoming vertically-stacked by evening. The associated cold front progressed through North Central Kansas during peak heating, initiating the thunderstorms. Prior to thunderstorm development, a subtle short-wave trough moved through, introducing cooler and drier air aloft and increasing potential instability. The exit region of an 85 knot upper-level jet streak nosed into Oklahoma, coupling with the cold front to create deep, large-scale lift.||Surface temperatures peaked around 80, while dewpoints were in the 60s ahead of the front. MLCAPE was not overly impressive, with 1000-1500 J/kg ahead of the front. Thunderstorms were numerous this afternoon, especially over eastern Kansas where the first batch of multicell storms developed. A second batch also affected eastern Kansas but they began modestly over North Central Kansas as a few scattered storms. The strongest of these storms developed over eastern Rooks county, just after 6 pm CDT, and intensified as it moved east across Osborne and Mitchell counties. Upon entering Mitchell county around 7:10 pm CDT, the storm bowed outward. This is when it began producing wind damage roughly along Highway 24, from Glen Elder east to Beloit. Trees and large branches were blown down, and a roof was blown off a building at a lumber yard. In Beloit, winds of 63 mph were measured at the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts out ahead of and with this thunderstorm produced wind gusts estimated near 70 mph. These wind gusts resulted in roof damage at a lumber yard in Glen Elder and a tree 18 inches in diameter being uprooted 4 miles south of town.  Dime to nickel size hail was also reported, with some piles of hail being 6 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather was widespread across the Central Plains on this Wednesday, and North Central Kansas was not spared. An EF-1 tornado occurred, as well as straight-line wind damage. During the afternoon, an extensive squall line formed from north-central Nebraska to western Kansas. Around 8 pm CDT, as the squall line approached North Central Kansas, the part of the line over Norton and Graham counties surged outward, resulting in an embedded bow echo. Spotters estimated winds of 60 to 70 mph, with the main swath of damage occurring over Phillips and Smith Counties. The spotter-estimated winds were confirmed by a measurement of 58 MPH at the Smith Center municipal airport. Trees and power poles were blown down in parts of Phillips county. Some of that damage was associated with a brief, non-supercell tornado south of Prairie View. Winds were estimated as high as 90 MPH. The line weakened once it past Smith Center, but it still produced winds as high as 50 mph in some locations.||These numerous thunderstorms, and the associated severe weather, were a result of lee cyclogenesis over Colorado. A negatively tilted upper-level trough extended from Colorado southeastward to Texas, with the leading edge of a 100 knot upper-level jet streak just to the west. The atmosphere was extremely unstable. Temperatures nearing 80 degrees, with dewpoints approaching 70 degrees, resulted in MLCAPE of 3000-3500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At mid-afternoon on this Saturday, discrete storms initiated over western Kansas, near a surface low and dryline and north of a developing warm front. Several of the storms organized along a common cold pool and moved northeast across north-central Kansas as an arc-shaped squall line. Several of the embedded storms produced hail just under one inch in diameter. There was a single report of one inch hail and estimated winds to 60 mph in northern Phillips county.||An upper-level trough was digging through the desert southwest and lee-side cyclogenesis was underway. Temperatures in the low-mid 80s and rich dewpoints in the mid-upper 60s resulted in MLCAPE of 2500-5000 J/kg. While deep layer shear was strong enough to support supercell storm structures, the number of cells resulted merging of individual cold pools and ultimately a multicell storm mode.

Thunderstorms developed over southeast Sherman County during the late afternoon and moved slowly southeast into Logan and Thomas Counties during the evening. Heavy rain produced flooding in Logan county.  Several other reports of marginally severe hail and strong winds were also received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this portion of North Central Kansas narrowly missed a more widespread onslaught of severe weather that impacted parts of central and northeast Kansas on this Tuesday afternoon and evening, a pair of severe storms snuck into Mitchell County and dropped quarter to golf ball size hail in the Hunter and Tipton areas. These storms primarily resided within the county between 730-930 pm CDT, as both of them peaked in intensity for around one hour before quickly dissipating. The first storm, which moved into areas east of Hunter out of northern Lincoln County, was a northwest-drifting offshoot from a nearly stationary cluster of severe storms centered over Ottawa County. The second storm, which formed slightly west of the first, tracked north across far western Mitchell County including the Tipton area. ||One of the main reasons this area of North Central Kansas was spared a more widespread severe weather threat was its position just northwest of a fairly well-defined quasi-stationary front stretched from west central to northeast Kansas. During the afternoon and early evening hours, the vast majority of severe storms in the region formed on and remained close to this surface front, largely because southwesterly mid level winds were aligned nearly parallel to it. This southwesterly mid and upper level flow was focused on the eastern side of a large-scale trough moving into the Rockies. In addition, the local area found itself on the backside of a departing mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) during the late afternoon, also helping to concentrate the majority of severe weather just to the south and east. Severe weather parameters in the Mitchell County area during the evening hours featured strong instability with around 3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE, in the presence of 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer shear.
An upper level short wave through moved into the plains helping to produce the necessary lift along a retreating dry line to produce thunderstorms, some of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 70 MPH were reported with three inch diameter tree limbs blown down.

Around 5 pm CDT on this Wednesday, a thunderstorm rapidly formed over northwestern Russell county and was quickly joined by a string of other storms. These storms formed a short line segment, the northeastern end of which extended into Osborne county. Just before 5:30 pm CDT, a new storm developed near the town of Osborne. The storm remained stationary until an apparent storm split occurred around 6 pm CDT. This newly split cell produced several reports of severe hail, roughly 5 miles west of Osborne. The hail was as large as golf balls. The southwestern terminus of the line segment of storms remained stationary over northeastern Ellis county, until around 6:20 pm CDT, when the storm split. The newly split left storm slowly drifted into southern Rooks county, briefly producing one inch hail south of Plainville just after 7 pm CDT. Thereafter, the storm rapidly weakened.||The upper-level flow was very complex, but an upper-level trough was crossing the Northern Plains. The storms formed within an inverted trough, between low pressure over the Oklahoma Panhandle, and a cold front progressing through the Northern Plains. Temperatures in the mid-70s and dewpoints in the mid-50s resulted in MLCAPE between 1000 and 1500 J/kg. While mostly short-lived multicells occurred, deep layer and low-level shear was marginally strong enough for the supercells.

A weak frontal boundary along with several upper level impulses led to an unsettled weather pattern over southeast Kansas. Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, wind damage, and flash flooding.
