EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet moved into the Texas Panhandle area putting southwest Kansas in the favorable left exit region providing enough to lift to produce thunderstorms, many that were severe.  The boundary layer was moist and unstable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief spin-up was observed lifting dirt but no damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed rapidly during the afternoon across Thomas County and expanded eastward through the afternoon and early evening hours.  A few hail reports were received from spotters.  Locally heavy rainfall of 1.50-2.50 was also reported across Thomas, Logan and Gove counties, but no significant flooding or damage occurred with the heavy rain.  A few storms produced strong outflow as they collapsed, resulting in minor tree and outbuilding damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Late report of six-eight inch diameter live tree limbs blown down. A small shed was also destroyed by strong thunderstorm outflow.  An empty stock tank was blown approximately one mile and came to rest in a corn field.  Time of report estimated from Doppler radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At mid-afternoon on this Saturday, discrete storms initiated over western Kansas, near a surface low and dryline and north of a developing warm front. Several of the storms organized along a common cold pool and moved northeast across north-central Kansas as an arc-shaped squall line. Several of the embedded storms produced hail just under one inch in diameter. There was a single report of one inch hail and estimated winds to 60 mph in northern Phillips county.||An upper-level trough was digging through the desert southwest and lee-side cyclogenesis was underway. Temperatures in the low-mid 80s and rich dewpoints in the mid-upper 60s resulted in MLCAPE of 2500-5000 J/kg. While deep layer shear was strong enough to support supercell storm structures, the number of cells resulted merging of individual cold pools and ultimately a multicell storm mode.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities were near zero with driving rain. The estimated wind gusts were up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of severe thunderstorms produced large hail during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Storms near Oakley, Kansas dropped hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter during the late afternoon before moving south and dissipating. A second round of storms developed just before sunset over Yuma County Colorado and Cheyenne County Kansas which ultimately moved slowly north before dissipating.  These supercells produced hail up to the size of golf balls. A window was broken in a home in western Cheyenne County as a result of golf ball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports from emergency manager (EM), trained spotters and the public were received in a short period of time with hail sizes ranging from 1.25 up to 2.50 in diameter.  The EM estimated at least 100 vehicles in town suffered some type of hail damage, with some having windshields or other glass broken. Two med-flight aircraft also had damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Sunday afternoon event was part of the same large-scale system that produced a couple severe thunderstorms the previous day. A digging upper-level trough was over the western United States, while low pressure continued to organize over South Dakota, becoming vertically-stacked by evening. The associated cold front progressed through North Central Kansas during peak heating, initiating the thunderstorms. Prior to thunderstorm development, a subtle short-wave trough moved through, introducing cooler and drier air aloft and increasing potential instability. The exit region of an 85 knot upper-level jet streak nosed into Oklahoma, coupling with the cold front to create deep, large-scale lift.||Surface temperatures peaked around 80, while dewpoints were in the 60s ahead of the front. MLCAPE was not overly impressive, with 1000-1500 J/kg ahead of the front. Thunderstorms were numerous this afternoon, especially over eastern Kansas where the first batch of multicell storms developed. A second batch also affected eastern Kansas but they began modestly over North Central Kansas as a few scattered storms. The strongest of these storms developed over eastern Rooks county, just after 6 pm CDT, and intensified as it moved east across Osborne and Mitchell counties. Upon entering Mitchell county around 7:10 pm CDT, the storm bowed outward. This is when it began producing wind damage roughly along Highway 24, from Glen Elder east to Beloit. Trees and large branches were blown down, and a roof was blown off a building at a lumber yard. In Beloit, winds of 63 mph were measured at the airport.

This event occurred on a Monday, which was also Memorial Day. The first and most intense thunderstorm of the day developed over northern Phillips county just after 4:30 pm CDT. This was the southern storm in a line segment of supercell storms, which exhibited multicell characteristics. This line extended northeast across the border into southern Nebraska. During the following four hours, this storm tracked east across Phillips, Smith and Jewell counties. Numerous storm chasers followed this storm. The initial storm report came from the rear flank of the storm, over Phillips county. It was here that the RFD produced winds estimated around 60 mph. As the storm moved east, two weak, brief tornadoes were spotted just northwest and northeast of Smith Center. A rain-wrapped EF-3 tornado, described as a wedge, then followed, tracking just north of Lebanon and Esbon. The tornado track was roughly 6 miles long and at its widest point, was nearly one mile wide. Even though this tornado was over rural areas, it still managed to produce damage. The worst damage was to a home that lost its roof and part of its second story. ||On radar, the hook echo was short and stubby. Because of this, there was substantial rain within the mesocyclone, which made it difficult to see the tornado except in its immediate vicinity. The Tornado Intercept Vehicle endured a direct hit from the tornado, with video confirmation. As this storm continued into Jewell County, no more reports of wind damage or tornadoes were received. However, the hail dramatically increased with multiple reports of hail to the size of tennis balls. The largest hail was yet to occur. Near Montrose, hail a little large than grapefruit size was reported (5 and 1/4 inches). After 7:00 pm CDT, other multicell storms developed, mainly affecting Mitchell county. These storms only produced a single report of 1 inch hail northwest of Beloit.||With the 8 am CDT Severe Weather Outlook, the Storm Prediction Center upgraded North Central Kansas to a moderate risk for severe thunderstorms. A weak short-wave trough was approaching the region, embedded within a broad upper-level trough, over the western United States. A quasi-stationary front, oriented in an east-west fashion, extended across Nebraska. During the early morning hours, a mesoscale convective system tracked through eastern Nebraska southeastward into Missouri. This MCS laid down an outflow boundary that extended across northern Kansas, to a lee-side low. It was to the north of this outflow boundary that storms initiated. At the time of initial thunderstorm development, temperatures were in the lower to middle 80s to near 90, with dewpoints in the middle 60s. This resulted in an extremely unstable atmosphere, with MLCAPE between 3000 and 4000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 40 to 50 knots, which was more that strong enough for supercell storm structures.

Scattered thunderstorms developed early, near midday, over northwestern Kansas on this Thursday. These storms formed a common cold pool and spread eastward, mostly along Interstate 70. However, some ancillary storms formed over North Central Kansas, between 3:30 and 4 pm CDT. Some storms merged over Osborne County, and intensified. The newly consolidated storm cluster become the strongest storm in the area, between 4 and 5 pm CDT. As the storm moved from Osborne County into Mitchell County it produced mostly sub-severe wind and hail. However, there were two reports of estimated wind gusts of 60 mph, near Tipton and Hunter.||A deep 991 mb surface low pressure system was over the Dakotas. These storms formed behind a cold front that moved through earlier in the day. Temperatures were in the middle 70s, with dewpoints falling through the 50s. Instability was weak with MLCAPE between 500 and 1000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was just below 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 mph were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Althought not heavy, an anomolous event of snow falling during the month of May in Kansas is noteworthy.  A strong cold front had moved through ushering in unseasonably cold temperatures. Upper level dynamics were sufficient to produce precipitation and with the very cold May temperatures there were several bands of snow.  The heaviest amount reported was 5 inches in Scott county.  Measureable snow was reported in Dodge City for only the 3rd time in May since 1874.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow was reported in Johnson.

During the afternoon hours of July 20, 2013 a pop up thunderstorm developed over Junction City as thunderstorm outflows collided with a boundary. The storm collapsed over Fort Riley causing a 60 mph wind gust which was measured by the AWOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, May marked the twelfth consecutive month of moderate (or worse) drought across nearly this entire six-county portion of North Central Kansas, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. However, areas mainly east of the Highway 281 corridor realized some limited improvement thanks to decent monthly rainfall. More specifically, eastern portions of Smith and Osborne counties improved to D2 Severe Drought over the course of the month, while southeastern Jewell County improved to D1 Moderate Drought. In Mitchell County, most of the county was in D1 Moderate Drought at month's end. However, a small sliver of eastern Mitchell County managed to shake free of official drought conditions, although was still assigned D0 Abnormally Dry. This marked the first time since June 2012 that any portion of the area featured something better than D1 drought. Farther west, conditions stayed worse, as the majority of Phillips and Rooks counties remained in D3 Extreme Drought, with the far western fringes of those counties still brushed with D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category). Based on reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 60 percent of the six-county area observed below normal May precipitation (based on normals generally between 3.75-4.25 inches). The driest areas were largely concentrated across Phillips, northwest Smith, western Rooks, western Jewell and southeast Osborne counties. In contrast, above normal rainfall concentrated within Mitchell, eastern Jewell and western Osborne counties. However, even within the below normal areas most places still measured at least 1.50 for the month. Per NWS cooperative observers, several of the highest-known May precipitation totals included 4.52 at Jewell, 4.39 at Mankato and 4.27 four miles west-northwest of Plainville. On the drier side of things, some of the lowest monthly totals were 1.45 near Long Island, 1.51 at Logan and 1.59 at Phillipsburg. In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of the area reported totals between 2.00 and 3.50.  Remarkably, for some areas a small fraction of May precipitation consisted of snow, as an unusually cold start to the month brought very light accumulations up to around 1 primarily on the 1st.||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation amounts (January-May), most of the six-county area received between 60 and 100 percent of normal, with Phillips County the overall-driest. In parts of the area, a near normal trend certainly was not enough to end drought issues, although the severity had eased somewhat since fall 2012. According to the USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending June 9th, topsoil moisture across the north central district had recently degraded a bit, rating 31% short to very short and 65% adequate (versus 15% and 82%, respectively, in early May). Subsoil moisture supplies in the area exhibited a very slight improvement during that same time, but were still 59% short to very short. The state-averaged wheat crop rated 47% poor to very poor and only 28% good to excellent. However, the young corn and soybean crop rated 60% and 64% good to excellent, respectively. Range and pasture conditions across Kansas rated 45% poor to very poor, a 17% improvement since early May. Hay and forage supplies had also improved slightly, with the poor to very poor ratings decreasing from 71% to 59%. This slight improvement was also noted in stock water supplies, checking in at 35% short to very short, versus 48% in early May.

This marked the first time since June 2012 that any portion of the area featured something better than D1 drought. Farther west, conditions stayed worse, as the majority of Phillips and Rooks counties remained in D3 Extreme Drought, with the far western fringes of those counties still brushed with D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category). Based on reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 60 percent of the six-county area observed below normal May precipitation (based on normals generally between 3.75-4.25 inches). The driest areas were largely concentrated across Phillips, northwest Smith, western Rooks, western Jewell and southeast Osborne counties. In contrast, above normal rainfall concentrated within Mitchell, eastern Jewell and western Osborne counties. However, even within the below normal areas most places still measured at least 1.50 for the month. Per NWS cooperative observers, several of the highest-known May precipitation totals included 4.52 at Jewell, 4.39 at Mankato and 4.27 four miles west-northwest of Plainville. On the drier side of things, some of the lowest monthly totals were 1.45 near Long Island, 1.51 at Logan and 1.59 at Phillipsburg. In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of the area reported totals between 2.00 and 3.50.  Remarkably, for some areas a small fraction of May precipitation consisted of snow, as an unusually cold start to the month brought very light accumulations up to around 1 primarily on the 1st.||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation amounts (January-May), most of the six-county area received between 60 and 100 percent of normal, with Phillips County the overall-driest. In parts of the area, a near normal trend certainly was not enough to end drought issues, although the severity had eased somewhat since fall 2012. According to the USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending June 9th, topsoil moisture across the north central district had recently degraded a bit, rating 31% short to very short and 65% adequate (versus 15% and 82%, respectively, in early May). Subsoil moisture supplies in the area exhibited a very slight improvement during that same time, but were still 59% short to very short. The state-averaged wheat crop rated 47% poor to very poor and only 28% good to excellent. However, the young corn and soybean crop rated 60% and 64% good to excellent, respectively. Range and pasture conditions across Kansas rated 45% poor to very poor, a 17% improvement since early May. Hay and forage supplies had also improved slightly, with the poor to very poor ratings decreasing from 71% to 59%. This slight improvement was also noted in stock water supplies, checking in at 35% short to very short, versus 48% in early May.

A complex of thunderstorms moving east around 25 mph produced torrential rains across the region from that Monday afternoon until late that night along, and north, of a nearly stationary front that was draped across Northern Oklahoma. The flooding|that resulted was widespread and major with numerous roads and highways barricaded. Even a 30-mile segment of the Kansas Turnpike was closed briefly in Chase County. The flooding was so serious that water rescues and evacuations were required.||The most serious flooding occurred from McPherson County, across Southern Marion and Chase counties, to Greenwood County. In Northern McPherson County, one elderly woman was injured in Lindsborg when she fell into a flood around 600 PM. In Lindsborg, heavy trucks and boats were used to evacuate homes around an 8-mile wide area.||Many areas received 3 to 5 inches of rain in only 3 or 4 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter sent a picture of street flooding in McPherson via twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed early on this Saturday. The first storms developed over North Central Kansas during the noon hour, and gradually increased in coverage and intensity. Very little severe weather occurred, but that may have been due to the the strongest storm of the event occurring over sparsely populated portions of Smith County, north of highway 36. The slow-moving supercell produced a single report of quarter sized hail north-northeast of Smith Center. Given the observed radar signatures, this storm may have produced significant severe hail. In addition, another storm produced a sub-severe wind gust of 54 mph at Osborne. The slow storm movement was the main story, with drenching rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches in 1 to 2 hours. These impressive totals occurred over parts of Smith, eastern Osborne and northern Mitchell counties.||These storms formed north of a stationary front that extended from Dodge City to Russell to Topeka. The region was at the southern end of the westerlies, with a very broad trough over the northern United States. Prior to thunderstorm initiation, temperatures were in the 80s with dewpoints in the 60s. Instability was modest, with MLCAPE between 1000 and 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was less than 30 knots, resulting in a multicell mode.

This Sunday evening featured several reports of strong to severe wind of 50-70 MPH, as a fairly compact mesoscale convective system (MCS) tracked southeast across nearly the entire six-county area. The most notable measured gusts at area airports included 66 MPH at Smith Center and 56 MPH near Stockton. Other storm reports featured estimated 70 MPH winds at Gaylord, estimated 70 MPH winds and quarter size hail near Downs, and at least one large tree downed in Osborne and Cawker City. Although rainfall amounts across most of the area averaged 1-2 inches at most, a narrow corridor extending from east-central Smith County across southwestern Jewell and northern Mitchell Counties tallied at least 2-3 inches, and even an unofficial gauge reading of 4.90 inches four miles southwest of Jewell. This resulted in localized minor flooding along the Brown Creek in southern Jewell County and near Solomon Rapids in northern Mitchell County. ||This line of storms already had a history of producing strong to severe winds as it dropped southward out of Nebraska into Phillips and Smith counties between 730-830 pm CDT. Between 830-1030 pm CDT, the line became increasingly-organized into a bowing-MCS as it charged southeast. By 11 pm CDT, the most intense storms had pushed south into central Kansas, leaving behind lingering, non-severe convection over parts of North Central Kansas through around 130 am CDT. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in seasonable quasi-zonal flow containing embedded, low-amplitude shortwave troughs, with northern Kansas displaced south of the strongest upper level flow aligned from the Dakotas to the Great Lakes. At the surface, the primary early-evening feature was a warm front bisecting the area along a line from southwest Nebraska to southeast Kansas, which put North Central Kansas in a low-level east-southeast flow regime. Early evening mesoscale severe weather parameters over the local area featured mixed layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree limbs were down across town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the afternoon until late in the evening of the 22nd, severe thunderstorms struck South-Central & Southeast Kansas. The severe thunderstorms were prolific wind producers. The most violent thunderstorms roared through South-Central Kansas, more specifically Kingman and Sumner Counties, with vicious winds that not only caused widespread tree, power line and power pole damage, but tore the roofs off a high school and elementary school in Argonia, located in extreme West-Central Sumner County.||Late that evening, another severe thunderstorm hit Russell County with hail as large as ping-pong balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles spanning a 1 mile long segment of K-61 were blown down.

A series of small but compact supercell thunderstorms developed just north of Highway 54, across South Central Kansas near Kingman and Zenda, Kansas.  One prolific supercell produced 70 to 100 mph wind damage and power outages along its path, from just north of Kingman, Kansas to Newton to Eureka, Kansas.  A marina at El Dorado Lake, Kansas was hardest hit, damaging several boats and swamping a 40 foot houseboat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A camper was overturned at a campground at El Dorado Lake. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eight to ten inch diameter tree was knocked down, with several small trees blocking roadways across town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late night/early morning a line of thunderstorms moved across northwest Kansas.  Strong to severe wind gusts accompanied the thunderstorms as they moved through the area.  A peak wind gust of 70 MPH was reported north of Atwood with the line of storms.

A slow-moving upper low pressure area stalled over Colorado, while ample low-level moisture moved in on southeast winds.  The result was a period of very heavy rainfall from repeat episodes of thunderstorms.  By Friday morning, September 13th, a large area of 2-6 inches of rain with locally higher amounts to 9 inches, was observed from Limon to Burlington Colorado, east along the I-70 corridor to Goodland, Oakley and Quinter in Kansas.  Numerous reports of flooded or washed out roads were received from law enforcement and county officials.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road 13 between Roads 52 and 55 was closed due to water over the road as a result of heavy rainfall. The Smoky Hill River was over the bridge at Road 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the afternoon until late in the evening of the 22nd, severe thunderstorms struck South-Central & Southeast Kansas. The severe thunderstorms were prolific wind producers. The most violent thunderstorms roared through South-Central Kansas, more specifically Kingman and Sumner Counties, with vicious winds that not only caused widespread tree, power line and power pole damage, but tore the roofs off a high school and elementary school in Argonia, located in extreme West-Central Sumner County.||Late that evening, another severe thunderstorm hit Russell County with hail as large as ping-pong balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The trained spotter also reported 60 to 70 mph winds that occurred around 20 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The estimated winds ranged from 60 to 70 mph and occurred for around 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, July 2013 marked the 15th consecutive month of D1 Moderate (or worse) drought within this six-county portion of North Central Kansas, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. Precipitation trends during July were a mixed bag across the area, with roughly an even-split between above and below normal rainfall, but with nearly all places receiving at least 1.50. That being said, Phillips County continued to dry out more than any other county in the area, enduring its 5th-consecutive month of below normal precipitation. On a positive note, July temperatures averaged a few degrees cooler than normal...in sharp contrast to the notably hot and dry July 2012 one year ago that put considerable stress on crops. All factors considered, much of this six-county area actually realized a one-category improvement in official drought classification during July according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, although a noticeable west-east disparity remained. In the west, most of Phillips and a decent chunk of Rooks counties remained in D3 Extreme Drought at month's end, with a sliver of D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category) still clipping western Phillips County. Farther east, most of Smith, Osborne and Jewell counties were a mix of D1 Moderate and D2 Severe drought, although southeast Osborne County had improved to D0 Abnormally Dry (one category better than true drought). Mitchell County remained in the overall best-shape, featuring almost exclusively the D0 category. Reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, confirmed the fairly even-split between above and below normal rainfall across the area (based on normals generally between 3.60-4.15 inches). Mitchell and Osborne counties were the overall-wettest in the area, while Phillips, Smith and northern Jewell were the overall driest. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the lowest July rainfall totals were 1.68 one mile north of Long Island, 1.89 at Lebanon and 2.16 at Phillipsburg. On the noticeably wetter side of things were locations such as Cawker City (8.54), Beloit (7.22) and Jewell (5.29). In between these wettest and driest extremes, much of the area tallied July totals between 2.50-4.50, including stations in or near Plainville, Smith Center, Burr Oak and Natoma. ||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (January 1-July 24), most of the six-county area only stood 51-92% of normal, with Phillips County clearly the overall-driest, including only 51% of normal at both Logan and Phillipsburg. In contrast, Mitchell County remained the overall-wettest, featuring 112% of normal at Beloit. The USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending July 21st indicated that dryland crops and pastures continued to suffer, especially in western portions of the state. Within the north central district, topsoil moisture rated 74% short to very short and only 25% adequate, a considerable decline versus values of 31% and 65%, respectively, in early June. Subsoil moisture supplies in the north central counties had also degraded over the past several weeks, now rating 71% short to very short versus 59% previously. The state-averaged corn crop rated 30% poor to very poor, but also 31% good to excellent. Soybeans rated 41% good to excellent and only 13% poor to very poor. State-averaged range and pasture conditions rated 55% poor to very poor, a degradation from 45% in early June. Stock water supplies checked in 42% short to very short, a 7% degradation over the past six weeks.

This Sunday evening featured several reports of strong to severe wind of 50-70 MPH, as a fairly compact mesoscale convective system (MCS) tracked southeast across nearly the entire six-county area. The most notable measured gusts at area airports included 66 MPH at Smith Center and 56 MPH near Stockton. Other storm reports featured estimated 70 MPH winds at Gaylord, estimated 70 MPH winds and quarter size hail near Downs, and at least one large tree downed in Osborne and Cawker City. Although rainfall amounts across most of the area averaged 1-2 inches at most, a narrow corridor extending from east-central Smith County across southwestern Jewell and northern Mitchell Counties tallied at least 2-3 inches, and even an unofficial gauge reading of 4.90 inches four miles southwest of Jewell. This resulted in localized minor flooding along the Brown Creek in southern Jewell County and near Solomon Rapids in northern Mitchell County. ||This line of storms already had a history of producing strong to severe winds as it dropped southward out of Nebraska into Phillips and Smith counties between 730-830 pm CDT. Between 830-1030 pm CDT, the line became increasingly-organized into a bowing-MCS as it charged southeast. By 11 pm CDT, the most intense storms had pushed south into central Kansas, leaving behind lingering, non-severe convection over parts of North Central Kansas through around 130 am CDT. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in seasonable quasi-zonal flow containing embedded, low-amplitude shortwave troughs, with northern Kansas displaced south of the strongest upper level flow aligned from the Dakotas to the Great Lakes. At the surface, the primary early-evening feature was a warm front bisecting the area along a line from southwest Nebraska to southeast Kansas, which put North Central Kansas in a low-level east-southeast flow regime. Early evening mesoscale severe weather parameters over the local area featured mixed layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor improvement was seen in drought conditions across northwest Kansas with some areas receiving beneficial rainfall.  Twelve of the 13 counties were rated as being in D4 (Exceptional) Drought at the beginning of August, with only Graham county rated as D3 (Extreme) Drought.  Rainfall during the month resulted in the following five counties improving slightly from D4 to D3 Drought during the month: Norton, Wallace, Logan, Greeley, Wichita.

Twelve of the 13 counties were rated as being in D4 (Exceptional) Drought at the beginning of August, with only Graham county rated as D3 (Extreme) Drought.  Rainfall during the month resulted in the following five counties improving slightly from D4 to D3 Drought during the month: Norton, Wallace, Logan, Greeley, Wichita.

Drought conditions remained essentially unchanged across northwest Kansas in July, despite a few rounds of precipitation.  All counties continue to be rated in D3 (Extreme) to D4 (Exceptional) Drought.
Early morning thunderstorms produced local flash flooding across parts of east central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over half a foot of water 30 yards flowing over 12th st.

A marina at El Dorado Lake, Kansas was hardest hit, damaging several boats and swamping a 40 foot houseboat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moving east around 25 mph produced torrential rains across the region from that Monday afternoon until late that night along, and north, of a nearly stationary front that was draped across Northern Oklahoma. The flooding|that resulted was widespread and major with numerous roads and highways barricaded. Even a 30-mile segment of the Kansas Turnpike was closed briefly in Chase County. The flooding was so serious that water rescues and evacuations were required.||The most serious flooding occurred from McPherson County, across Southern Marion and Chase counties, to Greenwood County. In Northern McPherson County, one elderly woman was injured in Lindsborg when she fell into a flood around 600 PM. In Lindsborg, heavy trucks and boats were used to evacuate homes around an 8-mile wide area.||Many areas received 3 to 5 inches of rain in only 3 or 4 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered Kansas Highway 4 south of Gypsum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 830-1030 pm CDT on this Sunday evening, a lone severe thunderstorm drifted slowly east, primarily impacting southeastern Smith and southwestern Jewell counties before rapidly weakening and fading away. The storm yielded a couple reports of large hail, including ping pong ball size stones nine miles south of Lebanon. ||This storm initiated shortly before sunset along a west-southwest to east-northeast oriented quasi-stationary front draped across the heart of this six-county area, and extending from eastern Colorado into southeast Nebraska. Mid-evening breezes near this boundary were easterly, as surface dewpoints averaged in the mid-upper 60s F. In the mid-upper levels, modest west-southwest flow contained a subtle impulse drifting east toward the area from northwest Kansas, well to the south of a larger, vigorous shortwave trough centered along the North Dakota-Manitoba border. Mid-evening severe weather parameters in North Central Kansas featured potent most-unstable CAPE around 3000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear around 35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail was quarter size with a few near ping pong ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late night/early morning a line of thunderstorms moved across northwest Kansas.  Strong to severe wind gusts accompanied the thunderstorms as they moved through the area.  A peak wind gust of 70 MPH was reported north of Atwood with the line of storms.

A slow-moving upper low pressure area stalled over Colorado, while ample low-level moisture moved in on southeast winds.  The result was a period of very heavy rainfall from repeat episodes of thunderstorms.  By Friday morning, September 13th, a large area of 2-6 inches of rain with locally higher amounts to 9 inches, was observed from Limon to Burlington Colorado, east along the I-70 corridor to Goodland, Oakley and Quinter in Kansas.  Numerous reports of flooded or washed out roads were received from law enforcement and county officials.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road 10 between Roads 59 and 64 was closed due to water over the road as a result of heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper low pressure system moved across the region during the day. Behind the system, strong winds aloft mixed down to the surface with wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph producing localized dust storms, vehicle accidents and road closures.  Interstate 70 was closed between Limon, Colorado and Goodland, Kansas due to zero visibility and strong winds, with road closures also occurring on adjacent U.S., state and county roads. Law enforcement reported near zero visibility at times in blowing dirt in Kit Carson County Colorado, as well as Greeley, Wallace and Sherman Counties in western Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility was reported at the Sherman-Wallace county border by law enforcement who were working a two-car accident at the county line. No injuries were reported. Numerous mesonet sites in the area were reporting several hours of peak wind gusts near 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed during the afternoon with gusts to 60 mph producing blowing dirt and wind damage.  Interstate 70 and Highway 27 were closed due to poor visibility. A mesonet station west of Goodland reported a peak gust of 61 mph while the Goodland ASOS recorded a 58 mph gust. A grain drier was blown off its support and onto a railroad car in south Goodland as a result of the strong winds.

Interstate 70 was closed between Limon, Colorado and Goodland, Kansas due to zero visibility and strong winds, with road closures also occurring on adjacent U.S., state and county roads. Law enforcement reported near zero visibility at times in blowing dirt in Kit Carson County Colorado, as well as Greeley, Wallace and Sherman Counties in western Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed during the afternoon with gusts estimated at 60 mph producing blowing dirt and at least one vehicle accident.  Highway 27 was closed from Tribune to Goodland due to visibility near zero at times.  Mesonet stations in the area reported peak wind gusts of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decent severe weather outbreak moved across central Kansas producing hail up the size of golf balls.  Widespread nuisance flooding was noted at several locations across the area as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph winds were also reported.

A complex of thunderstorms moving east around 25 mph produced torrential rains across the region from that Monday afternoon until late that night along, and north, of a nearly stationary front that was draped across Northern Oklahoma. The flooding|that resulted was widespread and major with numerous roads and highways barricaded. Even a 30-mile segment of the Kansas Turnpike was closed briefly in Chase County. The flooding was so serious that water rescues and evacuations were required.||The most serious flooding occurred from McPherson County, across Southern Marion and Chase counties, to Greenwood County. In Northern McPherson County, one elderly woman was injured in Lindsborg when she fell into a flood around 600 PM. In Lindsborg, heavy trucks and boats were used to evacuate homes around an 8-mile wide area.||Many areas received 3 to 5 inches of rain in only 3 or 4 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kansas Turnpike was closed briefly between mileposts 90 and 110. It was reopened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Sunday evening featured several reports of strong to severe wind of 50-70 MPH, as a fairly compact mesoscale convective system (MCS) tracked southeast across nearly the entire six-county area. The most notable measured gusts at area airports included 66 MPH at Smith Center and 56 MPH near Stockton. Other storm reports featured estimated 70 MPH winds at Gaylord, estimated 70 MPH winds and quarter size hail near Downs, and at least one large tree downed in Osborne and Cawker City. Although rainfall amounts across most of the area averaged 1-2 inches at most, a narrow corridor extending from east-central Smith County across southwestern Jewell and northern Mitchell Counties tallied at least 2-3 inches, and even an unofficial gauge reading of 4.90 inches four miles southwest of Jewell. This resulted in localized minor flooding along the Brown Creek in southern Jewell County and near Solomon Rapids in northern Mitchell County. ||This line of storms already had a history of producing strong to severe winds as it dropped southward out of Nebraska into Phillips and Smith counties between 730-830 pm CDT. Between 830-1030 pm CDT, the line became increasingly-organized into a bowing-MCS as it charged southeast. By 11 pm CDT, the most intense storms had pushed south into central Kansas, leaving behind lingering, non-severe convection over parts of North Central Kansas through around 130 am CDT. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in seasonable quasi-zonal flow containing embedded, low-amplitude shortwave troughs, with northern Kansas displaced south of the strongest upper level flow aligned from the Dakotas to the Great Lakes. At the surface, the primary early-evening feature was a warm front bisecting the area along a line from southwest Nebraska to southeast Kansas, which put North Central Kansas in a low-level east-southeast flow regime. Early evening mesoscale severe weather parameters over the local area featured mixed layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was observed in conjunction with the estimated 70 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning an outflow boundary from overnight storms in central Nebraska moved south into Northwest Kansas.  By early afternoon thunderstorms had begun to develop along the boundary, following it southward.  As the afternoon progressed storm coverage increased.  A few of the thunderstorms were strong enough to produce quarter size hail in Sherman, Logan, and Wallace counties.  One storm near I-70 blew a loaded semi off the road west of Goodland.  Due to the slow storm movement flash flooding also occurred west of Russell Springs on Highway 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado was seen from the weather office looking northeast.  It dissipated after about two minutes while remaining over open country.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper low pressure area stalled over Colorado, while ample low-level moisture moved in on southeast winds.  The result was a period of very heavy rainfall from repeat episodes of thunderstorms.  By Friday morning, September 13th, a large area of 2-6 inches of rain with locally higher amounts to 9 inches, was observed from Limon to Burlington Colorado, east along the I-70 corridor to Goodland, Oakley and Quinter in Kansas.  Numerous reports of flooded or washed out roads were received from law enforcement and county officials.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in Road 21 just east of Goodland being closed between Roads 64 and 65 due to water over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the afternoon until late in the evening of the 22nd, severe thunderstorms struck South-Central & Southeast Kansas. The severe thunderstorms were prolific wind producers. The most violent thunderstorms roared through South-Central Kansas, more specifically Kingman and Sumner Counties, with vicious winds that not only caused widespread tree, power line and power pole damage, but tore the roofs off a high school and elementary school in Argonia, located in extreme West-Central Sumner County.||Late that evening, another severe thunderstorm hit Russell County with hail as large as ping-pong balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A framed pole barn that was under construction was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into the area as a strong upper level short waved approached from the west.  Warm and moist air destabilized the atmosphere by  late afternoon.  The storms developed along and just north of the advancing warm front.

Several small thunderstorms formed in close proximity to each other, just before 3 pm CDT on this Thursday. The storms initially developed over Smith and Osborne counties. Only one of these storms survived. It grew as it moved across Osborne County into Mitchell County, eventually merging with two smaller, nearby storms. Several reports of one inch hail were received with this storm, from just east of Osborne to Tipton and Hunter. Strong winds were also estimated in Hunter, but no damage was reported. The storms exited North Central Kansas by 5 pm CDT.||These storms formed near the center of a weak low pressure, which was located along a convective outflow/differential heating boundary draped across northern Kansas. Just prior to initiation, temperatures were well into the 90s, with dewpoints near 70. This, combined with steep mid-level lapse rates, resulted in MLCAPE values around 3000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 50 knots. The flow aloft was northwesterly, with large and strong subtropical high pressure over the Desert Southwest and a trough over the East Coast.

A year long period of prolonged dry weather, lingered the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of June 2013. An unusually dry period from May 2012 through March, 2013, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for portions of Central  Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of April through May 2013, as spring showers with soaking rains occurred, which led to continued improvement of the drought during the month of May and into the early part of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across Central Kansas during the month of June. This was a year long continuation of a drought that started back in late May of 2012 and continued through the year and into May 2013.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A year long period of prolonged dry weather, lingered the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of June 2013. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of April through May 2013, as spring showers with soaking rains occurred, which led to continued improvement of the drought during the month of May and into the early part of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across Central Kansas during the first part of the month of June. This was a year long continuation of a drought that started in late May of 2012 and continued through the year into May 2012.  Much needed rainfall returned to the area by the middle of the month, dropping the drought to Moderate (D1).

A squall line of thunderstorms moved across Central Kansas on June 8th, 2013.  This rapid moving line of storms produced 60-70 mph wind damage in the town of Russell, Kansas.  The strong winds produced widespread wind damage across Russell, Kansas with some power lines knocked down and some power outages.  Some street flooding was also reported across town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inch tree limbs down on 5th street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, June marked 13 months since moderate (or worse) drought initially took hold within this six county portion of North Central Kansas, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. Unfortunately, June marked the fourth month in a row that at least half of the area observed below normal precipitation. However, this was the second consecutive month that the greatest concentration of above normal rainfall favored Mitchell and Osborne counties, while below normal rainfall primarily concentrated in Rooks, Smith and especially Phillips counties.  Fortunately, temperatures during June 2013 averaged only slightly above normal, easing drought impacts somewhat compared to one year ago when June 2012 averaged 3-6 degrees above normal. As a result of these trends, weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor changed very little during June over much of the area. At month's end, most of Phillips and Rooks counties, along with western portions of Smith and Osborne counties remained in D3 Extreme Drought, with a sliver of D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category) still clipping far western portions of Phillips and Rooks counties. Farther east, a mix of D2 Severe and D1 Moderate Drought prevailed across the majority of Jewell, Smith and Osborne counties, while Mitchell County remained in the overall-best shape with a mix of D1 Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry conditions. According to reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, at least 75 percent of the six-county area observed below normal June precipitation (based on normals generally between 3.00-3.75 inches). Per NWS cooperative observers, several of the lowest June precipitation totals featured 0.34 near Long Island, 0.61 at Logan, 0.74 at Phillipsburg and 1.38 four miles west-northwest of Plainville. Within the limited above normal areas, some of the wettest sites included Beloit (4.08), Cawker City (3.95) and Lebanon (3.80). In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of the area reported below normal amounts between 1.50 and 3.00, including stations in or near Jewell, Mankato, Smith Center and Natoma. ||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (January-June), most of the six-county area only stood 55-95% of normal, with Phillips County clearly the overall-driest, including 53% of normal at Phillipsburg, while Mitchell County the overall-wettest. According to the USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending June 9th, topsoil moisture across the north central district had recently degraded a bit, rating 31% short to very short and 65% adequate (versus 15% and 82%, respectively, in early May). Subsoil moisture supplies in the area exhibited a very slight improvement during that same time, but were still 59% short to very short. The state-averaged wheat crop rated 47% poor to very poor and only 28% good to excellent. However, the young corn and soybean crop rated 60% and 64% good to excellent, respectively. Range and pasture conditions across Kansas rated 45% poor to very poor, a 17% improvement since early May. Hay and forage supplies had also improved slightly, with the poor to very poor ratings decreasing from 71% to 59%. This slight improvement was also noted in stock water supplies, checking in at 35% short to very short, versus 48% in early May.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, June marked 13 months since moderate (or worse) drought initially took hold within this six county portion of North Central Kansas, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. Unfortunately, June marked the fourth month in a row that at least half of the area observed below normal precipitation. However, this was the second consecutive month that the greatest concentration of above normal rainfall favored Mitchell and Osborne counties, while below normal rainfall primarily concentrated in Rooks, Smith and especially Phillips counties.  Fortunately, temperatures during June 2013 averaged only slightly above normal, easing drought impacts somewhat compared to one year ago when June 2012 averaged 3-6 degrees above normal. As a result of these trends, weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor changed very little during June over much of the area. Farther east, a mix of D2 Severe and D1 Moderate Drought prevailed across the majority of Jewell, Smith and Osborne counties, while Mitchell County remained in the overall-best shape with a mix of D1 Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry conditions. Per NWS cooperative observers, several of the lowest June precipitation totals featured 0.34 near Long Island, 0.61 at Logan, 0.74 at Phillipsburg and 1.38 four miles west-northwest of Plainville. Several small thunderstorms formed in close proximity to each other, just before 3 pm CDT on this Thursday. The storms initially developed over Smith and Osborne counties. Only one of these storms survived. It grew as it moved across Osborne County into Mitchell County, eventually merging with two smaller, nearby storms. Several reports of one inch hail were received with this storm, from just east of Osborne to Tipton and Hunter. Strong winds were also estimated in Hunter, but no damage was reported. The storms exited North Central Kansas by 5 pm CDT.||These storms formed near the center of a weak low pressure, which was located along a convective outflow/differential heating boundary draped across northern Kansas. Just prior to initiation, temperatures were well into the 90s, with dewpoints near 70. This, combined with steep mid-level lapse rates, resulted in MLCAPE values around 3000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was around 50 knots. The flow aloft was northwesterly, with large and strong subtropical high pressure over the Desert Southwest and a trough over the East Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A year long period of prolonged dry weather, lingered the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of June 2013. An unusually dry period from May 2012 through March, 2013, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for portions of Central  Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of April through May 2013, as spring showers with soaking rains occurred, which led to continued improvement of the drought during the month of May and into the early part of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across Central Kansas during the first part of the month of June. This was a year long continuation of a drought that started in late May of 2012 and continued through the year into May 2012.  Much needed rainfall returned to the area by the middle of the month, dropping the drought to Moderate (D1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of thunderstorms moved across Central Kansas on June 8th, 2013.  This rapid moving line of storms produced 60-70 mph wind damage in the town of Russell, Kansas.  The strong winds produced widespread wind damage across Russell, Kansas with some power lines knocked down and some power outages.  Some street flooding was also reported across town.

A squall line of thunderstorms moved across Central Kansas on June 8th, 2013.  This rapid moving line of storms produced 60-70 mph wind damage in the town of Russell, Kansas.  The strong winds produced widespread wind damage across Russell, Kansas with some power lines knocked down and some power outages.  Some street flooding was also reported across town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down across the city. A few power lines were also knocked down, which led to portions of town to be without power for a short time.

This Sunday evening featured several reports of strong to severe wind of 50-70 MPH, as a fairly compact mesoscale convective system (MCS) tracked southeast across nearly the entire six-county area. The most notable measured gusts at area airports included 66 MPH at Smith Center and 56 MPH near Stockton. Other storm reports featured estimated 70 MPH winds at Gaylord, estimated 70 MPH winds and quarter size hail near Downs, and at least one large tree downed in Osborne and Cawker City. Although rainfall amounts across most of the area averaged 1-2 inches at most, a narrow corridor extending from east-central Smith County across southwestern Jewell and northern Mitchell Counties tallied at least 2-3 inches, and even an unofficial gauge reading of 4.90 inches four miles southwest of Jewell. This resulted in localized minor flooding along the Brown Creek in southern Jewell County and near Solomon Rapids in northern Mitchell County. ||This line of storms already had a history of producing strong to severe winds as it dropped southward out of Nebraska into Phillips and Smith counties between 730-830 pm CDT. Between 830-1030 pm CDT, the line became increasingly-organized into a bowing-MCS as it charged southeast. By 11 pm CDT, the most intense storms had pushed south into central Kansas, leaving behind lingering, non-severe convection over parts of North Central Kansas through around 130 am CDT. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in seasonable quasi-zonal flow containing embedded, low-amplitude shortwave troughs, with northern Kansas displaced south of the strongest upper level flow aligned from the Dakotas to the Great Lakes. At the surface, the primary early-evening feature was a warm front bisecting the area along a line from southwest Nebraska to southeast Kansas, which put North Central Kansas in a low-level east-southeast flow regime. Early evening mesoscale severe weather parameters over the local area featured mixed layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported an estimated wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning an outflow boundary from overnight storms in central Nebraska moved south into Northwest Kansas.  By early afternoon thunderstorms had begun to develop along the boundary, following it southward.  As the afternoon progressed storm coverage increased.  A few of the thunderstorms were strong enough to produce quarter size hail in Sherman, Logan, and Wallace counties.  One storm near I-70 blew a loaded semi off the road west of Goodland.  Due to the slow storm movement flash flooding also occurred west of Russell Springs on Highway 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 25 closed between mile markers 157 and 158 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 7:30 am CDT on this Saturday, a single thunderstorm developed over Webster County, Nebraska. In the ensuing half hour, additional thunderstorms formed southward into eastern Smith County Kansas, forming a short two-county line segment. The southern-most storm temporarily intensified over northwestern Jewell County, between 8:30 and 8:45 am CDT. At 8:33 am CDT, a cooperative observer in Burr Oak reported pea to dime size hail. However, he did find a single hailstone the size of a quarter.||These storms formed along a cold front that extended from the Canadian maritime provinces, across the Great Lakes and into extreme northern Kansas. A shortwave trough had exited a longwave trough over the western United States, and was affecting the Central Plains. As a result, a strong low-level jet was intersecting the front. Although the storms were probably elevated, surface temperatures were in the middle 70s, with dewpoints in the middle 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were steep, resulting in MUCAPE of 1500 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail that fell was pea to dime size, but one stone in particular was the diameter of a quarter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning an outflow boundary from overnight storms in central Nebraska moved south into Northwest Kansas.  By early afternoon thunderstorms had begun to develop along the boundary, following it southward.  As the afternoon progressed storm coverage increased.  A few of the thunderstorms were strong enough to produce quarter size hail in Sherman, Logan, and Wallace counties.  One storm near I-70 blew a loaded semi off the road west of Goodland.  Due to the slow storm movement flash flooding also occurred west of Russell Springs on Highway 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers began moving into North Central Kansas by late morning on this Saturday. Immediately behind the showers, clouds diminished and between 2 and 3 p.m. CDT, thunderstorms began forming within this area of clearing. These storms quickly expanded, becoming a squall line that by 4 p.m. CDT extended from North Central Kansas into southwestern Kansas. The northern end of this line tracked across Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties. Over southeastern Osborne County, a small-scale bow echo formed. As this small bow advanced across Mitchell County, one report of measured 60 to 65 mph winds was received.||A subtropical high was over the eastern Pacific, with a broad trough over the middle two-thirds of the country. The leading edge of a 125 knot, anticyclonically curved upper level jet was beginning to emerge onto the Central Plains. This resulted in a deepening, negatively tilted shortwave trough moving into the region. The result was an occluded low pressure system whose cold front advanced through North Central Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours. Temperatures were in the upper 70s to around 80 just prior to the thunderstorms. MLCAPE was only has high as 1000 J/kg. Despite the significant large-scale forcing, deep layer shear was only 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts of 60 to 65 mph were measured by an anemometer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture was streaming north in advance of an approaching short wave trough that eventually helped to initiate convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A parked and empty semi truck and trailer were blown over by high wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms began early on this Tuesday between 1 and 2 pm CDT.  The first storm developed over Norton County. As the afternoon progressed, this storm spawned another couple storms over Phillips and Rooks counties.  Isolated, slow-moving thunderstorms also developed over other parts of North Central Kansas. Overall, the storms exhibited single cell and occasionally multicellular characteristics. Severe weather was not widespread. One storm produced quarter size hail and winds estimated around 50 mph just north of Osborne. The strongest storm of the day occurred over Jewell County near Burr Oak. A merger of two small cells resulted in a combined cell that briefly intensified and pulsed to severe levels. One inch hail occurred and its slow movement resulted in 2.50 inches of rain.||Prior to thunderstorm formation, temperatures were in the middle 80s, with dewpoints in the upper 50s. MLCAPE fell between 1000 and 1500 J/kg. The forcing for thunderstorm initiation was weak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged from the size of peas to quarters.

A year long period of prolonged dry weather, lingered the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of June 2013. An unusually dry period from May 2012 through March, 2013, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for portions of Central  Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of April through May 2013, as spring showers with soaking rains occurred, which led to continued improvement of the drought during the month of May and into the early part of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across Central Kansas during the month of June. This was a year long continuation of a drought that started in late May of 2012 and continued through the year into May 2012.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Sunday evening featured several reports of strong to severe wind of 50-70 MPH, as a fairly compact mesoscale convective system (MCS) tracked southeast across nearly the entire six-county area. The most notable measured gusts at area airports included 66 MPH at Smith Center and 56 MPH near Stockton. Other storm reports featured estimated 70 MPH winds at Gaylord, estimated 70 MPH winds and quarter size hail near Downs, and at least one large tree downed in Osborne and Cawker City. Although rainfall amounts across most of the area averaged 1-2 inches at most, a narrow corridor extending from east-central Smith County across southwestern Jewell and northern Mitchell Counties tallied at least 2-3 inches, and even an unofficial gauge reading of 4.90 inches four miles southwest of Jewell. This resulted in localized minor flooding along the Brown Creek in southern Jewell County and near Solomon Rapids in northern Mitchell County. ||This line of storms already had a history of producing strong to severe winds as it dropped southward out of Nebraska into Phillips and Smith counties between 730-830 pm CDT. Between 830-1030 pm CDT, the line became increasingly-organized into a bowing-MCS as it charged southeast. By 11 pm CDT, the most intense storms had pushed south into central Kansas, leaving behind lingering, non-severe convection over parts of North Central Kansas through around 130 am CDT. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred in seasonable quasi-zonal flow containing embedded, low-amplitude shortwave troughs, with northern Kansas displaced south of the strongest upper level flow aligned from the Dakotas to the Great Lakes. At the surface, the primary early-evening feature was a warm front bisecting the area along a line from southwest Nebraska to southeast Kansas, which put North Central Kansas in a low-level east-southeast flow regime. Early evening mesoscale severe weather parameters over the local area featured mixed layer CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rain total of 4.90 inches fell 4 miles southwest of Jewell. Most of the rain occurred between 10 pm and Midnight CDT on June 16th. This report is from an unofficial rain gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A year long period of prolonged dry weather, lingered the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of June 2013. An unusually dry period from May 2012 through March, 2013, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for portions of Central  Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of April through May 2013, as spring showers with soaking rains occurred, which led to continued improvement of the drought during the month of May and into the early part of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought (D2), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of June. This was a year long continuation of a drought that started back in late May of 2012, and continued throughout the year into May 2013. Some improvement to the drought did occur in the previous month of May, which improved the drought conditions from an extreme drought (D3).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second night in a row, parts of this six-county area observed strong to severe storms. The initial development of this multicell cluster bubbled up over Rooks County between 10-11 pm CDT on Monday evening the 24th and eventually reached severe levels around midnight CDT as it slid east, producing quarter size hail northeast of Stockton, estimated 60-70 MPH winds in the Alton area, and a measured 60 MPH gust at the Osborne airport. By 1 am CDT on the 25th, storms diminished a bit in intensity, but remained strong enough to produce a measured 51 MPH gust at the Beloit airport as the initial cluster morphed into more of a northwest-southeast line segment across Jewell and Mitchell counties between 2-3 am CDT. By 4 am CDT, the last storms of the night within the local area had exited into northeast Kansas and southern Nebraska. ||Large-scale support for this event was provided by a passing, low-amplitude shortwave trough embedded within modest west-southwest flow aloft, along the southern periphery of a broad longwave trough draped through much of the central and northwest United States. Early morning surface analysis revealed a weak warm positioned just north of the area across southern Nebraska, although forecast soundings suggested storms were rooted up around 750 millibars in a zone of low-mid level theta-e advection, just above a formidable southerly low level jet of 50+ knots. Overnight severe weather parameters in North Central Kansas featured around 1500 J/kg most-unstable CAPE in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell for a few minutes. Wheat fields were damaged from the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 830-1030 pm CDT on this Sunday evening, a lone severe thunderstorm drifted slowly east, primarily impacting southeastern Smith and southwestern Jewell counties before rapidly weakening and fading away. The storm yielded a couple reports of large hail, including ping pong ball size stones nine miles south of Lebanon. ||This storm initiated shortly before sunset along a west-southwest to east-northeast oriented quasi-stationary front draped across the heart of this six-county area, and extending from eastern Colorado into southeast Nebraska. Mid-evening breezes near this boundary were easterly, as surface dewpoints averaged in the mid-upper 60s F. In the mid-upper levels, modest west-southwest flow contained a subtle impulse drifting east toward the area from northwest Kansas, well to the south of a larger, vigorous shortwave trough centered along the North Dakota-Manitoba border. Mid-evening severe weather parameters in North Central Kansas featured potent most-unstable CAPE around 3000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear around 35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size was falling for a few minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved over Northwest Kansas on the 20th from the southeast.  With little to no wind, the snow was able to fall straight down.  The only reductions in visibility occurred under the heavier bands of snow, which had snowfall rates of an inch or more an hour.  Snowfall totals ranged from 12-15 inches from Atwood to Oberlin to Hill City and Quinter.  Snowfall amounts declined to the southwest, with reports of around 8 inches at Colby, Russell Springs, and Wallace.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on the reports from surrounding counties, have counted Cheyenne county as meeting criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system ejected out of the southern Rockies and into the plains on February 21st and brought some of the highest snowfall amounts this area has seen in decades.  As of 7 pm on February 21st the Wichita Mid-Continent Airport has measured a storm total snowfall of 14.2 inches which makes this storm the 2nd worst on record!  The highest storm total snowfall for Wichita occurred back in 1962 when a storm dumped 15 inches.||One of the more unique aspects of this storm was the amount of thunder snow that took place during the early morning hours of February 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell through out the day and evening hours accumulating to very impressive amounts.  A few locations saw snow totals up to 12 inches in depth.  However, some drifts were much deeper between 2 and 3 feet.  Travel across the area was very treacherous.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday the 7th, peaking in intensity during the overnight hours and lingering in lighter form well into the daytime hours of Sunday the 8th, this was the first widespread measurable snow of the 2013-2014 winter season within this six-county portion of North Central Kansas. The majority of the area observed a range of amounts from 3 to slightly over 6 inches, with the greatest concentration of totals around 6 in Phillips, Smith and Jewell counties. In contrast, most of Mitchell County missed out on these higher totals with most places only in the 1-2 range, including 1.0 in Beloit. Thanks to cold surface temperatures that only averaged 10-15 degrees through most of the event, this was a light, fluffy snow that accumulated efficiently. Fortunately, blowing and drifting was not a significant issue as east-southeast winds only averaged 10-15 MPH. According to NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm total snow amounts featured 6.5 at Lebanon, 6.4 at Burr Oak and 6.0 at Phillipsburg, Mankato and Jewell. ||Accumulating snow first broke out over the area during the 5-7pm time frame on the 7th, but peaked in intensity between 8pm-8am, during which time modest mesoscale banding took place. Although most accumulations had already occurred by sunrise or shortly thereafter, at least on-and-off episodes of light snow or flurries continued through much of the day on the 8th before ending. On the synoptic scale aloft, this snow event was forced by a broad shortwave trough that gradually ejected east-northeast from the Intermountain West into the Central Plains. Out ahead of the primary trough axis, the main time frame of steadiest snowfall occurred as strong mid level isentropic lift developed over North Central Kansas within the entrance region of an upper jet streak centered from the southern Minnesota area across the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total snowfall of 6 inches occurred in the community of Phillipsburg, along with 5.5 inches in Long Island and 3 inches in Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of February. An unusually dry period from May through December, 2012, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas.  There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of February, as two soaking rains occurred.  One at the beginning of the month and another towards the end of the month.  The welcome rainfall actually led to the exceptional drought (D4) ending across most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across Central Kansas during the month of February. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of January and into February. Some improvement in the drought conditions did occur, as most locations across Central Kansas saw above normal rainfall for the first time in almost nine months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather continued the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of January. An unusually dry period from May through November kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas. Portions of Central Kansas continued to stay very dry, with exceptional drought (D4) conditions for most of the month of January. Most areas saw little if any rainfall during the month, which led to the drought actually getting worse across South Central Kansas during the beginning of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across Southeast Kansas during the month of January. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of December and into January. Some welcome rainfall occurred towards the end of the month, which led to the first above normal precipitation month in eight months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather continued the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of January. An unusually dry period from May through November kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas. Portions of Central Kansas continued to stay very dry, with exceptional drought (D4) conditions for most of the month of January. Most areas saw little if any rainfall during the month, which led to the drought actually getting worse across South Central Kansas during the beginning of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across Southeast Kansas during the month of January. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of December and into January. The drought intensified during the end of the month to an exceptional drought (D4), as little if any rainfall occurred during the month. This is the ninth consecutive month of below normal precipitation for Southeast Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of February. An unusually dry period from May through December, 2012, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas.  There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of February, as two soaking rains occurred.  One at the beginning of the month and another towards the end of the month.  The welcome rainfall actually led to the exceptional drought (D4) ending across most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of February. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of January and into February. Some improvement in the drought conditions did occur, as most locations across Southern Kansas saw above normal rainfall for the first time in almost nine months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system ejected out of the southern Rockies and into the plains on February 21st and brought some of the highest snowfall amounts this area has seen in decades.  As of 7 pm on February 21st the Wichita Mid-Continent Airport has measured a storm total snowfall of 14.2 inches which makes this storm the 2nd worst on record!  The highest storm total snowfall for Wichita occurred back in 1962 when a storm dumped 15 inches.||One of the more unique aspects of this storm was the amount of thunder snow that took place during the early morning hours of February 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell through out the day and evening hours accumulating to very impressive amounts.  A few locations saw snow totals up to 16 inches in depth.  However, some drifts were much deeper between 2 and 3 feet.  Travel across the area was very treacherous.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted parts of the southern and central plains with snow and ice on Friday and Saturday, December 20-21, 2013.  In Kansas there was a swath of very heavy snow stretching from southwest into northeast Kansas.  Some locations reported snowfall rates of 4 inches in only 30 minutes with near white-out conditions reported from the falling snow.  It is remarkable that there was no snow to 15 inches in less than 20 miles in distance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 15.5 inches was reported in Larned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday the 7th, peaking in intensity during the overnight hours and lingering in lighter form well into the daytime hours of Sunday the 8th, this was the first widespread measurable snow of the 2013-2014 winter season within this six-county portion of North Central Kansas. The majority of the area observed a range of amounts from 3 to slightly over 6 inches, with the greatest concentration of totals around 6 in Phillips, Smith and Jewell counties. In contrast, most of Mitchell County missed out on these higher totals with most places only in the 1-2 range, including 1.0 in Beloit. Thanks to cold surface temperatures that only averaged 10-15 degrees through most of the event, this was a light, fluffy snow that accumulated efficiently. Fortunately, blowing and drifting was not a significant issue as east-southeast winds only averaged 10-15 MPH. According to NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm total snow amounts featured 6.5 at Lebanon, 6.4 at Burr Oak and 6.0 at Phillipsburg, Mankato and Jewell. ||Accumulating snow first broke out over the area during the 5-7pm time frame on the 7th, but peaked in intensity between 8pm-8am, during which time modest mesoscale banding took place. Although most accumulations had already occurred by sunrise or shortly thereafter, at least on-and-off episodes of light snow or flurries continued through much of the day on the 8th before ending. On the synoptic scale aloft, this snow event was forced by a broad shortwave trough that gradually ejected east-northeast from the Intermountain West into the Central Plains. Out ahead of the primary trough axis, the main time frame of steadiest snowfall occurred as strong mid level isentropic lift developed over North Central Kansas within the entrance region of an upper jet streak centered from the southern Minnesota area across the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total snowfall of 6.5 inches occurred in the community of Lebanon and also 4.2 inches in Smith Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved over Northwest Kansas on the 20th from the southeast.  With little to no wind, the snow was able to fall straight down.  The only reductions in visibility occurred under the heavier bands of snow, which had snowfall rates of an inch or more an hour.  Snowfall totals ranged from 12-15 inches from Atwood to Oberlin to Hill City and Quinter.  Snowfall amounts declined to the southwest, with reports of around 8 inches at Colby, Russell Springs, and Wallace.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 7.8 inches was reported at Colby, with another report of 7 inches at Brewster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather continued the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of January. An unusually dry period from May through November kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas. Portions of Central Kansas continued to stay very dry, with exceptional drought (D4) conditions for most of the month of January. Most areas saw little if any rainfall during the month, which led to the drought actually getting worse across South Central Kansas during the beginning of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of January. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of December and into January. The drought intensified during the end of the month to an exceptional drought (D4), as little if any rainfall occurred during the month. This is the ninth consecutive month of below normal precipitation for South Central Kansas.
