EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, December 2013 marked the 19th consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued at least some portion of this six county North Central Kansas area, prolonging one of the most notable droughts in decades.  Thanks to monthly precipitation checking in well-below normal across the entire local area, the U.S. Drought Monitor noted a slight December degradation in drought classification. The primary change was that Category D1 Moderate Drought returned to some areas that had been designated as no-worse-than Category D0 Abnormally Dry for the past 3-4 months. As a result, drought classification at month���s end featured a continuation of Category D2 Severe Drought within far western Rooks County, along with roughly the northwest half of Phillips County. Meanwhile, D1 Moderate Drought not only covered the remainder of Phillips and Rooks counties as before, but now also included all of Smith and Osborne counties, along with western portions of Jewell and Mitchell counties. Eastern portions of Jewell and Mitchell continued to hold onto Category D0 Abnormally Dry (considered to be one category better than true drought).||Even though most of this six-county area totaled between 2-7��� of snow during December, essentially all locations suffered a notably dry month as most snowfall contained minimal liquid equivalent. Based on reports from around 20 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, at least 80 percent of the area totaled less than one-third of normal December precipitation (based on long-term December normals generally around 0.75). Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the lowest December precipitation totals featured 0.02 near Covert in rural Osborne County, 0.03 at Cawker City and 0.06 two miles southwest of Alton. Although still well below normal, a few of the highest monthly amounts included 0.33 at both Jewell and Mankato and 0.31 at Burr Oak. According to the USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of December, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies rated 29% short to very short and 68% adequate. Subsoil moisture supplies were slightly worse-off, rating 39% short to very short and only 59% adequate.||Looking back over the year 2013, although drought conditions were not as severe within this portion of North Central Kansas as they were in 2012, there was certainly a pronounced difference between western (drier) and eastern (wetter) areas. More specifically, much of Phillips and western Rooks counties again suffered notably below normal precipitation, while counties farther east such as Jewell and Mitchell fared considerably better, with near-normal to slightly above normal annual precipitation. As evidence of this, the driest cooperative observer site within the area during 2013 was one mile north of Long Island in Phillips County with only 13.57 inches (11.28 below normal), while the wettest site was Jewell in Jewell County with 28.39 inches (1.57 above normal). In general, however, most of the six-county area received somewhere between 75-100% of 30-year normal precipitation.

Based on reports from around 20 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, at least 80 percent of the area totaled less than one-third of normal December precipitation (based on long-term December normals generally around 0.75). Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the lowest December precipitation totals featured 0.02 near Covert in rural Osborne County, 0.03 at Cawker City and 0.06 two miles southwest of Alton. Although still well below normal, a few of the highest monthly amounts included 0.33 at both Jewell and Mankato and 0.31 at Burr Oak. According to the USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of December, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies rated 29% short to very short and 68% adequate. Subsoil moisture supplies were slightly worse-off, rating 39% short to very short and only 59% adequate.||Looking back over the year 2013, although drought conditions were not as severe within this portion of North Central Kansas as they were in 2012, there was certainly a pronounced difference between western (drier) and eastern (wetter) areas. More specifically, much of Phillips and western Rooks counties again suffered notably below normal precipitation, while counties farther east such as Jewell and Mitchell fared considerably better, with near-normal to slightly above normal annual precipitation. As evidence of this, the driest cooperative observer site within the area during 2013 was one mile north of Long Island in Phillips County with only 13.57 inches (11.28 below normal), while the wettest site was Jewell in Jewell County with 28.39 inches (1.57 above normal). In general, however, most of the six-county area received somewhere between 75-100% of 30-year normal precipitation.

A strong winter storm system moved over Northwest Kansas on the 20th from the southeast.  With little to no wind, the snow was able to fall straight down.  The only reductions in visibility occurred under the heavier bands of snow, which had snowfall rates of an inch or more an hour.  Snowfall totals ranged from 12-15 inches from Atwood to Oberlin to Hill City and Quinter.  Snowfall amounts declined to the southwest, with reports of around 8 inches at Colby, Russell Springs, and Wallace.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total snowfall of 10.7 inches was measured at Goodland, with snowfall amounts tapering off to 7 inches toward the east and southwest part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system ejected out of the southern Rockies and into the plains on February 21st and brought some of the highest snowfall amounts this area has seen in decades.  As of 7 pm on February 21st the Wichita Mid-Continent Airport has measured a storm total snowfall of 14.2 inches which makes this storm the 2nd worst on record!  The highest storm total snowfall for Wichita occurred back in 1962 when a storm dumped 15 inches.||One of the more unique aspects of this storm was the amount of thunder snow that took place during the early morning hours of February 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell through out the day and evening hours accumulating to very impressive amounts.  A few locations saw snow totals up to 10 inches in depth.  Travel across the area was very treacherous.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February was near or slightly above normal on precipitation resulting in the improvement of drought conditions but long term drought conditions continued across southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S Drought Monitor continued to report Severe Drought (D2) throughout the month of February. The COOP station near Walnut reported 3.25 of rainfall for the month of February. The COOP station near Pittsburg reported 3.40 of rainfall for the month of February. This is a continuation of the drought that began across the region in June 2012. The loss and damage estimates will be included in August's 2012 storm data section for the entire drought event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday the 7th, peaking in intensity during the overnight hours and lingering in lighter form well into the daytime hours of Sunday the 8th, this was the first widespread measurable snow of the 2013-2014 winter season within this six-county portion of North Central Kansas. The majority of the area observed a range of amounts from 3 to slightly over 6 inches, with the greatest concentration of totals around 6 in Phillips, Smith and Jewell counties. In contrast, most of Mitchell County missed out on these higher totals with most places only in the 1-2 range, including 1.0 in Beloit. Thanks to cold surface temperatures that only averaged 10-15 degrees through most of the event, this was a light, fluffy snow that accumulated efficiently. Fortunately, blowing and drifting was not a significant issue as east-southeast winds only averaged 10-15 MPH. According to NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm total snow amounts featured 6.5 at Lebanon, 6.4 at Burr Oak and 6.0 at Phillipsburg, Mankato and Jewell. ||Accumulating snow first broke out over the area during the 5-7pm time frame on the 7th, but peaked in intensity between 8pm-8am, during which time modest mesoscale banding took place. Although most accumulations had already occurred by sunrise or shortly thereafter, at least on-and-off episodes of light snow or flurries continued through much of the day on the 8th before ending. On the synoptic scale aloft, this snow event was forced by a broad shortwave trough that gradually ejected east-northeast from the Intermountain West into the Central Plains. Out ahead of the primary trough axis, the main time frame of steadiest snowfall occurred as strong mid level isentropic lift developed over North Central Kansas within the entrance region of an upper jet streak centered from the southern Minnesota area across the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 4 inches occurred near the rural community of Covert, 4 inches in Alton, and 3. 2 inches in Natoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of February. An unusually dry period from May through December, 2012, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas.  There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of February, as two soaking rains occurred.  One at the beginning of the month and another towards the end of the month.  The welcome rainfall actually led to the exceptional drought (D4) ending across most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across Central Kansas during the month of February. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of January and into February. Some improvement in the drought conditions did occur late in the month, as most locations across Central Kansas saw above normal rainfall for the first time in almost nine months.  By the end of the month, the drought conditions had decreased to a severe drought (D2).  Rainfall amounts at Salina was over an inch above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved northeast across the area January 29th and 30th, 2013. On the south side of the system, showers and thunderstorms were noted mainly south of Interstate 70. Snow was found on the northwest side of the system, with the area moving southeast across the area and ending on the 30th. Snowfall amounts were mainly from 1 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was measured at 1.5 inches in Atchison, and 3.0 inches in Effingham.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds combined with heavy snow to produce white out conditions throughout the morning and afternoon. Travel across the area was brought to a stand still.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant winter storm for the 2012-2013 season hit Southwestern Kansas Wednesday and Thursday, February 20th and 21st.  Early on the morning of February 20th, the first of two significant upper level PVA anomalies moved across western Kansas, bringing a round of snow from 1 to 6 inches to eastern counties.  By afternoon there was a 25 to 50 mile wide swath of more than 6 inches of snow from northwest Ness County southeast to the border of Comanche and Barber Counties with the Oklahoma border.  A second PVA anomaly approached from New Mexico and produced another round of heavy snow, strong winds, and resultant blizzard conditions. From 14 to 15 inches of snow fell east of a line from near Ellis in Ellis County to near Ness City in Ness County, to just northeast Garden City in Finney County, to near Meade in Meade County to near Liberal in Seward County.  Amounts of 6 inches or more fell east of a line from northern Kearny County to southwestern Finney County to extreme southeastern Morton County.  Less than 3 inches fell west of the six inch area.  In addition to the large snowfall amounts, many reports of whiteout blizzard conditions were received, as well as numerous road closures, mainly east of Highway 283. During the evening a couple severe thunderstorms developed over Northwest Kansas, producing large hail ranging from quarters to golf balls.  Many of the reports included a mention of hail covering the ground after the storms had moved through.  A few locations had hail deep enough on the road the snow plows were sent to clear the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail has nearly covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant outbreak of severe weather affected much of central and southeast Kansas. A total of 10 tornadoes touched down and strong winds along with large hail pelted the city of Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved through a farmstead and caused minor damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado moved over open country.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary and produced large tornadoes during the afternoon and evening of May 28th 2013.  2 tornadoes occurred in Nemaha county Kansas and at least 1 violent long lived tornado occurred in Ottawa County Kansas.  Amazingly no serious injuries and no fatalities were reported.  Later that night slow moving storms dumped 5 to 10 inches of rain across parts of Brown and Nemaha counties in Kansas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Concordia measured a non thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After 9pm CDT a squall line developed across north central Kansas and evolved into a bow echo.  Widespread 60 to 80 mph winds with pockets of 80-100 mph winds occurred across a large part of Ottawa, Cloud, Clay, Geary, Washington and Riley counties between 10pm and 12 am Saturday night.  At least 2 small tornadoes were confirmed that evening however most of the damage was due to straight line winds that in some cases were measured in excess of 100 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a roughly 36-hour period between the morning of Wednesday the 20th and the early evening of Thursday the 21st, this six-county portion of North Central Kansas not only experienced its heaviest snowfall event of the 2012-2013 winter season, but also one of the largest storm-total snowfalls on record for some areas. According to NWS cooperative observers, the entire area totaled at least 8-11 inches of snow, with the highest amounts of 12-18 concentrated within Phillips, Smith, Rooks and Osborne counties. Leading the way with 18 were locations such as Natoma and Covert in Osborne County, and Phillipsburg and Long Island in Phillips County. At Smith Center, 15.6 ranked as the largest two-day snowfall on record dating back to 1910. Near Plainville, 12 ranked as the ninth-largest snowfall on record since 1904. Farther east, most of Mitchell and Jewell counties dodged the heaviest snow bands, thus ending up with comparably lower totals including 8 at Hunter and Ionia, 9 at Beloit and 10.1 at Burr Oak. Although the heavy snow itself created considerable impact across the area including at least minor drifting, the magnitude of blowing snow was held in check by sustained easterly and northerly winds that generally topped out 15-20 MPH and mainly gusted under 25 MPH, keeping this storm from reaching blizzard status. Liquid-equivalent moisture in excess of one inch was common, and also welcome across the drought-stricken area, highlighted by an impressive 2.20 at Natoma.||This nearly 36-hour event consisted of several waves of steady snowfall, with brief breaks in between. The first few concentrated snow bands passed through from south-to-north on the afternoon and evening of the 20th, with much of the area totaling 3-9 by midnight. Snowfall during the pre-dawn hours of the 21st was fairly light and off-and-on, before the longest-lasting and most intense bands of the event took aim mainly between 9 AM and 5 PM, accompanied by temperatures hovering around 20 degrees. By 7 or 8 PM, all snow ended across North Central Kansas as a closed mid level low pressure system started lifting north of the area. On the synoptic scale aloft, this prolonged winter storm was forced by the approach and eventual passage of an expansive, amplified mid-upper level trough. The primary mid-level 700-500 millibar closed low tracked from southern California on the morning of the 20th, to the Texas Panhandle on the morning of the 21st, before deepening and lifting slowly north-northeast across central Kansas into southeast Nebraska during the daytime and evening hours. Periods of isentropic lift associated with smaller-scale shortwave troughs lifting out ahead of the main system were responsible for snowfall from the 20th into the morning of the 21st, with the most intense snow bands during the day on the 21st driven by pronounced deformation on the northwest periphery of the mid level low track. From a forecast perspective, this winter storm was one of the best-anticipated in recent memory thanks to unusually high consistency in computer model solutions. Although the finer details of timing and full snowfall potential did not come into focus until a few days out, signs of a potentially significant winter storm were recognized over one week in advance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 12 inches of snow fell at Alton, 18 inches of snow fell at Covert and 18 inches of snow fell at Natoma.

During the evening, a band of heavy snow moved in from Colorado.  The added effect of strong winds with the heavy snow caused visibilities to drop to almost zero as the band moved through Wallace and Sherman counties.  A peak wind gust of 56MPH was reported at the Goodland airport at 2335LT.  As the band of heavy snow moved east, the visibility sensor at Colby quit working and the wind gusts become lighter at both Colby and St. Francis.  Despite the lack of strong winds with the heavy snow, the snow itself likely caused blizzard like conditions for parts of Rawlins, Cheyenne, and Thomas counties as the band moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Using the manual observation at Goodland as a proxy, and since radar indicated the band of heavy snow was also over Wallace county, am certain blizzard conditions were experienced for Wallace county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a roughly 36-hour period between the morning of Wednesday the 20th and the early evening of Thursday the 21st, this six-county portion of North Central Kansas not only experienced its heaviest snowfall event of the 2012-2013 winter season, but also one of the largest storm-total snowfalls on record for some areas. According to NWS cooperative observers, the entire area totaled at least 8-11 inches of snow, with the highest amounts of 12-18 concentrated within Phillips, Smith, Rooks and Osborne counties. Leading the way with 18 were locations such as Natoma and Covert in Osborne County, and Phillipsburg and Long Island in Phillips County. At Smith Center, 15.6 ranked as the largest two-day snowfall on record dating back to 1910. Near Plainville, 12 ranked as the ninth-largest snowfall on record since 1904. Farther east, most of Mitchell and Jewell counties dodged the heaviest snow bands, thus ending up with comparably lower totals including 8 at Hunter and Ionia, 9 at Beloit and 10.1 at Burr Oak. Although the heavy snow itself created considerable impact across the area including at least minor drifting, the magnitude of blowing snow was held in check by sustained easterly and northerly winds that generally topped out 15-20 MPH and mainly gusted under 25 MPH, keeping this storm from reaching blizzard status. Liquid-equivalent moisture in excess of one inch was common, and also welcome across the drought-stricken area, highlighted by an impressive 2.20 at Natoma.||This nearly 36-hour event consisted of several waves of steady snowfall, with brief breaks in between. The first few concentrated snow bands passed through from south-to-north on the afternoon and evening of the 20th, with much of the area totaling 3-9 by midnight. Snowfall during the pre-dawn hours of the 21st was fairly light and off-and-on, before the longest-lasting and most intense bands of the event took aim mainly between 9 AM and 5 PM, accompanied by temperatures hovering around 20 degrees. By 7 or 8 PM, all snow ended across North Central Kansas as a closed mid level low pressure system started lifting north of the area. On the synoptic scale aloft, this prolonged winter storm was forced by the approach and eventual passage of an expansive, amplified mid-upper level trough. The primary mid-level 700-500 millibar closed low tracked from southern California on the morning of the 20th, to the Texas Panhandle on the morning of the 21st, before deepening and lifting slowly north-northeast across central Kansas into southeast Nebraska during the daytime and evening hours. Periods of isentropic lift associated with smaller-scale shortwave troughs lifting out ahead of the main system were responsible for snowfall from the 20th into the morning of the 21st, with the most intense snow bands during the day on the 21st driven by pronounced deformation on the northwest periphery of the mid level low track. From a forecast perspective, this winter storm was one of the best-anticipated in recent memory thanks to unusually high consistency in computer model solutions. Although the finer details of timing and full snowfall potential did not come into focus until a few days out, signs of a potentially significant winter storm were recognized over one week in advance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 10.1 inches of snow fell at Burr Oak, 8 inches of snow fell at Ionia, 8.8 inches of snow fell at Jewell, 11 inches of snow fell at Lovewell Dam and 15 inches of snow fell at Mankato.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March was slightly above normal on precipitation resulting in the improvement of drought conditions but long term drought conditions continued across southeast Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant winter storm for the 2012-2013 season hit Southwestern Kansas Wednesday and Thursday, February 20th and 21st.  Early on the morning of February 20th, the first of two significant upper level PVA anomalies moved across western Kansas, bringing a round of snow from 1 to 6 inches to eastern counties.  By afternoon there was a 25 to 50 mile wide swath of more than 6 inches of snow from northwest Ness County southeast to the border of Comanche and Barber Counties with the Oklahoma border.  A second PVA anomaly approached from New Mexico and produced another round of heavy snow, strong winds, and resultant blizzard conditions.  By the time this second upper level storm exited into Central Kansas during the evening on the 2st, there was from 18 to 21 inches of snowfall across eastern Ellis, most of Rush and northern Pawnee Counties.  From 14 to 15 inches of snow fell east of a line from near Ellis in Ellis County to near Ness City in Ness County, to just northeast Garden City in Finney County, to near Meade in Meade County to near Liberal in Seward County.  Amounts of 6 inches or more fell east of a line from northern Kearny County to southwestern Finney County to extreme southeastern Morton County.  Less than 3 inches fell west of the six inch area.  In addition to the large snowfall amounts, many reports of whiteout blizzard conditions were received, as well as numerous road closures, mainly east of Highway 283.   In addition, the strong northeast surface winds of 30 to 40 mph resulted in blowing and drifting snow, with snow drifts as large as 10 to 15 feet reported.

During the evening a couple severe thunderstorms developed over Northwest Kansas, producing large hail ranging from quarters to golf balls.  Many of the reports included a mention of hail covering the ground after the storms had moved through.  A few locations had hail deep enough on the road the snow plows were sent to clear the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ground is completely covered with hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with heavy snow early in the morning caused significant travel problems and near white out conditions when the weather quickly deteriorated into a blizzard. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening, a band of heavy snow moved in from Colorado.  The added effect of strong winds with the heavy snow caused visibilities to drop to almost zero as the band moved through Wallace and Sherman counties.  A peak wind gust of 56MPH was reported at the Goodland airport at 2335LT.  As the band of heavy snow moved east, the visibility sensor at Colby quit working and the wind gusts become lighter at both Colby and St. Francis.  Despite the lack of strong winds with the heavy snow, the snow itself likely caused blizzard like conditions for parts of Rawlins, Cheyenne, and Thomas counties as the band moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at St. Francis did not report blizzard conditions as the band of heavy snow moved across the county.  However neighboring Yuma, Dundy, and Sherman counties all reported blizzard conditions as the band moved through.  Even though there were no there were no impacts from the snow band reported, am thinking some of the county experienced near blizzard conditions for a brief time based on the reports from neighboring counties.

Near blizzard conditions may have occurred over parts of the county as the band of heavy snow moved through.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, March marked the tenth consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across this six-county portion of North Central Kansas.  This continued one of the worst droughts since the 1950s, as the majority of the area measured below normal March precipitation. Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, there was essentially no change in drought classification over the course of the month. At month's end, the vast majority of Phillips, Rooks, Smith and Osborne counties remained in category D3 extreme drought, while all of Jewell and Mitchell counties continued in somewhat less significant D2 severe drought. Based on reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 70 percent of the area tallied below normal March precipitation (based on normals generally between 1.70-2.15). Overall, Rooks, Osborne and western Phillips counties were the driest portions of the area, as evidenced by cooperative observer sites at Webster Dam (0.51), Logan (0.55), four miles west-northwest of Plainville (0.84) and Natoma (0.88). The limited places that observed near to slightly above normal precipitation primarily concentrated in parts of Smith, Jewell and northern Mitchell counties, including totals of 2.23 at Smith Center, 2.01 at Jewell and 1.85 at Cawker City. Much of this precipitation fell between March 9th-10th, thanks to a combination of thunderstorms followed by generally 3-7 of snow and blizzard conditions. As for March snowfall, the majority of the six-county area tallied well above normal amounts between 5-10, highlighted by 11.6 at Burr Oak and 10 at Smith Center, Mankato, Ionia and Jewell. ||Although severe to exceptional drought conditions remained firmly entrenched across North Central Kansas at the end of March, the past four months since December 2012 signaled an overall-encouraging trend, as most of the area tallied near to slightly above normal precipitation during that time, versus the solidly below normal values that defined much of the summer and fall of 2012. However, keeping optimism in check, December-March precipitation normally accounts for less than 15 percent of total annual precipitation. The Kansas USDA Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending March 31st indicated that topsoil moisture across the North Central District had improved over the past few months, now rating 30% short to very short and 68% adequate. However, subsoil moisture remained in worse shape, rating 83% short to very short. The state-averaged winter wheat crop checked in at 29% poor to very poor, 40% fair and only 31% good to excellent. State-averaged range and pasture conditions rated 79% poor to very poor, hay and forage supplies 74% short to very short and stock water supplies 67% short to very short.

A supercell thunderstorm became embedded within a line of storms and produced a small EF1 tornado near the Emporia airport.  This tornado produced a non-continuous damage path approximately 9 miles long with the majority of the damage rated EF0.  The EF1 damage was located near the airport to a home and outbuilding that had portion of the roof removed.

By afternoon there was a 25 to 50 mile wide swath of more than 6 inches of snow from northwest Ness County southeast to the border of Comanche and Barber Counties with the Oklahoma border.  A second PVA anomaly approached from New Mexico and produced another round of heavy snow, strong winds, and resultant blizzard conditions.  By the time this second upper level storm exited into Central Kansas during the evening on the 2st, there was from 18 to 21 inches of snowfall across eastern Ellis, most of Rush and northern Pawnee Counties.  From 14 to 15 inches of snow fell east of a line from near Ellis in Ellis County to near Ness City in Ness County, to just northeast Garden City in Finney County, to near Meade in Meade County to near Liberal in Seward County.  Amounts of 6 inches or more fell east of a line from northern Kearny County to southwestern Finney County to extreme southeastern Morton County.  Less than 3 inches fell west of the six inch area.  In addition to the large snowfall amounts, many reports of whiteout blizzard conditions were received, as well as numerous road closures, mainly east of Highway 283.   In addition, the strong northeast surface winds of 30 to 40 mph resulted in blowing and drifting snow, with snow drifts as large as 10 to 15 feet reported.

During the evening, a band of heavy snow moved in from Colorado.  The added effect of strong winds with the heavy snow caused visibilities to drop to almost zero as the band moved through Wallace and Sherman counties.  A peak wind gust of 56MPH was reported at the Goodland airport at 2335LT.  As the band of heavy snow moved east, the visibility sensor at Colby quit working and the wind gusts become lighter at both Colby and St. Francis.  Despite the lack of strong winds with the heavy snow, the snow itself likely caused blizzard like conditions for parts of Rawlins, Cheyenne, and Thomas counties as the band moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibilities were reported with manual observations as the band of heavy snow moved through.  A peak wind gust of 56MPH was reported at 2335LT.

Severe thunderstorms impacted parts of northern Kansas.  The most notable event occurred just west of Marysville where a strong tornado damaged approximately 30 homes and businesses.  No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were gusty with no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After 9pm CDT a squall line developed across north central Kansas and evolved into a bow echo.  Widespread 60 to 80 mph winds with pockets of 80-100 mph winds occurred across a large part of Ottawa, Cloud, Clay, Geary, Washington and Riley counties between 10pm and 12 am Saturday night.  At least 2 small tornadoes were confirmed that evening however most of the damage was due to straight line winds that in some cases were measured in excess of 100 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small tornado skipped northeast and produced EF1 damage at a home along Parallel road on the Clay and Washington county border.  Outbuildings were destroyed and grain bins were blown away. Ponderosa pines were sheared off near the stump in a narrow path to the northeast.

At least 2 small tornadoes were confirmed that evening however most of the damage was due to straight line winds that in some cases were measured in excess of 100 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The small tornado moved northeast of Parallel Road out of Clay County and dissipated after approximately 1 mile producing minor EF0 damage to trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, March marked the tenth consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across this six-county portion of North Central Kansas.  This continued one of the worst droughts since the 1950s, as the majority of the area measured below normal March precipitation. Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, there was essentially no change in drought classification over the course of the month. At month's end, the vast majority of Phillips, Rooks, Smith and Osborne counties remained in category D3 extreme drought, while all of Jewell and Mitchell counties continued in somewhat less significant D2 severe drought. Based on reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, roughly 70 percent of the area tallied below normal March precipitation (based on normals generally between 1.70-2.15). Overall, Rooks, Osborne and western Phillips counties were the driest portions of the area, as evidenced by cooperative observer sites at Webster Dam (0.51), Logan (0.55), four miles west-northwest of Plainville (0.84) and Natoma (0.88). The limited places that observed near to slightly above normal precipitation primarily concentrated in parts of Smith, Jewell and northern Mitchell counties, including totals of 2.23 at Smith Center, 2.01 at Jewell and 1.85 at Cawker City. Much of this precipitation fell between March 9th-10th, thanks to a combination of thunderstorms followed by generally 3-7 of snow and blizzard conditions. As for March snowfall, the majority of the six-county area tallied well above normal amounts between 5-10, highlighted by 11.6 at Burr Oak and 10 at Smith Center, Mankato, Ionia and Jewell. ||Although severe to exceptional drought conditions remained firmly entrenched across North Central Kansas at the end of March, the past four months since December 2012 signaled an overall-encouraging trend, as most of the area tallied near to slightly above normal precipitation during that time, versus the solidly below normal values that defined much of the summer and fall of 2012. However, keeping optimism in check, December-March precipitation normally accounts for less than 15 percent of total annual precipitation. The Kansas USDA Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending March 31st indicated that topsoil moisture across the North Central District had improved over the past few months, now rating 30% short to very short and 68% adequate. However, subsoil moisture remained in worse shape, rating 83% short to very short. The state-averaged winter wheat crop checked in at 29% poor to very poor, 40% fair and only 31% good to excellent. State-averaged range and pasture conditions rated 79% poor to very poor, hay and forage supplies 74% short to very short and stock water supplies 67% short to very short.

Supercell Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary and produced large tornadoes during the afternoon and evening of May 28th 2013.  2 tornadoes occurred in Nemaha county Kansas and at least 1 violent long lived tornado occurred in Ottawa County Kansas.  Amazingly no serious injuries and no fatalities were reported.  Later that night slow moving storms dumped 5 to 10 inches of rain across parts of Brown and Nemaha counties in Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm became embedded within a line of storms and produced a small EF1 tornado near the Emporia airport. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm swept through the Plains, February 25th through the 27th, resulting in the second round of heavy snows, that the region had experienced in under a week.  Temperatures near freezing led to a heavy wet snow that was slow to accumulate at the onset of the event. However, as snowfall rates increased overnight, snow rapidly began to accumulate, sticking to trees and powerlines, and resulting in numerous power outages across the area. Unlike the previous event from 5 days prior, the heaviest snow fell overnight and not during the morning commute. This allowed road crews open access to the highways during the peak of the event, even though they still struggled to keep up with snowfall rates as much as 2 inches per hour. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow was measured in Olathe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant winter storm for the 2012-2013 season hit Southwestern Kansas Wednesday and Thursday, February 20th and 21st.  Early on the morning of February 20th, the first of two significant upper level PVA anomalies moved across western Kansas, bringing a round of snow from 1 to 6 inches to eastern counties.  By afternoon there was a 25 to 50 mile wide swath of more than 6 inches of snow from northwest Ness County southeast to the border of Comanche and Barber Counties with the Oklahoma border.  A second PVA anomaly approached from New Mexico and produced another round of heavy snow, strong winds, and resultant blizzard conditions.  By the time this second upper level storm exited into Central Kansas during the evening on the 2st, there was from 18 to 21 inches of snowfall across eastern Ellis, most of Rush and northern Pawnee Counties.  From 14 to 15 inches of snow fell east of a line from near Ellis in Ellis County to near Ness City in Ness County, to just northeast Garden City in Finney County, to near Meade in Meade County to near Liberal in Seward County.  Amounts of 6 inches or more fell east of a line from northern Kearny County to southwestern Finney County to extreme southeastern Morton County.  Less than 3 inches fell west of the six inch area.  In addition to the large snowfall amounts, many reports of whiteout blizzard conditions were received, as well as numerous road closures, mainly east of Highway 283.   In addition, the strong northeast surface winds of 30 to 40 mph resulted in blowing and drifting snow, with snow drifts as large as 10 to 15 feet reported.

After 9pm CDT a squall line developed across north central Kansas and evolved into a bow echo. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm swept through the Plains, February 25th through the 27th, resulting in the second round of heavy snows, that the region had experienced in under a week.  Temperatures near freezing led to a heavy wet snow that was slow to accumulate at the onset of the event. However, as snowfall rates increased overnight, snow rapidly began to accumulate, sticking to trees and powerlines, and resulting in numerous power outages across the area. Unlike the previous event from 5 days prior, the heaviest snow fell overnight and not during the morning commute. This allowed road crews open access to the highways during the peak of the event, even though they still struggled to keep up with snowfall rates as much as 2 inches per hour. By the time the morning commute rolled around, most roads were snow-covered, but the combination of snow decreasing in intensity and many more people that chose to sit this one out, the morning commute was not nearly as disastrous as with the February 21 event.||The most snowfall in extreme eastern Kansas, was the 10 inches measured near |Basehor and in Bonner Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow was measured near Basehor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought a mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow to extreme southeastern Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks during the day of February 21. Precipitation which started as light snow transitioned over to freezing rain and sleet.  The winter precipitation was accompanied by thunderstorms which produced heavier rounds of sleet and freezing rain.  One to two inches of sleet was common along with a glaze of ice ranging from a trace to one quarter of an inch. Locally higher accumulations of freezing rain up to one half of an inch occurred in far south central Missouri resulting in downed tree limbs and power outages. Further north across portions of west central and central Missouri snow accumulations ranged from one to three inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought a mix of snow and sleet accompanied by thunder.  Sleet accumulations ranged from one to two inches with snow accumulations ranging from one to two inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow was measured west of Paola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary and produced large tornadoes during the afternoon and evening of May 28th 2013.  2 tornadoes occurred in Nemaha county Kansas and at least 1 violent long lived tornado occurred in Ottawa County Kansas.  Amazingly no serious injuries and no fatalities were reported.  Later that night slow moving storms dumped 5 to 10 inches of rain across parts of Brown and Nemaha counties in Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At intersection of K106 and Evergreen road water flowing over road several inches deep for a stretch of 300 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second official blizzard of the season pounded most of this six-county portion of North Central Kansas on this Sunday. The greatest impacts were realized within Smith, Osborne, Mitchell and especially Jewell counties, as generally 3-9 inches of snow whipped around in strong sustained north-northwest winds of 25-35 MPH that sometimes gusted over 40 MPH. Although Phillips and Rooks counties also endured wintry conditions, snowfall amounts there averaged no more than 1-3 inches and visibility reductions were not as extreme. Snow first started developing in southwest-to-northeast bands around midnight, after transitioning from a rain/sleet mix that fell on the evening of the 9th. By sunrise, 3-5 inches had fallen across much of the area with higher amounts to 6 inches reported at Burr Oak, which was positioned under a particularly heavy snow band especially between 4-9 AM CDT. Although most of the accumulating snow ended within Phillips and Rooks counties by mid-morning, steady light to moderate snow bands persisted in much of the other four counties well into the afternoon hours before finally tapering off to flurries and largely ending from south to north by 5 PM CDT. However, at least minor blowing snow issues continued well into the evening hours as sustained winds slowly eased below 20 MPH. ||Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest storm-total snow amounts included 9 at Burr Oak, 7 at Ionia and Jewell, 6 at Smith Center and Cawker City, and 5 at Beloit, Hunter and Covert. In contrast, locations farther west such as Logan only measured 0.5. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was powered by the passage of a highly amplified and slightly positively tilted mid-upper trough that slowly lumbered across the Central United States. At 700 millibars, the heaviest snow bands concentrated under a pronounced deformation zone on the northwest periphery of a closed low/vort max that tracked from southwest Kansas on the evening of the 9th to southern Iowa by late evening on the 10th. At mid-afternoon on the 10th, surface maps confirmed that strong north winds and blizzard conditions were promoted by a tight pressure gradient between a 1004 millibar low over north central Missouri and a 1022 millibar ridge extending southward into northwest Nebraska. Enhancing the March misery across North Central Kansas were daytime temperatures only averaging in the mid-upper 20s, yielding wind chill values generally 7-15 degrees above zero.

After 9pm CDT a squall line developed across north central Kansas and evolved into a bow echo.  Widespread 60 to 80 mph winds with pockets of 80-100 mph winds occurred across a large part of Ottawa, Cloud, Clay, Geary, Washington and Riley counties between 10pm and 12 am Saturday night.  At least 2 small tornadoes were confirmed that evening however most of the damage was due to straight line winds that in some cases were measured in excess of 100 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trailers flipped over and 6 inch tree limb through the side of a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of February. An unusually dry period from May through December, 2012, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas.  There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of February, as two soaking rains occurred.  One at the beginning of the month and another towards the end of the month.  The welcome rainfall actually led to the exceptional drought (D4) ending across most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across Central Kansas during the month of February. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of January and into February. Some improvement in the drought conditions did occur, as most locations across Central Kansas saw above normal rainfall for the first time in almost nine months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought a mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow to extreme southeastern Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks during the day of February 21. Precipitation which started as light snow transitioned over to freezing rain and sleet.  The winter precipitation was accompanied by thunderstorms which produced heavier rounds of sleet and freezing rain.  One to two inches of sleet was common along with a glaze of ice ranging from a trace to one quarter of an inch. Locally higher accumulations of freezing rain up to one half of an inch occurred in far south central Missouri resulting in downed tree limbs and power outages. Further north across portions of west central and central Missouri snow accumulations ranged from one to three inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought a mix of freezing rain and sleet accompanied by thunder.  Sleet accumulations ranged from one half to one and a half inches with freezing rain  accumulations ranging from a trace to one tenth of an inch.

Widespread 60 to 80 mph winds with pockets of 80-100 mph winds occurred across a large part of Ottawa, Cloud, Clay, Geary, Washington and Riley counties between 10pm and 12 am Saturday night.  At least 2 small tornadoes were confirmed that evening however most of the damage was due to straight line winds that in some cases were measured in excess of 100 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trailer home slid off the foundation near Lilly and Lyons Creek rd. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm became embedded within a line of storms and produced a small EF1 tornado near the Emporia airport.  This tornado produced a non-continuous damage path approximately 9 miles long with the majority of the damage rated EF0.  The EF1 damage was located near the airport to a home and outbuilding that had portion of the roof removed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Ramona volunteer FD of a brief tornado touchdown north of town in Dickinson County.  No damage and circulation on ground was only visible briefly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of high winds and high snow fall amounts caused treacherous conditions across much of South Central and portions of Central and Southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with heavy snow early in the morning caused significant travel problems and near white out conditions when the weather quickly deteriorated into a blizzard. The winds slightly diminished later in the day, but they still caused widespread blowing and drifting snow which only worsened when another batch of moderate snow moved through in the evening and continued for most of the night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major early-spring winter storm tracked across the Interstate 70 corridor, March 23rd through 24th, 2013. Widespread snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell in Overland Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued from January with northwest Kansas rated as being in Exceptional (D4) Drought.  February precipitation was highly variable, with 65% of normal in Hoxie and Sharon Springs, 120% of normal in Hill City and Dresden, 170% of normal near Atwood and Lenora and 235% of normal near Russell Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, February marked the ninth consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across this six-county portion of North Central Kansas, continuing one of the worst droughts since the 1950s. However, some slight improvements were noted during the month thanks to above normal precipitation across nearly the entire area. Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, all or most of Phillips, Rooks, Smith and Osborne counties started the month in category D4 exceptional drought (the worst possible category), but improved to category D3 extreme drought by month's end. Farther east, most of Jewell and Mitchell counties stayed in category D2 severe drought throughout the month. Based on reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, almost all of North Central Kansas observed above normal February precipitation (based on normals generally between 0.50-0.75). Of greater significance, limited areas tallied at least twice-normal February precipitation. For most places, the majority of monthly precipitation fell during a major winter storm that began during the daytime hours on the 20th and peaked in intensity that night and into the day of the 21st. This storm, which gave some places one of their highest storm-total snowfalls on record, dumped anywhere from 8-18 of snow across the six-county area, with the highest amounts concentrated over Phillips, Rooks and Osborne counties. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest February precipitation totals featured 2.26 at Natoma, 1.68 at Phillipsburg and 1.39 at Logan and Webster Dam. Leading the drier side of things were sites such as Beloit (0.55) and Burr Oak (0.62). ||Although severe to extreme drought conditions remained firmly entrenched across the six-county area, prevailing above normal precipitation during the meteorological winter season of December-February at least put a dent in the dryness heading into the spring. However, keeping optimism in check, precipitation during the meteorological winter season of December-February normally accounts for less than 10 percent of total annual precipitation, thus minimizing the effect of even major snow storms on drought relief. The February USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition report indicated a slight improvement in state-averaged winter wheat conditions, although the crop still rated 36 percent poor to very poor and only 23% good to excellent. Topsoil moisture also improved slightly during the month, but still averaged 61% short to very short. Statewide range and pasture conditions remained over 80% poor to very poor, and hay and forage along with stock water supplies continued to hover between 70-80% short to very short.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of March. An unusually dry period from May through December, 2012, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Central and Southern Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of February, as two soaking rains occurred, which led to some improvement of the drought going into early March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across Central Kansas during the month of March. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of February and into March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After 9pm CDT a squall line developed across north central Kansas and evolved into a bow echo.  Widespread 60 to 80 mph winds with pockets of 80-100 mph winds occurred across a large part of Ottawa, Cloud, Clay, Geary, Washington and Riley counties between 10pm and 12 am Saturday night. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance developed and moved into the inter-mountain west.  Ahead of this wave, a surface low pressure set up in the lee of the Rockies.  This surface low pressure system had a strong warm sector over southern and eastern Kansas where temperatures were in the upper 60s and low 70s with 50 to 60 degree dew point temperatures.  Also associated with this surface low was a very strong cold front.  As the upper wave moved eastward the surface cold front moved southeast.  This cold front was cold polar arctic air, but was very shallow, only extended a few thousand feet above ground.  What does this mean?  Well the mid and upper portions of the atmosphere over Nebraska, Kansas and Oklahoma were relatively warm, while the shallow cold air at the surface was plunging temperatures into the 20s and 30s. ||  The front moved through during the afternoon of April 9th, causing winds to instantly switch 180 degrees and temperatures to drop nearly 20 degrees in 10 to 15 minutes.  The cold air behind this front was so strong, the shallow surface front pushed into Oklahoma and Arkansas. ||  The combination of the shallow cold air and the warm mid and upper levels causes precipitation to fall as rain while surface temperatures are at or well below 32 degrees.  The rain that fell accumulated on elevated surfaces and froze.  Ice accumulations of a quarter of an inch up to a half of an inch were reported across Central and South Central Kansas.  The ground temperatures had been so warm that the non-elevated roadways were not as slick as elevated overpasses.  ||  Reports of car accidents and tree damage as well as power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter of an inch of ice was observed at the National Weather Service office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front pushed south across most of the Kansas during the afternoon hours of April 9th.  As this front pushed south, the clash of the moist and unstable air to the east of the front and the cold arctic air to the west, led to thunderstorms developing along and over the top of the front in the colder air. This setup led to elevated thunderstorms developing and producing large hail in an unusually cold surface environment.

A prolonged period of dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of April. An unusually dry period from May 2012 through January, 2013, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Central and Southern Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of April, as spring showers with soaking rains occurred, which led to some improvement of the drought by the end of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across Central Kansas during the month of April. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May 2012 and continued through the month of March and into April.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: KWCH Channel 12 reported the hail.

A prolonged period of dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of March. An unusually dry period from May through December, 2012, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Central and Southern Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of February, as two soaking rains occurred, which led to some improvement of the drought going into early March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across Central Kansas during the month of March. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of February and into March. Some improvement in the drought conditions did occur, as most locations across Central Kansas saw above normal rainfall for the first time in almost nine months which led to the drought dropping to severe (D2) by the middle of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lived and long tracked high precipitation supercell produced very large and damaging hail across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hen egg to tennis ball size hail was reported in Fort Scott, Kansas. It was estimated that two thirds of the homes in Fort Scott had damage because of hail. Windows to houses and cars were broken across the Fort Scott area. Damage was reported to siding and roofing of homes. The courthouse lost 66 windows as well as suffered damage to the roof, air conditioning and weather unit. One auto dealership had over a million dollar in damages to cars. The estimate damage cost for this storm for the entire county will be in this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundary, left over from the the previous evenings activity, caused penny sized hail across Linn County, during the early morning hours of May 20, 2013.

A large upper level trough of low pressure was observed across the Central Plains. A disturbance in the broad upper level trough, combined with an outflow boundary moving north into east central Kansas, helped produce a severe thunderstorm with one inch diameter hail, across Miami County, during the afternoon hours of May 20, 2013.

An upper level disturbance moved east-northeast into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri. A strong cap was observed across the area, so only a couple of severe thunderstorms were able to initiate, during the late evening hours of May 18, 2013. Before dissipating, as they exited into Missouri, the thunderstorms were able to generate damaging winds in Atchison County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated up to 60 mph. The wind caused a 24 inch diameter tree to be blown over, and several other tree branches to be damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Associated with increased lift from a strong upper level trough sliding east out of the Rocky Mountains and onto the Plains, narrow north-south bands of thunderstorms developed on this Saturday afternoon along an accompanying surface cold front.  These thunderstorms first developed roughly in the area of the Highway 281 corridor, before shifting east during the afternoon and evening hours as the parent surface low tracked into eastern Kansas. A very narrow axis of modest instability in the range of 500 to 1000 J/kg nosed into eastern portions of North Central Kansas during the afternoon and combined with strong 0-6 kilometer deep layer shear values in excess of 60 knots, resulting in an environment supportive of a few strong thunderstorms.  Small hail was reported with some of these storms, with the largest report of nickel size hail occurring north of Esbon in Jewell County.

Drought conditions continued from February across northwest Kansas.  All of northwest Kansas has been rated as being in either Extreme (D3) or Exceptional (D4) Drought at the end of March.  Moisture was highly variable during the month.  Observing stations in Greeley, Wichita and Graham counties received 56-73% of normal precipitation.  Elsewhere, moisture was more abundant with stations reporting 100-147% of normal precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance developed and moved into the inter-mountain west.  Ahead of this wave, a surface low pressure set up in the lee of the Rockies.  This surface low pressure system had a strong warm sector over southern and eastern Kansas where temperatures were in the upper 60s and low 70s with 50 to 60 degree dew point temperatures.  Also associated with this surface low was a very strong cold front.  As the upper wave moved eastward the surface cold front moved southeast.  This cold front was cold polar arctic air, but was very shallow, only extended a few thousand feet above ground.  What does this mean?  Well the mid and upper portions of the atmosphere over Nebraska, Kansas and Oklahoma were relatively warm, while the shallow cold air at the surface was plunging temperatures into the 20s and 30s. ||  The front moved through during the afternoon of April 9th, causing winds to instantly switch 180 degrees and temperatures to drop nearly 20 degrees in 10 to 15 minutes.  The cold air behind this front was so strong, the shallow surface front pushed into Oklahoma and Arkansas. ||  The combination of the shallow cold air and the warm mid and upper levels causes precipitation to fall as rain while surface temperatures are at or well below 32 degrees.  The rain that fell accumulated on elevated surfaces and froze.  Ice accumulations of a quarter of an inch up to a half of an inch were reported across Central and South Central Kansas.  The ground temperatures had been so warm that the non-elevated roadways were not as slick as elevated overpasses.  ||  Reports of car accidents and tree damage as well as power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to one half an inch of ice accumulation was noted on area trees and power lines.  Several people were without power and the roads were very slick across the county.

Drought conditions continued from February across northwest Kansas.  All of northwest Kansas has been rated as being in either Extreme (D3) or Exceptional (D4) Drought at the end of March.  Moisture was highly variable during the month.  Observing stations in Greeley, Wichita and Graham counties received 56-73% of normal precipitation.  Elsewhere, moisture was more abundant with stations reporting 100-147% of normal precipitation.

A strong upper level disturbance developed and moved into the inter-mountain west.  Ahead of this wave, a surface low pressure set up in the lee of the Rockies.  This surface low pressure system had a strong warm sector over southern and eastern Kansas where temperatures were in the upper 60s and low 70s with 50 to 60 degree dew point temperatures.  Also associated with this surface low was a very strong cold front.  As the upper wave moved eastward the surface cold front moved southeast.  This cold front was cold polar arctic air, but was very shallow, only extended a few thousand feet above ground.  What does this mean?  Well the mid and upper portions of the atmosphere over Nebraska, Kansas and Oklahoma were relatively warm, while the shallow cold air at the surface was plunging temperatures into the 20s and 30s. ||  The front moved through during the afternoon of April 9th, causing winds to instantly switch 180 degrees and temperatures to drop nearly 20 degrees in 10 to 15 minutes.  The cold air behind this front was so strong, the shallow surface front pushed into Oklahoma and Arkansas. ||  The combination of the shallow cold air and the warm mid and upper levels causes precipitation to fall as rain while surface temperatures are at or well below 32 degrees.  The rain that fell accumulated on elevated surfaces and froze.  Ice accumulations of a quarter of an inch up to a half of an inch were reported across Central and South Central Kansas.  The ground temperatures had been so warm that the non-elevated roadways were not as slick as elevated overpasses.  ||  Reports of car accidents and tree damage as well as power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice up to one half inch in diameter was causing limbs and power lines to fall due to the extra weight.  The roads were also very slick and several accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This small, short-lived event was the result of two different storms on this Sunday. One storm was an apparent supercell which tracked east along the southern borders of Rooks and Osborne counties. This storm was responsible for producing several reports of hail, the largest of which was the size of ping pong balls, just south of Plainville. The other severe weather occurred with a short line segment of storms, over Phillips county, that transitioned into a small-scale bow echo. This line of storms headed east across Smith and Jewell counties producing sub-severe hail and isolated wind damage, including estimated winds up to 70 mph. A car port was also blown onto a house near Lebanon.||The tail end of a cold front, associated with a low pressure system crossing the Great Lakes, sagged to the northern Kansas border by late afternoon. Temperatures in the 70s, with dewpoints in the 40s, was not initially conducive to thunderstorms. However, southerly winds drew dewpoints in the low to mid 50s northward. This provided just enough instability to result in thunderstorms over north-central Kansas around mid-afternoon. So, while deep layer shear was supportive of severe thunderstorms (30-40 knots), instability was marginally sufficient with mean layer CAPE ranging from 500 to 1000 J/kg.

Sub-severe hail fell from one of numerous elevated thunderstorms that formed in a strong pulse of warm air advection and frontogenesis this Tuesday night. A shallow continental-polar air mass was in place over the northern two-thirds of the United States. With a deep upper-level trough, over the western U.S., moisture and warm air advection were occurring aloft and there was enough instability to initiate thunderstorms. While surface and mean-layer parcels were stable, the most-unstable parcels resulted in 500-1000 J/kg of CAPE.

A cluster of showers and thunderstorms over Central and Southeast Kansas led to severe thunderstorms. This strong warm advection pattern led to supercells to develop, which produced large hail as big as golf balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The half dollar sized hail covered the roadway.

Extremely powerful severe thunderstorms erupted over Central Kansas in the afternoon, then went on a rampage across South-Central and Southeast Kansas from late in the afternoon through late that evening. Early in the event, the severe thunderstorms produced very large hail, as large as softballs, then evolved into prolific damaging wind producers. Widespread 70-90 mph winds occurred, no doubt causing tremendous damage to trees, many of which were quite large. A few trees were around 20-feet tall with 2-3 foot diameter trunks. Several trees fell onto houses that, in a few cases, caused roof damage. Damage to power lines and power poles was certainly widespread and resulted in numerous power outages. Around 25,000 residences lost power in the Wichita Metro area alone; with around 4,000 still without power as of the 30th. Many houses sustained roof damage, especially in Maize and Northwest Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the time the 92-mph gust occurred, the anemometer snapped. Numerous large trees uprooted with power lines and power poles down all across town.

Supercell Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary and produced large tornadoes during the afternoon and evening of May 28th 2013.  2 tornadoes occurred in Nemaha county Kansas and at least 1 violent long lived tornado occurred in Ottawa County Kansas.  Amazingly no serious injuries and no fatalities were reported.  Later that night slow moving storms dumped 5 to 10 inches of rain across parts of Brown and Nemaha counties in Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The resident of the EF-1 house south of the EF-3 farm described seeing several sub-tornadoes along with the main one.  It appears that a smaller satellite tornado hit this location producing EF1 damage to the south of the main strong tornado.  This would have been the 3rd tornado associated with this supercell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief touchdown with house and tree damage.  This was the last tornado associated with the supercell thunderstorm that produced the strong tornado to the west of Corning earlier.

A strong arctic cold front pushed south across most of the Kansas during the afternoon hours of April 9th.  As this front pushed south, the clash of the moist and unstable air to the east of the front and the cold arctic air to the west, led to thunderstorms developing along and over the top of the front in the colder air. This setup led to elevated thunderstorms developing and producing large hail in an unusually cold surface environment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lived and long tracked high precipitation supercell produced very large and damaging hail across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported relayed by Fort Scott Police Department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of April. An unusually dry period from May 2012 through January, 2013, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Central and Southern Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of April, as spring showers with soaking rains occurred, which led to some improvement of the drought by the end of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across Central Kansas during the month of April. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May 2012, and continued through the month of March and into April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This small, short-lived event was the result of two different storms on this Sunday. One storm was an apparent supercell which tracked east along the southern borders of Rooks and Osborne counties. This storm was responsible for producing several reports of hail, the largest of which was the size of ping pong balls, just south of Plainville. The other severe weather occurred with a short line segment of storms, over Phillips county, that transitioned into a small-scale bow echo. This line of storms headed east across Smith and Jewell counties producing sub-severe hail and isolated wind damage, including estimated winds up to 70 mph. A car port was also blown onto a house near Lebanon.||The tail end of a cold front, associated with a low pressure system crossing the Great Lakes, sagged to the northern Kansas border by late afternoon. Temperatures in the 70s, with dewpoints in the 40s, was not initially conducive to thunderstorms. However, southerly winds drew dewpoints in the low to mid 50s northward. This provided just enough instability to result in thunderstorms over north-central Kansas around mid-afternoon. So, while deep layer shear was supportive of severe thunderstorms (30-40 knots), instability was marginally sufficient with mean layer CAPE ranging from 500 to 1000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail covered the roadways near the intersection of roads 340 and D.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance developed and moved into the inter-mountain west.  Ahead of this wave, a surface low pressure set up in the lee of the Rockies.  This surface low pressure system had a strong warm sector over southern and eastern Kansas where temperatures were in the upper 60s and low 70s with 50 to 60 degree dew point temperatures.  Also associated with this surface low was a very strong cold front.  As the upper wave moved eastward the surface cold front moved southeast.  This cold front was cold polar arctic air, but was very shallow, only extended a few thousand feet above ground.  What does this mean?  Well the mid and upper portions of the atmosphere over Nebraska, Kansas and Oklahoma were relatively warm, while the shallow cold air at the surface was plunging temperatures into the 20s and 30s. ||  The front moved through during the afternoon of April 9th, causing winds to instantly switch 180 degrees and temperatures to drop nearly 20 degrees in 10 to 15 minutes.  The cold air behind this front was so strong, the shallow surface front pushed into Oklahoma and Arkansas. ||  The combination of the shallow cold air and the warm mid and upper levels causes precipitation to fall as rain while surface temperatures are at or well below 32 degrees.  The rain that fell accumulated on elevated surfaces and froze.  Ice accumulations of a quarter of an inch up to a half of an inch were reported across Central and South Central Kansas.  The ground temperatures had been so warm that the non-elevated roadways were not as slick as elevated overpasses.  ||  Reports of car accidents and tree damage as well as power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter of inch of ice was measured.  This caused very slick conditions across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure over central Kansas, initiated a large area of strong to severe thunderstorms. This area of thunderstorms moved east across extreme eastern Kansas, during the late afternoon and early evening hours of May 19, 2013. There were several reports of damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with very heavy rain, caused flash flooding in Johnson County, during the early morning hours of May 31, 2013. A strong low level jet, combined with an unstable airmass, led to the thunderstorm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cars were reported stranded due to high water, around 151st and Lindenwood, and at 139th and Mur-Lin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary, combined with a warm and humid airmass, led to a severe thunderstorm, that produced damaging winds and flash flooding, across Linn County, during the late afternoon hours of May 31, 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated up to 60 mph. The winds blew a roof off of a small outbuilding across a street. Small tree limbs were also broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary and produced large tornadoes during the afternoon and evening of May 28th 2013.  2 tornadoes occurred in Nemaha county Kansas and at least 1 violent long lived tornado occurred in Ottawa County Kansas.  Amazingly no serious injuries and no fatalities were reported.  Later that night slow moving storms dumped 5 to 10 inches of rain across parts of Brown and Nemaha counties in Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video shows a brief rope tornado touch down around 533pm CDT nearly 12 minutes prior to the main violent tornado touch down.

A prolonged period of dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of February. An unusually dry period from May through December, 2012, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas.  There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of February, as two soaking rains occurred.  One at the beginning of the month and another towards the end of the month.  The welcome rainfall actually led to the exceptional drought (D4) ending across most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across Southeast Kansas during the month of February. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of January and into February. Some welcome rainfall occurred towards the end of the month, which led to back to back above normal precipitation months.  This is the first time this has occurred in eight months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of April. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slipped into northern Kansas and became stationary very early this Monday morning. Rain showers occurred along the front through the overnight hours of Sunday.  By dawn, overnight showers departed to the east, while more showers formed over northwestern Kansas. Some of these showers strengthened into thunderstorms. The strongest storm formed over Rooks County. While brief, the storm was strong enough to produce multiple reports of hail, the largest of which was 1 inch in diameter, just west of Stockton. The reason for the transition to thunderstorms was the northward transport of moisture in the presence of warm air advection. It was just enough to destabilize the atmosphere, with most unstable CAPE between 500 and 1000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of dimes covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Disorganized storms moved into the area during the evening hours of May 8th after initiating over western and central Kansas. Most of Kansas was within the warm sector as a mid-level trough advanced across the region. The instability was modest although the shear was relatively weak which inhibited persistent updrafts. The severe weather that was produced over the area was isolated and short lived.

A boundary stalled across portions of the area near the Kansas Nebraska border. As daytime heating weakened the cap, surface cape values approach 2500-3000 j/kg. With instability in place and the cap eroded storms were able to develop along the low-level convergence zone near the boundary. Mid-level flow was very weak as a upper level system passed to the south across Oklahoma. Therefore the updrafts did not persist and the storms were nearly stationary, quickly dissipating after sunset.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of March. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of February and into March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slipped into northern Kansas and became stationary very early this Monday morning. Rain showers occurred along the front through the overnight hours of Sunday.  By dawn, overnight showers departed to the east, while more showers formed over northwestern Kansas. Some of these showers strengthened into thunderstorms. The strongest storm formed over Rooks County. While brief, the storm was strong enough to produce multiple reports of hail, the largest of which was 1 inch in diameter, just west of Stockton. The reason for the transition to thunderstorms was the northward transport of moisture in the presence of warm air advection. It was just enough to destabilize the atmosphere, with most unstable CAPE between 500 and 1000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ground was completely covered with pea to penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Disorganized storms moved into the area during the evening hours of May 8th after initiating over western and central Kansas. Most of Kansas was within the warm sector as a mid-level trough advanced across the region. The instability was modest although the shear was relatively weak which inhibited persistent updrafts. The severe weather that was produced over the area was isolated and short lived.

A boundary stalled across portions of the area near the Kansas Nebraska border. As daytime heating weakened the cap, surface cape values approach 2500-3000 j/kg. With instability in place and the cap eroded storms were able to develop along the low-level convergence zone near the boundary. Mid-level flow was very weak as a upper level system passed to the south across Oklahoma. Therefore the updrafts did not persist and the storms were nearly stationary, quickly dissipating after sunset.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant outbreak of severe weather affected much of central and southeast Kansas. A total of 10 tornadoes touched down and strong winds along with large hail pelted the city of Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tornado moved across rural areas in Sedgwick county causing damage to a few farmsteads.  None of the major structures took a direct hit from the tornado.  Most of the damage was limited to power poles, barns, silos, and farm equipment.  A horse trailer was thrown for approximately 220 yards by the tornado.  The Doppler on Wheels was also in the vicinity and recorded 155 mph winds at 70 meters above ground level.  This would be an EF3, however, based on damage it did not appear that wind speeds at that level reached the surface.  Hence the EF2 rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved southeast into Cheyenne county during the early afternoon.  The storm slowly moved through the county dumping three inches of rainfall and producing severe wind gusts, with estimated speeds of 70 MPH.  Flash flooding was reported 13 miles NW of St. Francis where the bridge over Hackberry Creek was covered with a foot of water.||As the afternoon progressed the storm continued to move southeast.  As it did so, more storms developed ahead of the initial storm to the southeast through most of Northwest Kansas.  Penny size hail from one of the storms, and a peak wind gust of 56 MPH were both reported at Goodland.
