EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance developed and moved into the inter-mountain west.  Ahead of this wave, a surface low pressure set up in the lee of the Rockies.  This surface low pressure system had a strong warm sector over southern and eastern Kansas where temperatures were in the upper 60s and low 70s with 50 to 60 degree dew point temperatures.  Also associated with this surface low was a very strong cold front.  As the upper wave moved eastward the surface cold front moved southeast.  This cold front was cold polar arctic air, but was very shallow, only extended a few thousand feet above ground.  What does this mean?  Well the mid and upper portions of the atmosphere over Nebraska, Kansas and Oklahoma were relatively warm, while the shallow cold air at the surface was plunging temperatures into the 20s and 30s. ||  The front moved through during the afternoon of April 9th, causing winds to instantly switch 180 degrees and temperatures to drop nearly 20 degrees in 10 to 15 minutes.  The cold air behind this front was so strong, the shallow surface front pushed into Oklahoma and Arkansas. ||  The combination of the shallow cold air and the warm mid and upper levels causes precipitation to fall as rain while surface temperatures are at or well below 32 degrees.  The rain that fell accumulated on elevated surfaces and froze.  Ice accumulations of a quarter of an inch up to a half of an inch were reported across Central and South Central Kansas.  The ground temperatures had been so warm that the non-elevated roadways were not as slick as elevated overpasses.  ||  Reports of car accidents and tree damage as well as power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One half an inch of ice was causing tree limbs to fall down.  Several power lines were also falling due to the weight of the ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary, combined with a warm and humid airmass, led to a severe thunderstorm, that produced damaging winds and flash flooding, across Linn County, during the late afternoon hours of May 31, 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half foot of water was reported over Highway 31, just west of Ingram Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary over Doniphan County, combined with a very warm and humid airmass, produced thunderstorms with very heavy rains, during the evening hours of May 28, 2013. There were two reports of flash flooding in Doniphan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 155 was reported washed out, due to heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary over Doniphan County, combined with a very warm and humid airmass, produced thunderstorms with very heavy rains, during the evening hours of May 28, 2013. There were two reports of flash flooding in Doniphan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 36 had a report of 8 inches of standing water on it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely powerful severe thunderstorms erupted over Central Kansas in the afternoon, then went on a rampage across South-Central and Southeast Kansas from late in the afternoon through late that evening. Early in the event, the severe thunderstorms produced very large hail, as large as softballs, then evolved into prolific damaging wind producers. Widespread 70-90 mph winds occurred, no doubt causing tremendous damage to trees, many of which were quite large. A few trees were around 20-feet tall with 2-3 foot diameter trunks. Several trees fell onto houses that, in a few cases, caused roof damage. Damage to power lines and power poles was certainly widespread and resulted in numerous power outages. Around 25,000 residences lost power in the Wichita Metro area alone; with around 4,000 still without power as of the 30th. Many houses sustained roof damage, especially in Maize and Northwest Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the late afternoon and into the evening of July 9, 2013 temperatures were very hot. Some areas reached a temperature of 108 and heat index up to 114. The dew points across the area ranged from the mid 60s to mid 70s. Temperatures and high dew points led to a very unstable environment as a surface boundary approached. With weak deep layer shear pulse like thunderstorms developed over central KS which moved along the boundary causing new updrafts to from. Isolated winds occurred in certain areas along the large outflow, and isolated large hail occurred with the strongest updrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report. Hail ranged from dimes to quarters and was wind driven. Wind estimated around 40 to 50 mph. There was minor crop damage report to several crops in the area. Rainfall total for the storm was approx 1 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this portion of North Central Kansas narrowly missed a more widespread onslaught of severe weather that impacted parts of central and northeast Kansas on this Tuesday afternoon and evening, a pair of severe storms snuck into Mitchell County and dropped quarter to golf ball size hail in the Hunter and Tipton areas. These storms primarily resided within the county between 730-930 pm CDT, as both of them peaked in intensity for around one hour before quickly dissipating. The first storm, which moved into areas east of Hunter out of northern Lincoln County, was a northwest-drifting offshoot from a nearly stationary cluster of severe storms centered over Ottawa County. The second storm, which formed slightly west of the first, tracked north across far western Mitchell County including the Tipton area. ||One of the main reasons this area of North Central Kansas was spared a more widespread severe weather threat was its position just northwest of a fairly well-defined quasi-stationary front stretched from west central to northeast Kansas. During the afternoon and early evening hours, the vast majority of severe storms in the region formed on and remained close to this surface front, largely because southwesterly mid level winds were aligned nearly parallel to it. This southwesterly mid and upper level flow was focused on the eastern side of a large-scale trough moving into the Rockies. In addition, the local area found itself on the backside of a departing mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) during the late afternoon, also helping to concentrate the majority of severe weather just to the south and east. Severe weather parameters in the Mitchell County area during the evening hours featured strong instability with around 3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE, in the presence of 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer shear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winter was slow to loosen its grip on North Central Kansas in 2013, as one of the top-5 coldest months of April on record featured a late-season snowfall of 1-4 inches that mainly fell between the late evening of Monday the 22nd and sunrise on Tuesday the 23rd. Although flurries or very light accumulating snow continued into the daytime hours for parts of the six-county area, the bulk of this event occurred overnight. The overall-highest accumulations fell under east-west oriented bands that targeted Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties, where NWS cooperative observers and CoCoRaHS observers reported amounts as high as 4 at Natoma and near Covert, 3.5 ten miles south of Alton, 3.4 four miles west-northwest of Plainville and 3 five miles south-southwest of Scottsville. Accompanying this primarily nocturnal snow event was a sustained north wind of 15-25 MPH with occasional higher gusts in the wake of a sharp cold front. Temperature-wise, readings hovered around 30 degrees as snow accumulated. Unseasonably cold readings persisted right on through the day of the 23rd, as most of North Central Kansas only attained highs in the mid-upper 30s. However, this snow did not stick around for long, as readings quickly rebounded to around 60 degrees the following day. ||In the mid and upper levels, forcing was instigated by a pronounced east-west zone of mid level frontogenesis residing underneath a stout upper level jet extending eastward from a large-scale, positively-tilted trough centered along an axis from south central Canada to the Central Rockies. At the surface, overnight analysis confirmed that North Central Kansas was positioned well north of a strong cold front aligned from west Texas to western Illinois, with local temperatures hovering around 30 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.4 inches of snow fell 4 miles west northwest of Plainville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Also measured 0.55 inches of rainfall so far.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slipped into northern Kansas and became stationary very early this Monday morning. Rain showers occurred along the front through the overnight hours of Sunday.  By dawn, overnight showers departed to the east, while more showers formed over northwestern Kansas. Some of these showers strengthened into thunderstorms. The strongest storm formed over Rooks County. While brief, the storm was strong enough to produce multiple reports of hail, the largest of which was 1 inch in diameter, just west of Stockton. The reason for the transition to thunderstorms was the northward transport of moisture in the presence of warm air advection. It was just enough to destabilize the atmosphere, with most unstable CAPE between 500 and 1000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny size hail completely covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened slightly in June across the region.  Cheyenne and Sherman counties were listed as being in D3 (Extreme) Drought, with all remaining counties in D4 (Exceptional) Drought.
A lone thunderstorm developed during the late afternoon and moved northeast across Wichita and Logan counties.  The storm produced half dollar sized hail and wind gusts to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported nickel to quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary and produced large tornadoes during the afternoon and evening of May 28th 2013.  2 tornadoes occurred in Nemaha county Kansas and at least 1 violent long lived tornado occurred in Ottawa County Kansas.  Amazingly no serious injuries and no fatalities were reported.  Later that night slow moving storms dumped 5 to 10 inches of rain across parts of Brown and Nemaha counties in Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey indicated that a tornado touched down initially approximately 4 miles west of Corning.  The tornado became large and strong producing EF3 damage along its path.  It moved slowly and eventually turned north northeast before ending.  Maximum winds were estimated around 150 mph.

Extremely powerful severe thunderstorms erupted over Central Kansas in the afternoon, then went on a rampage across South-Central and Southeast Kansas from late in the afternoon through late that evening. Early in the event, the severe thunderstorms produced very large hail, as large as softballs, then evolved into prolific damaging wind producers. Widespread 70-90 mph winds occurred, no doubt causing tremendous damage to trees, many of which were quite large. A few trees were around 20-feet tall with 2-3 foot diameter trunks. Several trees fell onto houses that, in a few cases, caused roof damage. Damage to power lines and power poles was certainly widespread and resulted in numerous power outages. Around 25,000 residences lost power in the Wichita Metro area alone; with around 4,000 still without power as of the 30th. Many houses sustained roof damage, especially in Maize and Northwest Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by trained spotter.

The Wichita Eagle contributed greatly to this narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winter was slow to loosen its grip on North Central Kansas in 2013, as one of the top-5 coldest months of April on record featured a late-season snowfall of 1-4 inches that mainly fell between the late evening of Monday the 22nd and sunrise on Tuesday the 23rd. Although flurries or very light accumulating snow continued into the daytime hours for parts of the six-county area, the bulk of this event occurred overnight. The overall-highest accumulations fell under east-west oriented bands that targeted Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties, where NWS cooperative observers and CoCoRaHS observers reported amounts as high as 4 at Natoma and near Covert, 3.5 ten miles south of Alton, 3.4 four miles west-northwest of Plainville and 3 five miles south-southwest of Scottsville. Accompanying this primarily nocturnal snow event was a sustained north wind of 15-25 MPH with occasional higher gusts in the wake of a sharp cold front. Temperature-wise, readings hovered around 30 degrees as snow accumulated. Unseasonably cold readings persisted right on through the day of the 23rd, as most of North Central Kansas only attained highs in the mid-upper 30s. However, this snow did not stick around for long, as readings quickly rebounded to around 60 degrees the following day. ||In the mid and upper levels, forcing was instigated by a pronounced east-west zone of mid level frontogenesis residing underneath a stout upper level jet extending eastward from a large-scale, positively-tilted trough centered along an axis from south central Canada to the Central Rockies. At the surface, overnight analysis confirmed that North Central Kansas was positioned well north of a strong cold front aligned from west Texas to western Illinois, with local temperatures hovering around 30 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.0 inches of snow fell 5 miles south southwest of Scottsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, April marked the 11th consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought within this six-county portion of North Central Kansas. This continued one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s, although limited parts of the area saw encouraging trends during April. Based on Drought Monitor weekly issuances, there was essentially no change in drought classification over the course of the month for five of the six counties, with a one-category improvement from D2 Severe Drought to D1 Moderate Drought noted in southeastern Mitchell County. This marked the first time since July 2012 that any portion of the six-county area featured something better than Severe Drought. At month's end, the vast majority of Phillips, Rooks, Smith and Osborne counties remained in D3 Extreme Drought, with the far western fringes of Rooks and Phillips brushed with D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category). Farther east, all of Jewell and northwestern Mitchell counties continued in D2 Severe Drought. Based on reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, at least 60 percent of the area observed below normal April precipitation (based on normals generally between 2.10-2.50 inches). However, even within the below normal zones most places still measured at least 1.30 for the month. In other words, no areas were left bone-dry, as had been the case several times within the past year. In general, Mitchell, Jewell and Smith counties were the wettest counties in April, compared to drier conditions in most of Osborne, Rooks and Phillips. A few of the lowest April precipitation totals at cooperative observer sites were 1.26 at Phillipsburg, 1.38 four miles west-northwest of Plainville and 1.40 near Covert in rural Osborne County. On the wetter side of things were locations such as Beloit (4.01), Jewell (3.59) and Lebanon (2.77). As for April snowfall, a predominant below normal temperature regime set the stage for mainly above normal amounts, highlighted by 6.5 at Natoma and 6.3 at Plainville 4WNW. ||Given the prevailing near to slightly below normal precipitation regime within much of North Central Kansas during April, there remained a long way to go to fully relieve the area of severe or worse drought given that longer-term impacts to subsoil moisture and groundwater deficits were still very evident. The longer-term trend was generally on the right track, however, as most of the six-county area observed between 75 and 115 percent of normal precipitation during the first four months of 2013. The USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending May 5th indicated that topsoil moisture across the North Central District continued to improve, rating only 15 percent short to very short and 82% adequate, compared to late-March when it was 30% short to very short. However, subsoil moisture still showed considerable room for improvement, rating 63% short to very short, although this was still a 20% improvement since late March. The state-averaged wheat crop rated 40% poor to very poor and only 27% good to excellent. Pasture and range conditions across Kansas rated 62% poor to very poor, a 17% improvement since five weeks prior, while hay and forage supplies exhibited little change at 71% short to very short. Stock water supplies had improved somewhat in recent weeks but still rated 48% short to very short.

Large hail up to 1.75 inches in diameter, wind gusts up to 62 MPH, a couple tornadoes were reported with these storms.

Several trees fell onto houses that, in a few cases, caused roof damage. Damage to power lines and power poles was certainly widespread and resulted in numerous power outages. Around 25,000 residences lost power in the Wichita Metro area alone; with around 4,000 still without power as of the 30th. Many houses sustained roof damage, especially in Maize and Northwest Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winter was slow to loosen its grip on North Central Kansas in 2013, as one of the top-5 coldest months of April on record featured a late-season snowfall of 1-4 inches that mainly fell between the late evening of Monday the 22nd and sunrise on Tuesday the 23rd. Although flurries or very light accumulating snow continued into the daytime hours for parts of the six-county area, the bulk of this event occurred overnight. The overall-highest accumulations fell under east-west oriented bands that targeted Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties, where NWS cooperative observers and CoCoRaHS observers reported amounts as high as 4 at Natoma and near Covert, 3.5 ten miles south of Alton, 3.4 four miles west-northwest of Plainville and 3 five miles south-southwest of Scottsville. Accompanying this primarily nocturnal snow event was a sustained north wind of 15-25 MPH with occasional higher gusts in the wake of a sharp cold front. Temperature-wise, readings hovered around 30 degrees as snow accumulated. Unseasonably cold readings persisted right on through the day of the 23rd, as most of North Central Kansas only attained highs in the mid-upper 30s. However, this snow did not stick around for long, as readings quickly rebounded to around 60 degrees the following day. ||In the mid and upper levels, forcing was instigated by a pronounced east-west zone of mid level frontogenesis residing underneath a stout upper level jet extending eastward from a large-scale, positively-tilted trough centered along an axis from south central Canada to the Central Rockies. At the surface, overnight analysis confirmed that North Central Kansas was positioned well north of a strong cold front aligned from west Texas to western Illinois, with local temperatures hovering around 30 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 3.5 inches of snow fell 10 miles south of Alton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of June 18, 2013 diminishing thunderstorms crossed from Southern Nebraska into Northeast Kansas leaving an outflow boundary to proceed southeastward. Meanwhile a baroclinic zone was setting up along a line from Manhattan to Ottawa evident in a building cumulus field. As the outflow encountered the boundary thunderstorms developed. The air mass ahead of the thunderstorms was plenty unstable. There was an equal amount of hail, wind, and flash flooding reported with this event including the microburst in north Topeka.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small limbs down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This small, short-lived event was the result of two different storms on this Sunday. One storm was an apparent supercell which tracked east along the southern borders of Rooks and Osborne counties. This storm was responsible for producing several reports of hail, the largest of which was the size of ping pong balls, just south of Plainville. The other severe weather occurred with a short line segment of storms, over Phillips county, that transitioned into a small-scale bow echo. This line of storms headed east across Smith and Jewell counties producing sub-severe hail and isolated wind damage, including estimated winds up to 70 mph. A car port was also blown onto a house near Lebanon.||The tail end of a cold front, associated with a low pressure system crossing the Great Lakes, sagged to the northern Kansas border by late afternoon. Temperatures in the 70s, with dewpoints in the 40s, was not initially conducive to thunderstorms. However, southerly winds drew dewpoints in the low to mid 50s northward. This provided just enough instability to result in thunderstorms over north-central Kansas around mid-afternoon. So, while deep layer shear was supportive of severe thunderstorms (30-40 knots), instability was marginally sufficient with mean layer CAPE ranging from 500 to 1000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was a public report, relayed by the emergency manager. The ping pong size hail occurred along with 1.5 inches of rain and the hail was 6 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a unique Tuesday afternoon and evening of weather within North Central Kansas, as winter and spring collided to produce showers and elevated thunderstorms with a varied mix of sleet, hail and freezing rain. This was all made possible by strong frontogenesis and easterly warm air advection evident at 850 millibars flowing over the top of a shallow, cold low level airmass featuring mid-afternoon surface temperatures generally between the upper-20s and mid-30s through most of the six-county area, or 30+ degrees below normal for the date. In addition to widespread accumulating sleet in several spots, there were a few reports of hail ranging from dime to ping pong ball size in Rooks County between 230-530 PM CDT, and likely several other instances of hail across the area that are not known. To cite just one example of the unusual nature of this event, as ping pong ball size stones fell near Plainville around 245 PM CDT, the surface temperature at nearby Hill City airport was 27 degrees and north winds were sustained around 25 MPH, yielding a wind chill temperature around 12 degrees. Despite this very cold surface regime, elevated thunderstorms thrived in an environment featuring most unstable CAPE of 500-1000 J/kg and 30-50 knots of effective deep layer vertical wind shear. ||Thunderstorms first broke out within this portion of North Central Kansas between noon and 3 PM CDT, primarily in Rooks and Phillips counties. By this time, the area was already positioned well to the west of a sharp synoptic cold front extending from extreme southeast Nebraska into south central Kansas. During the rest of the afternoon and lasting through around 9 PM CDT in the evening, multiple clusters and line segments of fast-moving elevated storms streamed north-northeast across the six-county area while gradually ending from west to east. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred just downstream from a powerful, closed mid level low centered over Colorado, which anchored an expansive and highly-amplified longwave trough dominating the western half of the United States. The rapid increase in afternoon thunderstorm development was aided by the arrival of the exit region of a 100+ knot south-southwest jet streak evident at 300 millibars. As it turned out, the 9th marked the first of three consecutive notably cold April days, as temperatures across most of this area failed to exceed the low-40s through the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of ping pong balls lasted for 5 minutes.

A supercell thunderstorm became embedded within a line of storms and produced a small EF1 tornado near the Emporia airport.  This tornado produced a non-continuous damage path approximately 9 miles long with the majority of the damage rated EF0.  The EF1 damage was located near the airport to a home and outbuilding that had portion of the roof removed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down near the Emporia airport and produced EF1 damage to a home and outbuilding on the property.  The tornado produced a non-continuous damage path along a 9 mile track producing subsequent EF0 damage along the remainder of its track. The tornado lifted with additional straight line wind damage noted further north just west of Reading.  There was some question whether this was another short lived tornado however after further investigation it appears that this was due to non-tornado winds within a bowing line segment of storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely powerful severe thunderstorms erupted over Central Kansas in the afternoon, then went on a rampage across South-Central and Southeast Kansas from late in the afternoon through late that evening. Early in the event, the severe thunderstorms produced very large hail, as large as softballs, then evolved into prolific damaging wind producers. Widespread 70-90 mph winds occurred, no doubt causing tremendous damage to trees, many of which were quite large. A few trees were around 20-feet tall with 2-3 foot diameter trunks. Several trees fell onto houses that, in a few cases, caused roof damage. Damage to power lines and power poles was certainly widespread and resulted in numerous power outages. Around 25,000 residences lost power in the Wichita Metro area alone; with around 4,000 still without power as of the 30th. Many houses sustained roof damage, especially in Maize and Northwest Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gusts occurred near the Maize Road/NW 21st intersection but no damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of June 18, 2013 diminishing thunderstorms crossed from Southern Nebraska into Northeast Kansas leaving an outflow boundary to proceed southeastward. Meanwhile a baroclinic zone was setting up along a line from Manhattan to Ottawa evident in a building cumulus field. As the outflow encountered the boundary thunderstorms developed. The air mass ahead of the thunderstorms was plenty unstable. There was an equal amount of hail, wind, and flash flooding reported with this event including the microburst in north Topeka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell Thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary and produced large tornadoes during the afternoon and evening of May 28th 2013.  2 tornadoes occurred in Nemaha county Kansas and at least 1 violent long lived tornado occurred in Ottawa County Kansas. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Concordia Airport measured non thunderstorm wind gusts.

Extremely powerful severe thunderstorms erupted over Central Kansas in the afternoon, then went on a rampage across South-Central and Southeast Kansas from late in the afternoon through late that evening. Early in the event, the severe thunderstorms produced very large hail, as large as softballs, then evolved into prolific damaging wind producers. Widespread 70-90 mph winds occurred, no doubt causing tremendous damage to trees, many of which were quite large. A few trees were around 20-feet tall with 2-3 foot diameter trunks. Several trees fell onto houses that, in a few cases, caused roof damage. Damage to power lines and power poles was certainly widespread and resulted in numerous power outages. Around 25,000 residences lost power in the Wichita Metro area alone; with around 4,000 still without power as of the 30th. Many houses sustained roof damage, especially in Maize and Northwest Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No details were provided with respect to damage.

This was a unique Tuesday afternoon and evening of weather within North Central Kansas, as winter and spring collided to produce showers and elevated thunderstorms with a varied mix of sleet, hail and freezing rain. This was all made possible by strong frontogenesis and easterly warm air advection evident at 850 millibars flowing over the top of a shallow, cold low level airmass featuring mid-afternoon surface temperatures generally between the upper-20s and mid-30s through most of the six-county area, or 30+ degrees below normal for the date. In addition to widespread accumulating sleet in several spots, there were a few reports of hail ranging from dime to ping pong ball size in Rooks County between 230-530 PM CDT, and likely several other instances of hail across the area that are not known. To cite just one example of the unusual nature of this event, as ping pong ball size stones fell near Plainville around 245 PM CDT, the surface temperature at nearby Hill City airport was 27 degrees and north winds were sustained around 25 MPH, yielding a wind chill temperature around 12 degrees. Despite this very cold surface regime, elevated thunderstorms thrived in an environment featuring most unstable CAPE of 500-1000 J/kg and 30-50 knots of effective deep layer vertical wind shear. ||Thunderstorms first broke out within this portion of North Central Kansas between noon and 3 PM CDT, primarily in Rooks and Phillips counties. By this time, the area was already positioned well to the west of a sharp synoptic cold front extending from extreme southeast Nebraska into south central Kansas. During the rest of the afternoon and lasting through around 9 PM CDT in the evening, multiple clusters and line segments of fast-moving elevated storms streamed north-northeast across the six-county area while gradually ending from west to east. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event occurred just downstream from a powerful, closed mid level low centered over Colorado, which anchored an expansive and highly-amplified longwave trough dominating the western half of the United States. The rapid increase in afternoon thunderstorm development was aided by the arrival of the exit region of a 100+ knot south-southwest jet streak evident at 300 millibars. As it turned out, the 9th marked the first of three consecutive notably cold April days, as temperatures across most of this area failed to exceed the low-40s through the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel size hail was reported.

A year long period of prolonged dry weather, lingered drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of May 2013. An unusually dry period from May 2012 through March, 2013, kept an extreme drought (D3) going for portions of Central and South Central Kansas. There was some improvement in the drought conditions towards the end of April 2013, as spring showers with soaking rains occurred, which led to continued improvement of the drought during the late part of April into the early part of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across the Flint Hills of Southeast Kansas during the month of May. This was a year long continuation of a drought that started in late May of 2012 and continued through the month of April 2013.

A prolonged period of dry weather continued the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of January. An unusually dry period from May through November kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas. Portions of Central Kansas continued to stay very dry, with exceptional drought (D4) conditions for most of the month of January. Most areas saw little if any rainfall during the month, which led to the drought actually getting worse across South Central Kansas during the beginning of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions continued during the month of January across Central Kansas. Some welcome rainfall did occur during the middle of the month, leading to rainfall amounts a little below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short-term drought conditions continue to improve over extreme easternl Kansas, through the month of February 2013. Recent rains and snowstorms have led to this improvement in the short-term, with retention ponds, streams, and rivers, beginning to return to normal or near normal levels. Long-term impacts continue to be the prevailing source for our drought conditions, but with the magnitude of the recent snow melt and rains, even the long-term impacts have diminished. As a result, severe drought (D2) continues in extreme eastern Kansas, with extreme (D3) conditions found earlier in western Leavenworth County, improving to severe (D2) conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions prevailed across the county. Overland Park reported 2.99 inches of precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, November 2013 marked the 18th consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued at least some portion of this six county North Central Kansas area, prolonging one of the most notable droughts in decades. Despite at least slightly above normal November precipitation across the majority of the area, the U.S. Drought Monitor reflected no change whatsoever during the month in drought classification within the western-most counties of Phillips and Rooks, but actually indicated a slight degradation across most of the remainder of the area. Nonetheless, a west (worse) to east (better) disparity in drought severity remained intact. As a result, the lingering effects of longer-term dryness kept D2 Severe Drought in place for northwestern Phillips and far western Rooks counties. Meanwhile, category D1 Moderate Drought persisted through southeastern Phillips, northwestern Smith and the majority of Rooks counties. Meanwhile, although still better-off than counties farther west, all of Osborne, Jewell and Mitchell counties, along with southeastern Smith County, ended the month in category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought). For most places, a significant percentage of monthly precipitation fell during an early-month rain event on the 5th, while very little, if hardly any fell thereafter. On the drier side of the ledger were sites including Logan (0.77) Phillipsburg (0.85) and one mile north of Long Island (0.89).||Taking a look at longer-term precipitation trends, without question the driest conditions so far in 2013 continued to plague Phillips and western Rooks counties, while the overall best-off areas resided across Mitchell and eastern Jewell counties. According to the USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending November 24th, topsoil moisture within the north central district had improved somewhat since late-October, as the short or very short category decreased from 55% to 37%. During that same time frame, north central Kansas subsoil moisture remained largely unchanged, now checking in at 67% short or very short.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short-term drought conditions continue to improve over extreme easternl Kansas, through the month of February 2013. Recent rains and snowstorms have led to this improvement in the short-term, with retention ponds, streams, and rivers, beginning to return to normal or near normal levels. Long-term impacts continue to be the prevailing source for our drought conditions, but with the magnitude of the recent snow melt and rains, even the long-term impacts have diminished. As a result, severe drought (D2) continues in extreme eastern Kansas, with extreme (D3) conditions found earlier in western Leavenworth County, improving to severe (D2) conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions prevailed across the county. Leavenworth reported 2.01 inches of precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, January marked the eighth consecutive month of severe (or worse) drought across this six-county portion of North Central Kansas, as one of the worst droughts since the 1950s, which took hold during the summer of 2012, continued into the new year. Despite the second consecutive month of at least slightly above normal precipitation in most spots, all six counties remained under a federally-designated drought disaster. According to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor there was essentially no change whatsoever in drought classification during the month. Category D4 exceptional drought (the worst-possible classification) remained firmly in place across Phillips and Rooks counties along with the majority of Smith and Osborne counties. Farther east, a mix of category D2 severe and category D3 extreme drought persisted in Jewell and Mitchell Counties. Based on reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, most of the area realized above normal precipitation in January (per normals generally between 0.40-0.70). Some of this above normal precipitation was tied to a widespread 2-5 snowfall from the 29th-30th. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest January precipitation totals featured 1.36 near Covert in rural Osborne County, 1.19 at Mankato and 1.06 near Alton. On the lower side of monthly totals, although still near normal to slightly above normal, were sites such as Logan (0.38), near Long Island (0.48) and Phillipsburg (0.50). ||Despite the encouraging recent development of above normal precipitation during December and January, this trend did little to ease serious drought conditions. This is largely due to the fact that winter precipitation events generally account for less than 10 percent of total annual precipitation, thus minimizing the effect of even major snow storms on drought relief. The USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition report for January indicated little change since December, with the state-averaged winter wheat crop averaging 35 to 40 percent poor to very poor, and only around 20% good to excellent. State-averaged range and pasture conditions remained over 80% poor to very poor, and hay and forage and stock water supplies continued to check in between 70-80% short to very short.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A early spring winter storm clobbered parts of northeast and east central Kansas with anywhere from 5 to as much as 9 inches of heavy wet snow.  The snow began to fall during the late afternoon on the 23rd and intensified overnight.  The snow ended across the area by around noon on the 24th. North winds of 20 to 30 mph produced widespread blowing and drifting of snow into the 24th especially in the outlying, rural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer at Lane measured 8.3 inches of snow from the storm.  EM also reported widespread 7 to 9 inches of snow with the heaviest amounts across the eastern half of the county.

An unusually dry period from May through November kept an extreme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas. Portions of Central Kansas continued to stay very dry, with exceptional drought (D4) conditions for most of the month of January. Most areas saw little if any rainfall during the month, which led to the drought actually getting worse across South Central Kansas during the beginning of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of January. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of December and into January. This is the ninth consecutive month of below normal precipitation for South Central Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day and nighttime hours of Saturday, November 16th, a cold front gradually advanced southeastward across Kansas. Its forward progress was slowed by the ejection of multiple low pressure centers out of Colorado, that subsequently rode northeast along the front. The final low was near Topeka at sunrise on Sunday the 17th. The trailing cold front was along the Kansas-Oklahoma border and continuing to press southward. By sunset, the low was over Michigan and was 10 mb deeper (to 982 mb). This rapid deepening was due to the upper-level trough becoming negatively tilted during the day. A 140 kt jet streak was upstream of the trough axis.||Strong winds occurred, over North Central Kansas, in the wake of the departing low between the hours of 3 and 9 pm. Sustained winds generally averaged between 30 and 40 mph, with maximum gusts of 50 to 55 mph. The strongest sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph, occurred between 11 am and 12 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained wind speeds of up to 43 MPH and a peak wind gust of 53 MPH were recorded by the AWOS located at the Smith Center Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the late afternoon of Tuesday the 29th and the pre-dawn hours of Wednesday the 30th, the majority of the six-county area received a widespread 2 to 5 inches of snow. The only exception was Phillips County, most of which measured only 1-2. The balance of accumulating snow targeted the evening hours, generally between 5 PM and 11 PM CST. Strong winds were not a significant player during this event, but at least minor blowing snow issues developed during the overnight hours as sustained north winds increased to 20-30 MPH. According to NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest totals included 5 near Covert in rural Osborne County, 4.7 at Jewell and 4 at Burr Oak, Natoma and Hunter. Along the far northwest edge of the snow shield, only 1 was reported at Logan and Long Island. On the large scale aloft, forcing was provided by an expansive longwave trough passing through the central United States. On a more localized scale, North Central Kansas snowfall focused underneath a band of mid level frontogenesis evident at 700 millibars, and within the entrance region of a 140+ knot upper jet streak centered from northeast Nebraska into Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals throughout the county generally ranged from 3 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A early spring winter storm clobbered parts of northeast and east central Kansas with anywhere from 5 to as much as 9 inches of heavy wet snow.  The snow began to fall during the late afternoon on the 23rd and intensified overnight.  The snow ended across the area by around noon on the 24th. North winds of 20 to 30 mph produced widespread blowing and drifting of snow into the 24th especially in the outlying, rural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer at Gridley measured 6 inches of snow Sunday morning on the 24th.  EM also reported widespread 6 to 7 inch amounts across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts of 45 to 55 MPH during the late morning stirred up a large cloud of dirt over Crowley and Otero counties in East Central Colorado. The cloud of dust was pushed east-northeast by the strong wind gusts. Visibilities along and south of Highway 24 dropped to near zero at times in the blowing dust.  The visibilities improved behind a cold front that moved through during the mid to late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility was reported across much of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short-term drought conditions continue to improve over extreme easternl Kansas, through the month of February 2013. Recent rains and snowstorms have led to this improvement in the short-term, with retention ponds, streams, and rivers, beginning to return to normal or near normal levels. Long-term impacts continue to be the prevailing source for our drought conditions, but with the magnitude of the recent snow melt and rains, even the long-term impacts have diminished. As a result, severe drought (D2) continues in extreme eastern Kansas, with extreme (D3) conditions found earlier in western Leavenworth County, improving to severe (D2) conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions prevailed across the county. Atchison reported 1.49 inches of precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From late in the afternoon of July 23rd until late that night, extremely powerful severe thunderstorms went on a rampage from Central to South-Central Kansas.||The thunderstorms first developed over Lincoln & Ellsworth counties and quickly evolved into monstrous super cells as they invaded the rest of Central & South-Central Kansas as the evening progressed. Several areas in Central Kansas were bombarded by tennis ball to softball-sized hail. Reno County was hardest hit, where an astonishing 12 reports of tennis ball to softball-sized hail occurred and where winds reached a horrific 80-100 mph along the Reno/Kingman County line.||Initially, the super cells were prolific hail-producers, but as the evening progressed, they evolved into a large squall line that unleashed damaging and, at times, destructive, winds that ranged from 70-100 mph as it tore through South-Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained speeds at 65 mph were measured with gusts that reached 75 mph at the 30th Street/Hendrick intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of large complexes of thunderstorms moved along or just north of a stationary frontal boundary situated over Southern Kansas, during the early morning hours of August 4th and August 5th, 2012.   Abundant moisture running over the top of this boundary, and the slow movement and back building of the complex of storms led to widespread flash flooding across portions of Central Kansas from Hutchinson to Newton to the Flint Hills. The hardest hit areas were over Hutchinson, which received 6.7 inches of rainfall in a six hour period, and across Barton and Rice counties, which had reports of 4 to 5 inches. The city streets were overwhelmed with heavy rainfall over a short period of time, which led to numerous stalled cars, flooded basements and evacuations.  This heavy rainfall was a continuation of a stagnant weather pattern that brought heavy rainfall to the area beginning the last week of July, 2012 and continued into early August.  This relentless heavy rainfall pattern led to river flooding, and widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cars were stalled along Meridan and Harry from water over the roads.

Reno County was hardest hit, where an astonishing 12 reports of tennis ball to softball-sized hail occurred and where winds reached a horrific 80-100 mph along the Reno/Kingman County line.||Initially, the super cells were prolific hail-producers, but as the evening progressed, they evolved into a large squall line that unleashed damaging and, at times, destructive, winds that ranged from 70-100 mph as it tore through South-Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of large complexes of thunderstorms moved along or just north of a stationary frontal boundary situated over Southern Kansas, during the early morning hours of August 4th and August 5th, 2012.   Abundant moisture running over the top of this boundary, and the slow movement and back building of the complex of storms led to widespread flash flooding across portions of Central Kansas from Hutchinson to Newton to the Flint Hills. The hardest hit areas were over Hutchinson, which received 6.7 inches of rainfall in a six hour period, and across Barton and Rice counties, which had reports of 4 to 5 inches. The city streets were overwhelmed with heavy rainfall over a short period of time, which led to numerous stalled cars, flooded basements and evacuations.  This heavy rainfall was a continuation of a stagnant weather pattern that brought heavy rainfall to the area beginning the last week of July, 2012 and continued into early August.  This relentless heavy rainfall pattern led to river flooding, and widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low water crossings had water over them across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down across a road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme street flooding and flash flooding occurred across the area, as 6 to 7 inches of rainfall fell over Hutchinson and Nickerson during the early morning hours.  Numerous cars stalled out and were abandoned on city streets, with widespread basement flooding reported.  During a 90 minute period, between 130am and 3am, some locations in Hutchinson picked up 3 to 5 inches of rainfall.  At least 325 homes in the city of Hutchinson reported some property damage due to flooding, with 10 to 24 of the homes receiving major damage.  150 residents from a nursing home in Northwest Hutchinson were evacuated to a local hospital, because water was coming into the building.  The water on city streets was so deep, at times, that large military type vehicles could not venture down the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of Oct 4 a strong system moving through the northern plains brought a cold front to the region. An unstable air mass was able to move into the area ahead of the system setting the stage for thunderstorms. As the main trough moved through the plains in continued to deepen allowing for strong winds. The combination of wind shear and instability lead to an outbreak of severe thunderstorms in northeast KS as the cold front progressed eastward. Damaging winds were the primary outcome from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measured at AWOS.

Reno County was hardest hit, where an astonishing 12 reports of tennis ball to softball-sized hail occurred and where winds reached a horrific 80-100 mph along the Reno/Kingman County line.||Initially, the super cells were prolific hail-producers, but as the evening progressed, they evolved into a large squall line that unleashed damaging and, at times, destructive, winds that ranged from 70-100 mph as it tore through South-Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of large complexes of thunderstorms moved along or just north of a stationary frontal boundary situated over Southern Kansas, during the early morning hours of August 4th and August 5th, 2012.   Abundant moisture running over the top of this boundary, and the slow movement and back building of the complex of storms led to widespread flash flooding across portions of Central Kansas from Hutchinson to Newton to the Flint Hills. The hardest hit areas were over Hutchinson, which received 6.7 inches of rainfall in a six hour period, and across Barton and Rice counties, which had reports of 4 to 5 inches. The city streets were overwhelmed with heavy rainfall over a short period of time, which led to numerous stalled cars, flooded basements and evacuations.  This heavy rainfall was a continuation of a stagnant weather pattern that brought heavy rainfall to the area beginning the last week of July, 2012 and continued into early August.  This relentless heavy rainfall pattern led to river flooding, and widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several county roads southwest of Great Bend had water over them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over eastern Colorado late on this Friday afternoon, taking the form a squall line oriented from northeast to southwest. This line advanced east through northwestern Kansas during the evening hours. Around 11 pm CDT, these storms moved into the northwestern corner of Phillips County and over the following four hours, this line slowly advanced across North Central Kansas. While these storms remained below severe levels in most locations, sporadic damage to buildings, power poles and trees was reported in localized portions of Osborne, Jewell and Mitchell counties. This included several large tree limbs knocked down in Cawker City, minor damage to a home in Downs, and a few power poles toppled along a swath in southern Jewell County. ||The upper-level flow was highly amplified, with a ridge over the eastern United States and a trough over the west. These storms were initiated by a slow-moving cold front associated with the trough. While archived instability information was unavailable, late afternoon high temperatures were in the lower 90, with dewpoints in the middle 50s. These storms formed in a strong wind environment with 40 kts around 4500 ft above ground level and 50 kts at 6000 ft. Deep layer shear was near 70 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor damage was reported to the roof, siding and garage of one home in Downs from wind gusts estimated to be near 60 mph..  A larger tree branch was also broken off of the main trunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of freezing rain, sleet and snow developed across eastern Kansas during the early morning hours of Saturday December 21st.  Widespread snow, sleet and freezing rain overspread the area during the afternoon of the 21st with the height of the storm occurring between 5pm Saturday and 3am Sunday.  Freezing rain accumulating to around one quarter inch across parts of the area while snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches were common across other areas north of interstate 70 by Sunday morning the 22nd. Impacts were mainly limited to hazardous travel with numerous accidents reported. Only sporadic power outages were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was measured at White City in Morris County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of small but compact supercell thunderstorms developed just north of Highway 54, across South Central Kansas near Kingman and Zenda, Kansas.  One prolific supercell produced 70 to 100 mph wind damage and power outages along its path, from just north of Kingman, Kansas to Newton to Eureka, Kansas.  A marina at El Dorado Lake, Kansas was hardest hit, damaging several boats and swamping a 40 foot houseboat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over Highway 96 in and around Nickerson.  It was reported that between 6 to 8 inches of water was flowing over the road.  1.15 inches of rainfall fell in a very short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over eastern Colorado late on this Friday afternoon, taking the form a squall line oriented from northeast to southwest. This line advanced east through northwestern Kansas during the evening hours. Around 11 pm CDT, these storms moved into the northwestern corner of Phillips County and over the following four hours, this line slowly advanced across North Central Kansas. While these storms remained below severe levels in most locations, sporadic damage to buildings, power poles and trees was reported in localized portions of Osborne, Jewell and Mitchell counties. This included several large tree limbs knocked down in Cawker City, minor damage to a home in Downs, and a few power poles toppled along a swath in southern Jewell County. ||The upper-level flow was highly amplified, with a ridge over the eastern United States and a trough over the west. These storms were initiated by a slow-moving cold front associated with the trough. While archived instability information was unavailable, late afternoon high temperatures were in the lower 90, with dewpoints in the middle 50s. These storms formed in a strong wind environment with 40 kts around 4500 ft above ground level and 50 kts at 6000 ft. Deep layer shear was near 70 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to at least 60 mph.  A few power poles were knocked down along this path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of large complexes of thunderstorms moved along or just north of a stationary frontal boundary situated over Southern Kansas, during the early morning hours of August 4th and August 5th, 2012.   Abundant moisture running over the top of this boundary, and the slow movement and back building of the complex of storms led to widespread flash flooding across portions of Central Kansas from Hutchinson to Newton to the Flint Hills. The hardest hit areas were over Hutchinson, which received 6.7 inches of rainfall in a six hour period, and across Barton and Rice counties, which had reports of 4 to 5 inches. The city streets were overwhelmed with heavy rainfall over a short period of time, which led to numerous stalled cars, flooded basements and evacuations.  This heavy rainfall was a continuation of a stagnant weather pattern that brought heavy rainfall to the area beginning the last week of July, 2012 and continued into early August.  This relentless heavy rainfall pattern led to river flooding, and widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered a few county roads.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, September 2013 marked the 16th-consecutive month of D2 Severe (or worse) Drought within this six-county portion of North Central Kansas, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. However, there was positive news as the overall coverage and severity of drought continued to dwindle and shift westward. Over the course of the month, the U.S. Drought Monitor reflected one-category improvement across much of the area, thanks in large part to a widespread 1.00-2.50 rainfall late in the month on the 27th that especially targeted Phillips and Rooks counties. The resultant above normal monthly rainfall in primarily western portions of the local area was quite welcome, and targeted those places that needed it most based on long-term precipitation deficits. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor at month's end, the entire six-county area was void of not only category D4 Exceptional Drought (the worst-possible category) but also D3 Extreme Drought, for the first time since July 2012. Nonetheless, the lingering effects of longer-term dryness kept D2 Severe Drought intact across northwestern Phillips and far western Rooks counties. Meanwhile, category D1 Moderate Drought prevailed across southeastern Phillips, northwestern Smith and the majority of Rooks counties. On the somewhat better-off side of things, category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought) prevailed across most of Smith, western Osborne and northern Jewell counties. Across eastern portions of the area, the coverage of locations depicted to be void of all drought or abnormally dry conditions increased during September, not only including all of Mitchell County as before, but now also including the majority of Osborne and Jewell counties. ||Reports from around 20 NWS Cooperative Observers, and also radar-derived estimation, revealed that at least 60 percent of the local area registered below normal September precipitation, while the greatest concentration of above normal amounts focused within Phillips and Rooks counties (based on September normals that range from around 1.70��� in the west to around 2.60��� in the east). Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and CoCoRaHS observers, a few of the highest September precipitation totals featured 4.85��� at Logan, 4.65��� just northwest of Prairie View , 4.49��� at Plainville and 4.22 at Jewell. On the opposite, drier side of the spectrum were sites such as Lovewell Dam (1.22���), two miles southwest of Alton (1.44���) and Beloit (1.48). In between these wettest and driest extremes, the majority of the six-county area reported September rainfall totals somewhere between 1.50-3.25, including stations in or near Mankato, Smith Center and Natoma. ||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (Jan. 1-Sep. 18), most of the six-county area stood somewhere between 70-105% of normal, with Phillips County clearly the overall-driest, including only 64% of normal at Phillipsburg. In contrast, Mitchell County remained the overall-wettest, featuring 106% of normal at Beloit. The USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending September 15th indicated that topsoil moisture within the north central district had recently degraded, now rating 48% short or very short, compared to 25% short or very short in mid-August. Over that same four weeks, subsoil moisture had also degraded from 44% to 64% short or very short. The state-averaged corn crop rated 39% good to excellent and 33% poor to very poor, only a slight degradation since mid-August. Soybeans across Kansas also degraded a bit, now rating 48% good or excellent versus 61% in mid-August. State-averaged range and pasture conditions checked in 37% poor or very poor, only a 2% downgrade since August 18th. Stock water supplies also degraded slightly, rating 29% short or very short and 69% adequate, compared to 25% short or very short four weeks prior.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a large upper trough over the southwest United States and a strong surface low pressure system triggered a line of strong to severe thunderstorms which rolled from eastern Colorado into western Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the late afternoon and early evening.  Estimated winds to 80 mph snapped several power poles and caused other minor damage.  Marginally severe hail was also reported with the storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glass door was broken at Cowboy Corner in downtown Goodland, either from being blown open or something being blown into it.  Time of report estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front that was moving into the area which then became a heavy rain event.  Flooding of near historic proportions was observed across far southern Ford and northern Clark counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 4.80 inches was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was crossing highway 160 at this location and a car entering the water lost control and went off the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front plowed south across the area during the morning and despite the very cold April air, elevated severe thunderstorms developed north of the front in the cold air.  Large hail was occurring with surface temperatures at or slightly below freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper low pressure system moved across the region during the day. Behind the system, strong winds aloft mixed down to the surface with wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph producing localized dust storms, vehicle accidents and road closures.  Interstate 70 was closed between Limon, Colorado and Goodland, Kansas due to zero visibility and strong winds, with road closures also occurring on adjacent U.S., state and county roads. Law enforcement reported near zero visibility at times in blowing dirt in Kit Carson County Colorado, as well as Greeley, Wallace and Sherman Counties in western Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds developed late in the morning and continued through the afternoon. Peak wind gusts estimated at 60 mph from nearby mesonet sites, as well as zero visibility in blowing dirt, resulted in numerous traffic accidents involving a total of 10 vehicles and one injury. Highways 27 and 96 were both closed to traffic for several hours during the afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 1.7 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall during the month of September eased drought conditions across northwest Kansas. Precipitation was widespread during a major, slow-moving storm system in mid-September.  Rainfall generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.50.  Highest rain totals occurred in Goodland, Oakley and Wallace with amounts between six and seven inches. Drought ratings area-wide at the beginning of the month were D3-D4 (Extreme to Exceptional).  By the end of the month, ratings improved to D2-D3 (Severe to Extreme) Drought.  Wallace county fared best with a drought rating improving to D1 (Moderate) Drought.

From late in the afternoon of July 23rd until late that night, extremely powerful severe thunderstorms went on a rampage from Central to South-Central Kansas.||The thunderstorms first developed over Lincoln & Ellsworth counties and quickly evolved into monstrous super cells as they invaded the rest of Central & South-Central Kansas as the evening progressed. Several areas in Central Kansas were bombarded by tennis ball to softball-sized hail. Reno County was hardest hit, where an astonishing 12 reports of tennis ball to softball-sized hail occurred and where winds reached a horrific 80-100 mph along the Reno/Kingman County line.||Initially, the super cells were prolific hail-producers, but as the evening progressed, they evolved into a large squall line that unleashed damaging and, at times, destructive, winds that ranged from 70-100 mph as it tore through South-Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and power poles were blown down around the community causing power outages. Information was received via Sumner County Emergency Management Facebook Page. Time is estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A year long period of prolonged dry weather, came to a rapid end during the last week of July 2013, as tropical moisture streamed north leading to numerous rounds of showers and thunderstorms.  The night after night rainfall rapidly refilled lakes and ponds and restored soil moisture. This ended an unusually dry period from May 2012 through March, 2013, which kept an extreme drought (D3) going for portions of Central Kansas early into the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across Central Kansas during the first part of the month of July. This was a yearlong continuation of a drought that started in late May of 2012 and continued through the year into June 2012. Copious amounts of rainfall returned to the area by the end of the month, dropping the drought to Moderate (D1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed north of a frontal boundary as a short wave trough moved out of the high plains of Colorado.  Heavy rainfall in the Dodge City area caused local flash flooding.   There was also flooding back into Gray county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Boot Hill Museum, a six inch diameter tree was blown down, a pop-out trailer was moved, a tee-pee was knocked down and a large sign was blown down by high wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms became numerous and severe producing heavy rain, hail and high winds during the afternoon and continuing into the evening hours.  A 70 knot upper level jet streak interacted with a relatively strong baroclinic zone to provide lift for the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was partially torn off an office at a motel.  Several large limbs were blown down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isentropic lifting along a narrow baroclinic zone helped to produce thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.08 was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of large complexes of thunderstorms moved along or just north of a stationary frontal boundary situated over Southern Kansas, during the early morning hours of August 4th and August 5th, 2012.   Abundant moisture running over the top of this boundary, and the slow movement and back building of the complex of storms led to widespread flash flooding across portions of Central Kansas from Hutchinson to Newton to the Flint Hills. The hardest hit areas were over Hutchinson, which received 6.7 inches of rainfall in a six hour period, and across Barton and Rice counties, which had reports of 4 to 5 inches. The city streets were overwhelmed with heavy rainfall over a short period of time, which led to numerous stalled cars, flooded basements and evacuations.  This heavy rainfall was a continuation of a stagnant weather pattern that brought heavy rainfall to the area beginning the last week of July, 2012 and continued into early August.  This relentless heavy rainfall pattern led to river flooding, and widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water caused the closing of old Highway 40 between Ellsworth and Wilson due to water over the road. Numerous rural roads were impassible due to high water.  Highway 14 north of Ellsworth also closed due to high water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across South Central Kansas during the first part of the month of July. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of large complexes of thunderstorms moved along or just north of a stationary frontal boundary situated over Southern Kansas, during the early morning hours of August 4th and August 5th, 2012.   Abundant moisture running over the top of this boundary, and the slow movement and back building of the complex of storms led to widespread flash flooding across portions of Central Kansas from Hutchinson to Newton to the Flint Hills. The hardest hit areas were over Hutchinson, which received 6.7 inches of rainfall in a six hour period, and across Barton and Rice counties, which had reports of 4 to 5 inches. The city streets were overwhelmed with heavy rainfall over a short period of time, which led to numerous stalled cars, flooded basements and evacuations.  This heavy rainfall was a continuation of a stagnant weather pattern that brought heavy rainfall to the area beginning the last week of July, 2012 and continued into early August.  This relentless heavy rainfall pattern led to river flooding, and widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple streets flooded and numerous cars flooded and stalled, throughout town, blocking streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early morning hours of Sep 1 a line of thunderstorms moved through southeast Nebraska into far northeast Kansas. The line began to bow out along the state line, and this where strong winds made it to the surface. A few large trees, some small branches, and power poles were brought down due to the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees and numerous small limbs down. Minor power outages. Delayed report. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms became numerous and severe producing heavy rain, hail and high winds during the afternoon and continuing into the evening hours.  A 70 knot upper level jet streak interacted with a relatively strong baroclinic zone to provide lift for the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.75 was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From late in the afternoon of July 23rd until late that night, extremely powerful severe thunderstorms went on a rampage from Central to South-Central Kansas.||The thunderstorms first developed over Lincoln & Ellsworth counties and quickly evolved into monstrous super cells as they invaded the rest of Central & South-Central Kansas as the evening progressed. Several areas in Central Kansas were bombarded by tennis ball to softball-sized hail. Reno County was hardest hit, where an astonishing 12 reports of tennis ball to softball-sized hail occurred and where winds reached a horrific 80-100 mph along the Reno/Kingman County line.||Initially, the super cells were prolific hail-producers, but as the evening progressed, they evolved into a large squall line that unleashed damaging and, at times, destructive, winds that ranged from 70-100 mph as it tore through South-Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The baseball-sized hail damaged trees and cracked windshields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, August 2013 marked the 15th consecutive month of D2 Severe (or worse) drought within this six-county portion of North Central Kansas, continuing one of the most notable droughts since the 1950s. Despite predominantly below normal August rainfall across at least two-thirds of the area, weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor reflected a one-category improvement during the month in most locations. Much of the August rainfall occurred during the cooler first half of the month, while little if any fell during the much-hotter later stages of the month, putting stress on maturing crops. For western counties, the dry just kept getting drier, as Phillips County and vicinity endured its 6th-consecutive month of prevailing below normal precipitation. ||Per the U.S Drought Monitor at month's end, a noticeable west-east disparity in drought severity remained in place. In the west, D3 Extreme Drought continued across much of Phillips and far western Rooks counties. Category D2 Severe Drought was noted in much of Rooks and southeastern Phillips counties.  Category D1 Moderate Drought was now assigned to much of Smith and western Osborne counties. On the somewhat better-off side of things, category D0 Abnormally Dry conditions (considered to be one category better than true drought) was depicted across the majority of Jewell and Osborne counties, which generally reflected a one-category improvement since late-July. As had been the case for a while, Mitchell County remained in the overall-best shape of the six counties, and in fact was depicted to be free of all drought or abnormally dry conditions. This marked the first time since June 2012 that any portion of the local area was shown to be drought-free. Reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, and also radar-derived estimation, confirmed that at least two-thirds of the area observed below normal August rainfall (with normal defined as roughly 3.15). The majority of Jewell and portions of Smith counties were the primary wetter exceptions with above normal rainfall, including NWS cooperative observer totals of 4.00 at Mankato and 3.60 at Burr Oak. A CoCoRaHS observer three miles east-northeast of Webber measured an even higher total of 6.46, although much of this fell during a single storm on the night of the 1st. Within the drier majority of the area, some of the lowest cooperative observer sites included Hunter (0.40), two miles southwest of Alton (0.80) and Webster Dam (0.96). In between these wettest and driest extremes, most of the area tallied August totals between 1.40-3.00, including stations in or near Beloit, Phillipsburg and Natoma. ||Focusing on the longer-range picture of year-to-date precipitation (Jan. 1-Aug. 19), most of the six-county area ranged anywhere from 60-105% of normal, with Phillips County clearly the overall-driest, including only 60% of normal at Phillipsburg. In contrast, Mitchell County remained the overall-wettest, featuring 117% of normal at Beloit. The USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending August 18th indicated that topsoil moisture within the north central district had improved considerably versus four weeks prior, rating only 25% short or very short and 75% adequate or surplus, compared to 74% short or very short in mid-July. Subsoil moisture in the north central counties had also improved quite a bit, now rating only 44% short or very short and 56% adequate or surplus, versus 71% short or very short four weeks prior. The state-averaged corn crop had improved to 41% good or excellent and only 28% poor or very poor. Soybeans across Kansas rated 61% good or excellent and only 10% poor or very poor. State-averaged range and pasture conditions checked in 35% poor or very poor, down from 55% in mid-July. Over the past four weeks, stock water supplies improved from 42% to only 25% short or very short.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.90 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of small but compact supercell thunderstorms developed just north of Highway 54, across South Central Kansas near Kingman and Zenda, Kansas.  One prolific supercell produced 70 to 100 mph wind damage and power outages along its path, from just north of Kingman, Kansas to Newton to Eureka, Kansas.  A marina at El Dorado Lake, Kansas was hardest hit, damaging several boats and swamping a 40 foot houseboat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front that was moving into the area which then became a heavy rain event.  Flooding of near historic proportions was observed across far southern Ford and northern Clark counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 6.75 inches was reported.  This caused widespread flooding across this part of the county.  Many roads were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of large complexes of thunderstorms moved along or just north of a stationary frontal boundary situated over Southern Kansas, during the early morning hours of August 4th and August 5th, 2012.   Abundant moisture running over the top of this boundary, and the slow movement and back building of the complex of storms led to widespread flash flooding across portions of Central Kansas from Hutchinson to Newton to the Flint Hills. The hardest hit areas were over Hutchinson, which received 6.7 inches of rainfall in a six hour period, and across Barton and Rice counties, which had reports of 4 to 5 inches. The city streets were overwhelmed with heavy rainfall over a short period of time, which led to numerous stalled cars, flooded basements and evacuations.  This heavy rainfall was a continuation of a stagnant weather pattern that brought heavy rainfall to the area beginning the last week of July, 2012 and continued into early August.  This relentless heavy rainfall pattern led to river flooding, and widespread flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred along Cow Creek in Hutchinson, with numerous evacuations from high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 3-7 pm CDT on this Monday, a narrow line of strong to briefly marginally-severe thunderstorms marched from west-to-east across this six county portion of North Central Kansas before exiting into northeast Kansas. Across the vast majority of the area, these storms produced nothing more than small hail and sub-severe wind gusts. However, shortly after this convective line first entered the western fringes of North Central Kansas, quarter size hail was reported west-northwest of Webster State Park in far western Rooks County. This ended up being the final severe thunderstorm event of 2013 within the six-county area. Prior to the primary round of afternoon convection, weak, scattered thunderstorm activity drifted through various parts of the local area during the day, developing in advance of a strong shortwave trough kicking out of Colorado into Nebraska. This disturbance was embedded within the southeast periphery of a larger-scale trough dominating the Northern Rockies and Northern Plains regions. The presence of widespread cloud cover through much of the day kept instability to a minimum locally, although by mid-late afternoon a dry slot and associated area of clearing skies developed over western Kansas, promoting modest mixed-based CAPE of 500-1000 J/kg in the presence of strong 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 50+ knots. As a surface cold front intersected this narrow instability axis, a southwest-northeast oriented line of storms intensified over northwest Kansas before spreading east into the local area. However, due in large part to widespread cloud cover earlier in the day, atmospheric instability dropped off markedly with eastward extent across North Central Kansas, resulting in the prevailing sub-severe nature of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground for half an hour after the hail stopped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After 9pm CDT a squall line developed across north central Kansas and evolved into a bow echo.  Widespread 60 to 80 mph winds with pockets of 80-100 mph winds occurred across a large part of Ottawa, Cloud, Clay, Geary, Washington and Riley counties between 10pm and 12 am Saturday night.  At least 2 small tornadoes were confirmed that evening however most of the damage was due to straight line winds that in some cases were measured in excess of 100 mph.
